title: United States v. Pavlo Kharmanskyi (xDedic)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Kharmanskyi is an xDedic administrator case arising from the same marketplace cluster as Habasescu, but with a different custody path. Instead of overseas extradition, DOJ says Kharmanskyi was arrested at Miami International Airport while entering the United States.

## Facts

DOJ identified Kharmanskyi as an xDedic administrator who advertised for the site, paid administrators, and provided customer support to buyers. This placed him in a managerial and operational role rather than in a mere buyer or seller position.

The case is useful because it fills in the middle tier of xDedic responsibility between technical mastermind and downstream users.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The prosecution emerged from the broader xDedic investigation involving Belgium, Ukraine, Europol, the Dutch National Police, and Germany's BKA. Even though the arrest happened in the United States, the case depended on a multinational investigation.

### Arrest and Extradition

Kharmanskyi was arrested at Miami International Airport in 2019 while attempting to enter the United States. This distinguishes the case from other xDedic prosecutions that required formal extradition from third countries.

### Asset Recovery

The case is tied to the larger xDedic seizure action from January 2019, when DOJ seized the marketplace's domain names and dismantled the infrastructure.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The Middle District of Florida had a direct U.S. nexus because xDedic sold unauthorized access devices affecting U.S. residents and infrastructure, with thousands of compromised systems in Florida alone.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Operational-role liability:** The case shows that customer-support and payment roles inside a cybercrime marketplace can still support U.S. prosecution.
- **Opportunistic-custody strategy:** It also illustrates a recurring cybercrime tactic: waiting until a foreign suspect enters a reachable jurisdiction rather than relying only on extradition from a home state.

### Precedent Value

The case has *medium to high* value because it demonstrates a distinct path to custody in multinational cybercrime cases and rounds out the xDedic administrator picture.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| Pre-2019 | Kharmanskyi allegedly advertises for xDedic and supports marketplace operations |
| 2019 | Kharmanskyi arrested at Miami International Airport |
| 2021-03-24 | Kharmanskyi pleads guilty |
| 2021-06-29 | Judgment entered |
| 2024-01-04 | DOJ roundup publicly summarizes his xDedic role |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests that xDedic enforcement used multiple custody pathways at once: extradition where necessary, U.S.-entry arrest where available, and continued prosecution after the original site takedown.

## Korean Relevance

No South Korean role is identified. The case is still relevant because opportunistic arrests during international travel remain one of the few realistic ways to bring some foreign cybercrime actors into court.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. DOJ's public materials are not fully consistent on sentence labeling versus aggregate roundup language.
2. The public record does not provide a granular description of Kharmanskyi's specific transactions or communications.
3. It remains unclear how responsibility was split among the different xDedic administrators over time.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|------|-----|
| 1 | 19 Individuals Worldwide Charged in Transnational Cybercrime Investigation of the xDedic Marketplace | DOJ M.D. Fla. | 2024-01-04 | https://www.justice.gov/usao-mdfl/pr/19-individuals-worldwide-charged-transnational-cybercrime-investigation-xdedic |

title: United States v. Konovolov et al. (GozNym Network)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Konovolov et al. is the prosecution of 10 members of the GozNym cybercriminal network, which used GozNym malware to steal online banking credentials from over 41,000 victim computers and attempt approximately USD 100 million in theft. The case is *almost certainly* unprecedented in its use of simultaneous parallel prosecutions: while the US indicted 10 defendants in the Western District of Pennsylvania, Bulgaria, Georgia, Moldova, and Ukraine conducted parallel domestic prosecutions of their own nationals.

## Facts

The GozNym network exemplified "cybercrime as a service," assembling specialists recruited from underground criminal forums. Each member played a specialized role: malware development, credential theft, money mule management, or "cashing out" stolen funds. The network primarily targeted US businesses and financial institutions.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

This was described by DOJ as an "unprecedented" international law enforcement operation involving six countries. Europol coordinated the multinational investigation and Eurojust facilitated judicial cooperation.

### Parallel Prosecutions

Rather than extraditing all defendants to the US, participating countries conducted their own prosecutions:
- **Georgia**: Prosecuted Konovolov domestically
- **Bulgaria**: Prosecuted several defendants while extraditing Nikolov to the US
- **Moldova and Ukraine**: Conducted domestic proceedings against their nationals

### Extradition

Krasimir Nikolov was extradited from Bulgaria to the US to face charges.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The Western District of Pennsylvania asserted jurisdiction because the network targeted US businesses and financial institutions and because substantial victim and investigative effects were centered in the United States.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Parallel prosecutions versus extradition:** The case tested whether synchronized domestic prosecutions could substitute for mass extradition without sacrificing accountability.
- **Sovereignty-respecting enforcement design:** It also showed how cybercrime enforcement can be structured to preserve national control over local defendants while still presenting a unified multinational case.

### Precedent Value

**High.** The case established a model for coordinated multinational cybercrime prosecutions where each country prosecutes its own nationals in parallel, avoiding the delays and complications of mass extradition. This approach *likely* reflects the growing maturity of international cybercrime cooperation frameworks.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2016 | Investigation begins |
| 2016-12 | Nikolov extradited from Bulgaria to the United States |
| 2019-04-10 | Nikolov pleaded guilty in Pittsburgh |
| 2019-05-16 | 10-count indictment unsealed in W.D. Pa. |
| 2019-05 | Parallel prosecutions announced in Bulgaria, Georgia, Moldova, and Ukraine |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The GozNym case *almost certainly* represents a high-water mark for multinational cybercrime prosecution cooperation, with six countries agreeing to a coordinated approach that respected each nation's sovereignty while ensuring comprehensive enforcement. Its main strength was structural: participating states distributed prosecutions across jurisdictions instead of depending on a single court to obtain custody over every suspect.

## Korean Relevance (한국 관련성)

No Korean involvement identified.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. What were the outcomes of the parallel prosecutions in Georgia, Bulgaria, Moldova, and Ukraine?
2. Were any additional defendants arrested after the initial announcement?
3. What specific role did the Budapest Convention play in enabling the cooperation?

title: United States v. Kutleshi (Rydox Marketplace)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Kutleshi is the Western District of Pennsylvania case tied to the [[rydox-marketplace-takedown|Rydox marketplace takedown]]. It is a strong example of a cybercrime-marketplace case where the operational takedown and the criminal case are publicly linked on the same day.

## Facts

DOJ alleged that Rydox was an illicit marketplace dedicated to selling stolen personal information, access devices, login credentials, and cybercrime tools. The public announcement said the site completed over 7,600 sales, offered more than 321,000 cybercrime products, served over 18,000 users, and generated at least USD 230,000 in revenue.

The charging theory focused on the role of administrators rather than lower-level vendors. Ardit Kutleshi and Jetmir Kutleshi were named in the U.S. indictment, while Shpend Sokoli was arrested in Albania for expected local prosecution.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The public DOJ release shows a multi-jurisdictional evidence picture: Kosovo and Albania for arrests, Malaysia for server seizure, and the United States for the indictment and domain seizure. The Office of International Affairs reportedly provided significant assistance.

### Arrest and Extradition

The most explicit cooperation feature is the Kosovo arrest of Ardit and Jetmir Kutleshi pursuant to a U.S. extradition request. Sokoli's arrest in Albania instead reflects a split-prosecution approach rather than a single extradition pipeline.

### Asset Recovery

The United States obtained judicial authorization to seize the `Rydox.cc` domain and approximately USD 225,000 in cryptocurrency. Malaysian authorities and the FBI also seized the Kuala Lumpur servers hosting the marketplace.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The Western District of Pennsylvania asserted jurisdiction because the marketplace sold tools and data derived from thousands of U.S. victims and because the criminal effects were felt in the United States even though the administrators and infrastructure were overseas.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Marketplace-admin liability:** The case focuses on administrator responsibility for a cybercrime marketplace rather than only on downstream fraud users.
- **Distributed enforcement design:** The operation combines extradition requests, local foreign prosecution, and infrastructure seizure in different countries, which is a mature multinational enforcement pattern.

### Precedent Value

The case has *high* value because it ties an internationally distributed takedown to a named U.S. indictment and shows how cybercrime-market infrastructure can be dismantled without requiring every suspect to be physically present in the United States at the outset.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2016-02 | Rydox allegedly begins operating |
| 2024-12-12 | Indictment unsealed in W.D. Pa. |
| 2024-12-12 | Ardit and Jetmir Kutleshi arrested in Kosovo |
| 2024-12-12 | Shpend Sokoli arrested in Albania for expected domestic prosecution |
| 2024-12-12 | Rydox domain, Malaysian servers, and cryptocurrency seized |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The action appears highly effective because it removed the domain, took the servers offline, secured arrests in two countries, and tied those steps to a named U.S. indictment. That is much stronger than an operation page built only on seizure language.

## Korean Relevance

No South Korean role is identified in the collected source. The case is still relevant because it shows how cybercrime marketplaces with relatively modest direct revenue can nonetheless justify full multinational action when they service a broader fraud ecosystem.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The public source does not yet provide a final case number or later disposition for the extradition proceedings.
2. The exact division of evidence between the U.S. prosecution and the planned Albanian prosecution is not spelled out publicly.
3. It remains unclear how mu

title: United States v. Allen Levinson (xDedic Tax-Fraud Buyer)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Allen Levinson is an xDedic-derived buyer case in which access purchased from the marketplace allegedly enabled a large-scale U.S. tax-refund fraud. It is one of the clearest examples of downstream harm flowing from a criminal access marketplace.

## Facts

DOJ stated that Levinson used access to U.S.-based accounting firms obtained through xDedic to file hundreds of false tax returns in the names of identity-theft victims. The scheme sought more than USD 63 million in fraudulent refunds, of which more than USD 5.5 million was actually paid by the IRS.

This case is analytically distinct from the admin cases because it shows what buyers did with xDedic access after purchase.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The case sits within the xDedic umbrella investigation, but its most visible foreign element is custody rather than broad technical cooperation. Even so, the purchased-access pathway was inseparable from the multinational marketplace investigation.

### Arrest and Extradition

Levinson was arrested in the United Kingdom on 2 December 2020 and extradited to the United States. He pleaded guilty in early 2022 and was sentenced in December 2022.

### Asset Recovery

The sentencing release emphasizes loss and imprisonment rather than a distinct asset-seizure package, though the fraud involved millions of dollars in tax refunds.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The Middle District of Florida had a direct U.S. nexus because the scheme targeted the United States Treasury and involved access to U.S.-based firms and U.S. taxpayer identities.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Marketplace-to-fraud causation:** The case demonstrates a traceable line from access-market purchases to real-world tax-refund fraud against the U.S. government.
- **Buyer accountability:** It shows that darknet access-market enforcement can extend beyond admins and sellers to end-user fraud operators.

### Precedent Value

The case has *medium to high* value because it concretizes the victim impact of xDedic and shows how access brokers indirectly fuel very large domestic financial crimes.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2014 to 2019 | Levinson allegedly runs tax-fraud scheme using access obtained through xDedic |
| 2019-01 | xDedic domains seized |
| 2020-12-02 | Levinson arrested in the United Kingdom |
| 2022-02-03 | Levinson pleads guilty upon arrival in the United States |
| 2022-12-05 | Levinson sentenced to 78 months |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests that xDedic enforcement was effective downstream as well as upstream. Authorities did not stop at marketplace seizure; they tracked a foreign buyer, secured extradition, and proved large-scale tax harm.

## Korean Relevance

No South Korean role is identified. The case is still relevant because access-market purchases often fuel secondary frauds well beyond the original compromise, a pattern Korean investigators also confront in multi-stage cybercrime.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The collected source does not provide the detailed charging instrument tying each purchased access point to specific refund filings.
2. Public materials do not disclose how much of the USD 5.5 million paid out was later recovered.
3. The exact xDedic listings or sellers used by Levinson are not identified in the sentencing release.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|------|-----|
| 1 | Nigerian National Sentenced to More Than Six Years in Federal Prison for International Tax Fraud Scheme | DOJ M.D. Fla. | 2022-12-05 | https://www.justice.gov/usao-mdfl/pr/nigerian-national-sentenced-more-six-years-federal-prison-international-tax-fraud |

title: United States v. Matthew Jones (Silk Road Vendor)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Jones is a Silk Road vendor case involving hundreds of completed online drug sales under multiple aliases.

## Facts

DOJ said Jones operated on Silk Road under aliases including "Caligirl" and distributed oxycodone and other controlled substances through the marketplace between April and September 2013. The public announcement frames the case as part of the early wave of follow-on prosecutions that converted marketplace transaction records into named federal defendants after the original Silk Road seizure.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Context

No extradition or foreign arrest was required, but the case depended on evidence recovered from the Silk Road platform and associated investigative records developed in the broader post-takedown federal investigation.

## Legal Analysis

### Precedent Value

The case is useful because it shows how DOJ moved from marketplace takedown to downstream vendor sentencing, establishing that the original Silk Road operation generated a durable stream of prosecutable vendor cases rather than only a single operator prosecution.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2013-04 to 2013-09 | Jones conducts Silk Road sales under multiple aliases |
| 2013-10 | Original Silk Road infrastructure seized in the Ross Ulbricht investigation |
| 2014-12-18 | DOJ announces Jones's sentence in the Middle District of Florida |

title: United States v. McNeely et al. (xDedic Buyers)
text:
## Summary

United States v. McNeely et al. is an xDedic-derived buyer case group in the Middle District of Florida. It demonstrates that the marketplace investigation did not stop with high-profile foreign defendants and also produced prison terms for downstream U.S.-based participants.

## Facts

The official DOJ victim-services page identifies McNeely, Carr, and Williams as defendants in an xDedic-related case. The public summary is short, but it confirms guilty pleas and sentences for all three, placing them within the downstream exploitation side of the xDedic ecosystem.

This matters because xDedic was not just an administrator problem. The marketplace also enabled domestic fraud operators who converted purchased access into separate criminal conduct.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

Even though the defendants were prosecuted in the United States, the case depended on the larger xDedic investigation, which DOJ says involved Belgium, Ukraine, Europol, the Dutch National Police, and Germany's BKA.

### Arrest and Extradition

No foreign arrest or extradition is identified for these defendants. The international element lies in the marketplace evidence and infrastructure seizure that supported later domestic prosecutions.

### Asset Recovery

The public summary does not describe a separate forfeiture package for this defendant group.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The Middle District of Florida had a direct nexus because xDedic sold unauthorized access devices affecting U.S. victims and because the resulting fraud cases were brought in that district.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Downstream-user accountability:** The case shows that access-market investigations can reach buyers and follow-on fraud actors, not only admins and sellers.
- **Evidence reuse across case layers:** It also illustrates how a single marketplace takedown can generate multiple prosecutions against distinct actor types over time.

### Precedent Value

The case has *medium* value because it helps map the prosecution ecosystem around xDedic and shows that domestic participants were also sentenced as part of the larger operation.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2019-01 | xDedic domains seized |
| 2022-03-29 | Brandon Williams pleads guilty |
| 2022-04-14 | T'Andre McNeely pleads guilty |
| 2022-04-19 | Michael Carr pleads guilty |
| 2022-04-20 | Carr judgment entered |
| 2022-06-23 | McNeely and Williams judgments entered |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests that xDedic enforcement had depth. Authorities were able to convert a multinational marketplace seizure into a layered prosecution set that included U.S.-based downstream actors years later.

## Korean Relevance

No South Korean role is identified. The case is still relevant because it shows how illicit access markets can generate cascades of secondary offending by actors far removed from the original compromise.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The public victim-services page does not provide the underlying factual narrative for each defendant.
2. The exact xDedic credentials or victim systems used by this defendant group are not described in the collected source.
3. It remains unclear how these defendants were prioritized relative to the broader 2024 xDedic roundup.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|------|-----|
| 1 | xDedic Marketplace victim-services case summary | DOJ M.D. Fla. | 2024-08-07 update | https://www.justice.gov/usao-mdfl/victim-witness-services/xDedic |

title: United States v. Jeremiah Sam Evans Miller (Operation PowerOFF)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Jeremiah Sam Evans Miller is the Central District of California prosecution of a RoyalStresser.com operator whose case formed part of Operation PowerOFF. The case is *likely* one of the most concrete publicly traceable U.S. prosecutorial outcomes tied to that broader multinational booter-service campaign.

## Facts

DOJ stated that Miller operated RoyalStresser.com, formerly known as Supremesecurityteam.com, as a DDoS-for-hire service. He pleaded guilty in April 2023 and was sentenced on 15 July 2024 to nine months in federal prison.

The DOJ sentencing release connects Miller's case directly to the December 2022 enforcement wave in which authorities seized 48 booter domains and charged six defendants. That linkage makes the case a useful judicial anchor for an operation that is otherwise often described through infrastructure seizures and coordination announcements.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The sentencing release does not enumerate specific foreign evidence used against Miller. Even so, because DOJ expressly framed the case as part of Operation PowerOFF, evidence or operational intelligence from UK and Dutch partners was *likely* part of the broader investigative environment that enabled the U.S. charges.

### Arrest and Extradition

No foreign arrest or extradition component has been identified for Miller individually.

### Asset Recovery

The sentencing release notes that a DOJ forfeiture specialist was handling seizure of the Astrostress domain in a related case, but it does not describe a separate Miller-specific forfeiture order.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The case used ordinary U.S. criminal jurisdiction because Miller was a domestic defendant running a booter service that attacked protected computers in the United States and abroad. What makes the case notable is not the jurisdictional theory itself, but its embedding within a multinational disruption campaign.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Operation-to-case conversion:** The case shows how a multinational disruption campaign can produce ordinary district-court prosecutions against identifiable administrators.
- **Service reconstitution risk:** Operation PowerOFF repeatedly confronted booter reappearance under new domains, so the deterrent value of prosecutions like Miller's is *likely* greater when paired with recurring seizures.

### Precedent Value

The case has *moderate* value as a model for linking international disruption activity to a concrete sentencing outcome that can be cited on a case page rather than only on an operation page.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2022-12-14 | DOJ announces PowerOFF charging wave against six defendants |
| 2023-04-06 | Miller pleads guilty |
| 2024-07-15 | Miller sentenced to 9 months |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests that Operation PowerOFF was not merely a domain-seizure exercise. It *likely* translated international coordination into at least some individualized criminal accountability, which strengthens the operation's deterrence narrative.

## Korean Relevance (한국 관련성)

South Korea was not directly involved in Miller's case. The case is still *potentially* relevant because Korean agencies confronting DDoS-for-hire services face the same problem of turning technical takedowns into sustained prosecutorial outcomes.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The public case materials do not specify the precise foreign-partner evidence tied to Miller personally.
2. The public record remains clearer on Miller than on some other PowerOFF defendants, creating an uneven case-level picture of the broader operation.
3. The exact deterrent impact of individual sentencings within PowerOFF remains unmeasured.

title: United States v. Nazarovi et al. (QQAAZZ)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Nazarovi et al. is the principal U.S. criminal case tied to the [[qqaazz-money-laundering-takedown|QQAAZZ money laundering network takedown]]. It focuses not on a single malware family, but on a laundering service that allegedly supported multiple major cybercrime groups.

## Facts

DOJ alleged that QQAAZZ opened and maintained hundreds of bank accounts around the world, used shell companies and false identity documents, and laundered tens of millions of dollars stolen from victims of computer fraud, wire fraud, and bank fraud. The network allegedly retained fees of 40 to 50 percent before returning the balance to cybercriminal clients.

The public DOJ summary linked QQAAZZ to cybercrime ecosystems involving Dridex, Trickbot, and GozNym. That makes the case especially useful for understanding how financial-service providers enable multiple malware crews at once.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

DOJ described a years-long investigation involving 16 countries and parallel investigative work across Europe. The Office of International Affairs reportedly coordinated requests for searches, arrests, extraditions, and evidence sharing, making this a textbook multinational laundering case.

### Arrest and Extradition

The press release emphasized parallel prosecutions in the United States, Portugal, Spain, and the United Kingdom rather than a single extradition funnel. It also reported more than 40 house searches in Latvia, Bulgaria, the United Kingdom, Spain, and Italy.

### Asset Recovery

DOJ specifically noted the seizure of a bitcoin-mining operation in Bulgaria associated with the group. The operation therefore combined criminal charges with infrastructure and financial disruption.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The Western District of Pennsylvania had a clear U.S. nexus because QQAAZZ allegedly laundered proceeds from cybercrime affecting U.S. victims and U.S. banks, including PNC Bank in Pittsburgh.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Cybercrime-enabler liability:** The case targets laundering specialists who did not necessarily conduct the initial hacks but allegedly made large-scale theft economically workable.
- **Parallel prosecution model:** It shows that money-laundering networks can be fragmented across jurisdictions and still be prosecuted through coordinated domestic cases instead of relying entirely on extradition.

### Precedent Value

The case has *high* value because it maps the financial-service layer of international cybercrime and shows how anti-money-laundering prosecution can cut across multiple malware families and victim sets.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2016 to 2019 | QQAAZZ allegedly launders cybercrime proceeds through global bank accounts |
| 2019-09-25 | First related QQAAZZ indictment unsealed in W.D. Pa. |
| 2020-03 | Maksim Boiko arrested by criminal complaint while visiting the United States |
| 2020-09-29 | Nazarovi indictment filed in W.D. Pa. |
| 2020-10-15 | DOJ announces international QQAAZZ operation and parallel prosecutions |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests that the operation was effective because it did not just seize assets or publish intelligence. It generated charges across several countries, disrupted bank-account infrastructure, and exposed the service layer connecting separate cybercrime groups.

## Korean Relevance

No South Korean role is identified in the collected source. The case remains relevant because Korean investigators confronting ransomware, banking-trojan, or BEC losses often face the same downstream laundering bottleneck that QQAAZZ allegedly served.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The collected source does not provide final dispositions for the 20 charged QQAAZZ defendants.
2. The exact boundary between U.S. prosecution and the European parallel cases is not fully detailed in public summaries.
3. Public records do not fully quantify how much of the launde

title: United States v. Odedeyi and Davies (xDedic U.K. Extradition Case)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Odedeyi and Davies is the U.K.-arrest branch of an xDedic-related tax-fraud prosecution in the Middle District of Florida. It is useful because it isolates the extradition-facing part of a larger multi-defendant scheme.

## Facts

DOJ alleged that Odedeyi, Davies, and others participated in a transnational conspiracy that used unauthorized access to U.S. business servers and stolen personal data to file false tax forms seeking refunds from the IRS. The indictment announcement framed the case as both wire fraud and identity theft.

This case sits upstream of the later sentencing of Adejumo and Jinadu, who handled related proceeds in the United States.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The public release credited the Office of International Affairs, FBI and IRS-CI international operations in Mission UK, and U.K. authorities. That indicates a formal cross-border process rather than a purely domestic tax-fraud file.

### Arrest and Extradition

Odedeyi and Davies were arrested by the U.K. National Extradition Unit in London and detained pending extradition. This is the clearest custody-related international feature of the case.

### Asset Recovery

The public announcement focused on charges and arrests rather than a separate forfeiture package.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The Middle District of Florida had a direct U.S. nexus because the fraud targeted the IRS and involved theft of U.S. personal data and use of U.S. financial channels.

### Key Legal Issues

- **International tax-fraud chain:** The case shows how access-market intrusions can lead to refund fraud conducted across several countries.
- **Parallel defendant treatment:** It also illustrates how some conspirators are prosecuted through U.S. arrests while others remain in foreign extradition proceedings.

### Precedent Value

The case has *medium* value because it clarifies the custody and extradition layer of the xDedic-enabled tax-fraud cluster.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2021-09-09 | Odedeyi and Davies arrested in London |
| 2021-09-15 | DOJ announces indictments |
| 2023-08 to 2023-11 | Related U.S.-based conspirators plead and are sentenced |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests that xDedic enforcement did not stop at domestic arrests. U.S. authorities also used U.K. extradition channels to pursue foreign conspirators linked to the same fraud chain.

## Korean Relevance

No South Korean role is identified. The case remains relevant because the same access-market infrastructure can support frauds against tax, banking, and commercial systems in multiple jurisdictions at once.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The collected source does not establish the final extradition outcome for Odedeyi and Davies.
2. The public record does not separate each defendant's precise role inside the conspiracy.
3. The total attempted tax-refund amount for this defendant subset is not specified in the indictment announcement.

## References

| 1 | Four Men Federally Charged in Transnational Wire Fraud and Identity Theft Conspiracy | DOJ M.D. Fla. | 2021-09-15 | https://www.justice.gov/usao-mdfl/pr/four-men-federally-charged-transnational-wire-fraud-and-identity-theft-conspiracy |

title: United States v. Odufuye and Nwoke (BEC)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Odufuye and Nwoke is a District of Connecticut BEC case that provides a concrete judicial counterpart to [[operation-wirewire|Operation WireWire]]. The case matters because it moved beyond arrest headlines into extradition, guilty pleas, sentencing, and restitution.

## Facts

DOJ described a spoofing-based business email compromise scheme that targeted controllers, CFOs, CEOs, nonprofits, and schools across the United States. The conspirators allegedly used deceptive executive-style emails and, in some instances, malware-bearing attachments to induce victims to send funds to accounts controlled by the scheme.

The public Connecticut materials describe one Torrington victim company that sent five wire transfers totaling more than USD 500,000 to accounts in Virginia, Florida, Washington, D.C., and Hong Kong. DOJ also identified dozens of wire confirmations linked to the conspirators' accounts and millions of dollars in intended losses.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The case had a clear cross-border evidence picture: the scheme used overseas actors, funds moved through foreign jurisdictions including Hong Kong, and DOJ specifically thanked the Office of International Affairs and foreign police partners for assistance.

### Arrest and Extradition

Odufuye was arrested in the United Kingdom in December 2016 and extradited to the United States. Nwoke was arrested in Mauritius on 8 May 2018 and extradited on 25 May 2018, which DOJ described in broader Operation WireWire materials as the first Mauritius extradition in more than 15 years.

### Asset Recovery

The collected case materials emphasize restitution rather than a large forfeiture action. Nwoke agreed to restitution of USD 662,053.87, and Odufuye was ordered to pay USD 921,497.87.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The District of Connecticut had a direct territorial and victim nexus because the spoofing campaign targeted Connecticut businesses and caused actual losses there, even though important actors and transfers were outside the United States.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Extradition-backed BEC enforcement:** The case shows that high-value spoofing schemes can support actual custody over foreign defendants rather than only fugitive indictments.
- **Hybrid email-fraud and malware conduct:** DOJ's references to malware-bearing attachments indicate that some BEC cases sit at the boundary between pure social-engineering fraud and more technical intrusion conduct.

### Precedent Value

The case has *high* practical value because it demonstrates a full enforcement arc for international BEC: indictment, foreign arrest, extradition, guilty plea, sentencing, and restitution.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2015 to 2016 | BEC scheme targets U.S. businesses, including Connecticut victims |
| 2016-12-19 | Odufuye arrested in the United Kingdom |
| 2016-12-20 | Seven-count indictment returned in D. Conn. |
| 2018-01-03 | Odufuye pleads guilty |
| 2018-05-08 | Nwoke arrested in Mauritius |
| 2018-05-25 | Nwoke extradited to the United States |
| 2018-12-12 | Odufuye sentenced to 45 months |
| 2019-05-07 | Nwoke pleads guilty |
| 2019-08-02 | Nwoke sentenced to 36 months |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

This case suggests that Operation WireWire was not just a sweep with diffuse outcomes. At least in this Connecticut matter, the operation translated into custody, convictions, restitution, and a durable evidentiary record.

## Korean Relevance

No South Korean involvement is identified in the collected source. The case is still relevant because export-oriented companies in Korea face the same spoofed-invoice and executive-impersonation risks that made this prosecution viable.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The public summaries do not identify every foreign bank-account jurisdiction used in the laundering chain.
2. The second defendant named in the original 2016 indictment is not fully

It supports comparison across xDedic, AlphaBay, Silk Road, and other dark-web prosecution clusters while avoiding unsupported expansion beyond the cited record.

title: United States v. Okpe and Obogo
text:
## Summary

United States v. Okpe and Obogo is a District of Arizona fraud prosecution arising from a multi-year cyber-enabled scheme that included business email compromise, work-from-home fraud, romance fraud, and credit-card abuse. The case is *highly likely* one of the cleaner examples in the repository where DOJ explicitly described both overseas arrests and extraditions in a BEC-related matter.

## Facts

According to DOJ, the conspiracy ran from December 2011 through January 2017 and targeted victims in Arizona and elsewhere in the United States. The scheme allegedly used phishing, account compromise, impersonation, fake job offers, counterfeit-check activity, and romance-scam narratives to cause more than USD 1 million in intended losses.

Okpe pleaded guilty in December 2022 to conspiracy to commit wire, bank, and mail fraud, as well as aggravated identity theft. Obogo pleaded guilty to the same fraud conspiracy. Their sentencing outcomes were materially different, but both convictions appear to stem from the same long-running fraud network.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The public DOJ releases do not enumerate each foreign evidence request, but DOJ said the Office of International Affairs provided significant assistance throughout the investigation. That strongly suggests formal cross-border legal process was used alongside FBI investigative work.

### Arrest and Extradition

This is the clearest international-cooperation feature of the case. Okpe was arrested in Malaysia at U.S. request and fought extradition for more than two years before transfer to Arizona. Obogo was arrested in the United Kingdom and also extradited in 2022.

### Asset Recovery

No public forfeiture or asset-freezing component was identified in the collected sources.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

U.S. jurisdiction was *likely* anchored by victim losses in Arizona, use of U.S. banks and businesses, and the defendants' participation in frauds directed at U.S. persons.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Hybrid cyber-enabled fraud charging:** The case combines BEC with several adjacent fraud modalities rather than treating them as separate conspiracies.
- **Extradition-intensive prosecution:** The public record underscores that physically securing defendants remained a central challenge.

### Precedent Value

The case has *high* practical precedent value because it demonstrates that a BEC-centered conspiracy can still be brought to U.S. sentencing even when the principal defendants are located in Malaysia and the United Kingdom.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2011-12 to 2017-01 | Fraud conspiracy operates |
| 2019 | Arizona case filed |
| 2022 | Okpe and Obogo extradited to Arizona |
| 2022-12 | Both defendants plead guilty |
| 2023-03-20 | Obogo sentenced to 1 year and 1 day |
| 2023-03-27 | Okpe sentenced to 4 years and 1 month |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests that U.S. cooperation with foreign partners was effective enough to move from investigation to custody and then to convictions. In practical terms, that is *likely* more valuable for deterrence than an indictment alone.

## Korean Relevance (한국 관련성)

South Korea was not directly involved. The case is still *likely* relevant to Korean investigators because BEC and adjacent financial-fraud schemes frequently move money through multiple jurisdictions and therefore depend on the same extradition and evidence-sharing mechanisms.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The collected sources do not identify the exact treaty channels used with Malaysia and the United Kingdom.
2. The public record does not quantify actual losses with the same precision as intended losses.
3. It remains unclear whether additional co-conspirators were charged or remained abroad.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- US DOJ (Office of Public Affairs), 2023-03-27: Foreign National Sentenced for Victimizing U.S. Persons T

title: United States v. Olivia Bolles (Silk Road Vendor)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Bolles is a Silk Road case involving a licensed doctor who sold controlled substances under the vendor alias \"MDPro.\"

## Facts

According to DOJ, Bolles operated as "MDPro" on Silk Road and mailed controlled substances to customers in the United States and abroad. The case is notable because it involved a licensed physician using a darknet marketplace as a retail distribution channel, with sales volume measured in hundreds of completed shipments.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Cross-Border Aspect

The public release indicates distribution to more than 17 countries, which gives the case a clear transnational sales dimension even though the available source does not identify a formal MLA or extradition step.

## Legal Analysis

### Precedent Value

The case shows that darknet enforcement was not limited to stereotypical anonymous criminal actors; licensed professionals were also drawn into marketplace-based drug distribution, and DOJ treated those cases as conventional federal narcotics prosecutions.

title: United States v. Bamidele Omotosho (xDedic Cyber Scams)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Omotosho is a major downstream xDedic case in which stolen credentials and PII purchased from the marketplace were used to run multiple separate fraud schemes. It is one of the clearest examples of the real-world harm xDedic access enabled.

## Facts

DOJ said Omotosho and his coconspirators purchased stolen access credentials and PII on xDedic, then used that information to commit at least six different fraud schemes. The government reported over USD 7.5 million in intended losses, over USD 2 million in actual losses, and restitution of USD 2,051,108.70.

Compared with other xDedic-related cases, this one is notable for the scale of punishment and the breadth of downstream fraud conduct.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The collected sentencing release does not emphasize foreign arrest process, but the core access and PII were allegedly sourced from xDedic, a marketplace dismantled through a multinational investigation. The case therefore sits downstream of that international evidence base.

### Arrest and Extradition

No foreign arrest or extradition element is established in the collected source.

### Asset Recovery

The public summary emphasizes restitution rather than forfeiture. The court ordered more than USD 2 million in restitution to victims.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The Middle District of Florida had jurisdiction because the fraud schemes targeted U.S. victims and used U.S.-based financial systems and identities, even though the access marketplace itself was multinational.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Marketplace-enabled fraud aggregation:** The case shows how prosecutors can aggregate multiple follow-on frauds that arise from one criminal access marketplace.
- **Access-to-loss linkage:** It also provides a strong illustration of how purchased credentials translate into measurable intended and actual losses.

### Precedent Value

The case has *high* value because it provides one of the strongest sentencing records in the xDedic ecosystem and quantifies the downstream victim impact of market-bought access.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2017 to 2018 | Omotosho-led group allegedly uses xDedic-sourced data in multiple fraud schemes |
| 2022-11-02 | Omotosho pleads guilty |
| 2024-07-23 | Sentenced to 12 years and 7 months |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests that marketplace takedowns can generate substantial delayed accountability when investigators continue following the access into downstream fraud schemes and victim losses.

## Korean Relevance

No South Korean role is identified. The case remains relevant because compromised-access markets often serve as starting points for diverse fraud schemes that later cross many sectors and jurisdictions.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The public source does not specify which of the six fraud schemes caused the largest losses.
2. The exact custody history before Omotosho's plea is not described in the collected release.
3. The public record does not separate which stolen credentials came directly from xDedic versus related channels.

## References

| 1 | Nigerian National Sentenced to More Than 12 Years in Federal Prison for Cyber Scams | DOJ M.D. Fla. | 2024-07-23 | https://www.justice.gov/usao-mdfl/pr/nigerian-national-sentenced-more-12-years-federal-prison-cyber-scams |

title: United States v. Dariy Pankov (xDedic / NLBrute)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Pankov is an xDedic-related prosecution focused on a major seller-side actor rather than a marketplace administrator or buyer. That makes it one of the more analytically useful cases in the xDedic cluster.

## Facts

DOJ alleged that Pankov developed `NLBrute`, malware capable of decrypting login credentials and compromising protected computers around the world. He allegedly used the tool to obtain tens of thousands of credentials and sold those credentials, including through the xDedic Marketplace, earning more than USD 358,000 in proceeds.

Unlike the core administrator cases, this prosecution shows how the xDedic ecosystem depended on upstream malware-enabled credential supply.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The prosecution sits within the same broad xDedic investigation that involved Belgium, Ukraine, Europol, and other foreign partners. Even where the Pankov case is more individualized, the evidence picture is still inherently multinational.

### Arrest and Extradition

Pankov was taken into custody in the Republic of Georgia on 4 October 2022 and extradited to the United States. DOJ later stated that he pleaded guilty and was sentenced to 60 months in prison.

### Asset Recovery

The indictment notified Pankov that the United States sought forfeiture of USD 358,437, alleged to be traceable to the offense proceeds.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The Middle District of Florida asserted jurisdiction because the xDedic ecosystem sold unauthorized access devices and facilitated fraud affecting U.S. victims and infrastructure, including Florida-linked victims.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Supplier-side cybercrime liability:** The case shows that providing credential-harvesting malware can be prosecuted as part of a broader marketplace ecosystem even when the defendant is not the platform operator.
- **Marketplace-enabled fraud chain:** It also illustrates how malware development, credential theft, and criminal resale markets can be treated as one integrated criminal enterprise.

### Precedent Value

The case has *high* value because it captures the supply chain behind access marketplaces and shows that extradition can reach not only admins but also key technical enablers.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| Pre-2019 | Pankov allegedly develops and uses NLBrute in the xDedic ecosystem |
| 2019-01 | xDedic infrastructure seized |
| 2022-10-04 | Pankov arrested in Georgia |
| 2023-02-22 | DOJ announces extradition to the United States |
| 2023-09-14 | DOJ announces guilty plea |
| 2024-01-04 | DOJ roundup lists 60-month sentence |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests that the xDedic operation achieved more than site takedown. It reached a technical enabler in a third country, secured extradition, and converted that into conviction and sentence.

## Korean Relevance

No South Korean role is identified. The case remains relevant because access-broker ecosystems and brute-force tooling often underpin attacks against globally exposed RDP and enterprise infrastructure, including in Asia.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The public source does not provide the full case number in the extradition release.
2. The exact overlap between Pankov's credential inventory and xDedic's total listings is not quantified.
3. Public materials do not fully describe which jurisdictions hosted the most affected systems.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|------|-----|
| 1 | Russian Malware Developer Arrested and Extradited to the United States | DOJ M.D. Fla. | 2023-02-22 | https://www.justice.gov/usao-mdfl/pr/russian-malware-developer-arrested-and-extradited-united-states |

title: United States v. Behrouz Parsarad
text:
## Summary

United States v. Behrouz Parsarad is the named U.S. prosecution that followed the March 2024 seizure of Nemesis Market infrastructure. DOJ and Treasury materials make clear that the case depends on cross-border cooperation with Germany and Lithuania and on evidence preserved after the takedown.

## Facts

DOJ alleged that Parsarad founded and operated Nemesis Market, a darknet marketplace that sold narcotics and cybercrime-related services. The official U.S. materials say the market processed more than 400,000 orders, and the case was announced after the infrastructure had already been seized in cooperation with German and Lithuanian partners.

## International Cooperation Elements

German and Lithuanian assistance is expressly acknowledged in DOJ materials, and the DOJ announcement says the Cybercrime Liaison Prosecutor to [[eurojust]] and the Office of International Affairs provided significant help. Treasury's sanctions release adds a parallel financial-enforcement layer.

## Legal Analysis

The case is significant because it shows how infrastructure seizure can mature into attribution, sanctions and charges without an immediate arrest. The current public record still leaves open whether Parsarad will ever be brought into custody, but it already provides a concrete model for transnational evidence conversion into a U.S. criminal case.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2022-10 | Nemesis investigation already underway according to BKA |
| 2024-03-20 | Nemesis infrastructure seized in Germany and Lithuania |
| 2025-03-04 | OFAC sanctions Parsarad |
| 2025-04-17 | DOJ and USAO announce charges against Parsarad |

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- DOJ OPA, 2025-04-17: Iranian National Indicted for Operating Online Marketplace Offering Fentanyl and Money Laundering Services.
- US DOJ USAO, 2025-04-17: Iranian National Indicted for Operating Online Marketplace Offering Fentanyl, Other Drugs, and Money Laundering Services.
- U.S. Department of the Treasury, 2025-03-04: Treasury Sanctions Head of Online Darknet Marketplace Tied to Fentanyl Sales.
- Bundeskriminalamt / ZIT Frankfurt, 2024-03-21: Illegaler Darknet-Marktplatz "Nemesis Market" abgeschaltet.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- DOJ announced on 17 April 2025 that Behrouz Parsarad was charged for founding and operating Nemesis Market.
- The release ties the later criminal case to the 20 March 2024 multinational infrastructure seizure and explicitly highlights German and Lithuanian assistance.
- DOJ announced on April 17, 2025 that Behrouz Parsarad, alleged administrator of Nemesis Market, was charged in the District of Columbia for operating the darknet marketplace seized in March 2024.
- The release links the later criminal case to the earlier infrastructure takedown.
- This Northern District of Ohio release gives the district-level prosecutorial framing for the Nemesis indictment, including the charging theories, the role of J-CODE and the importance of German and Lithuanian assistance.
- contraband made accessible on the darknet for more than three years,” said Matthew...from the harms caused by fentanyl and darknet marketplaces and pursuing accountability
- OFAC sanctioned Behrouz Parsarad on 4 March 2025 as the alleged sole administrator of Nemesis.
- Treasury stated that Nemesis had over 30,000 active users and 1,000 vendors and facilitated nearly USD 30 million in drug sales between 2021 and 2024.

title: United States v. Roger Thomas Clark (Silk Road Adviser)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Clark is a major Silk Road prosecution involving the marketplace operator's long-time adviser and facilitator.

## Facts

DOJ characterized Clark, also known as "Variety Jones," as a senior adviser to Ross Ulbricht who helped shape Silk Road's operations at a strategic level rather than simply acting as a vendor. The public announcement ties him to narcotics conspiracy and other core marketplace conduct over Silk Road's 2011-2013 operating period.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Transnational Dimension

Clark was a Canadian national, and the case underscores that U.S. prosecutors continued to pursue non-U.S. participants in Silk Road leadership years after the marketplace itself was shut down. The available source does not detail the full cross-border evidentiary process, but the case clearly belongs to the international tail of the Silk Road investigation.

## Legal Analysis

### Precedent Value

The case is important because it expands accountability beyond the site operator and shows that senior advisers who help manage or sustain a darknet marketplace can face operator-level sentencing exposure.

title: United States v. Stepanov et al. (DanaBot — Operation Endgame)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Stepanov et al. is the 16-count indictment of 16 Russian nationals for operating the DanaBot malware network, announced on 23 May 2025 as part of [[operation-endgame-phase2|Operation Endgame Phase 2]]. DanaBot infected 300,000+ computers worldwide and caused an estimated USD 50 million+ in financial damages. The malware served dual purposes: cybercrime (credential theft, fraud) and espionage (targeting government and military entities).

> [!warning] Source verification needed
> Key details (16 defendants, 300K infections, USD 50M damages) are primarily from Tier 3 news sources. Official DOJ indictment text has not been independently verified due to justice.gov access restrictions.

## Facts

DanaBot was a banking trojan that evolved into a multi-purpose botnet platform. Key named defendants include Aleksandr Stepanov (aka "JimmBee"), 39, and Artem Kalinkin (aka "Onix"), 34, both of Novosibirsk, Russia. The malware's dual-use nature (cybercrime and espionage) distinguishes it from other botnet prosecutions.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Multinational Operation

The DanaBot indictment was part of Operation Endgame Phase 2, involving seven countries (Canada, Denmark, France, Germany, Netherlands, United Kingdom, United States) coordinated by Europol and Eurojust.

### Infrastructure Takedown

Concurrent with the indictment, the broader operation resulted in 300 servers taken down, 650 domains neutralized, EUR 3.5 million in cryptocurrency seized, 20 international arrest warrants, and 18 EU Most Wanted list additions.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

Public reporting suggests US jurisdiction was based on DanaBot's impact on US victims and networks, but the precise district-level theory remains under-documented because the underlying court record is not yet well surfaced in official sources.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Source-verification gap:** The present public narrative relies heavily on secondary reporting, creating a documentation problem that is unusual for a supposedly major US indictment.
- **Hybrid malware use-case:** DanaBot's alleged use for both financially motivated crime and espionage complicates classification, victim analysis, and attribution narratives.

### Precedent Value

**Medium.** If the official charging record is confirmed, the case will matter as part of a very large coordinated botnet action, but its documentary value remains limited until primary-source court materials are secured.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2025-05-19 to 22 | Operation Endgame Phase 2 action days |
| 2025-05-23 | 16-count indictment unsealed; Europol announcement |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case shows strong operational cooperation because the indictment ran in parallel with a broad infrastructure action across multiple jurisdictions. Its weakness is documentary rather than operational: without a fully confirmed primary-source court record, the legal durability of the public narrative is harder to assess.

## Korean Relevance (한국 관련성)

No Korean involvement identified.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. Official DOJ source for DanaBot indictment details needs confirmation.
2. Are any of the 16 defendants likely to be apprehended given their location in Russia?
3. What was the scope of DanaBot's espionage activity (government/military targeting)?

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- US DOJ, 2025-05-23: Leader of Qakbot Malware Conspiracy Indicted for Involvement in Global Ransomware Scheme.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- As part of Operation Endgame Phase 2 (announced 23 May 2025), the US Department of Justice unsealed a 16-count indictment charging 16 Russian nationals for operating the DanaBot malware network.
- Key Named Defendants - **Aleksandr Stepanov** (aka "JimmBee"), 39, of Novosibirsk, Russia

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_END -->

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|

title: United States v. Ulbricht (Silk Road)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Ulbricht is the landmark criminal prosecution of Ross William Ulbricht, creator and operator of the Silk Road dark web marketplace, in the Southern District of New York. Ulbricht was convicted on all seven counts on 4 February 2015 and sentenced to two concurrent life terms plus 40 years on 29 May 2015, with a forfeiture order of USD 183,961,921. At the time, this was *almost certainly* the most severe sentence ever imposed for a cybercrime.

Ulbricht was pardoned by President Trump on 21 January 2025 after serving approximately 11 years.

## Facts

The Silk Road operated from approximately February 2011 to October 2013 as a Tor-hidden marketplace facilitating an estimated USD 1.2 billion in illegal transactions (primarily narcotics) using Bitcoin. The FBI arrested Ulbricht at the Glen Park Branch Library in San Francisco on 1 October 2013 while he was logged in as "Dread Pirate Roberts." The FBI seized approximately 173,991 BTC (~USD 33.6 million at the time).

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The FBI obtained a cloned image of the Silk Road server from Icelandic police. The server was hosted at a data center in Reykjavik. The legal basis for this cooperation is disputed: the government initially stated it had an MLAT with Iceland, but an assistant US attorney later acknowledged this was "not technically correct." The cooperation was *likely* based on informal police-to-police channels.

### Arrest and Extradition

- **Gary Davis** (alias "Libertas"), a Silk Road administrator, was arrested in Ireland in January 2014 and extradited to the US in July 2018 after fighting extradition for over four years. He was sentenced to 78 months in July 2019.
- **Roger Thomas Clark** (alias "Variety Jones"), Ulbricht's senior adviser, was arrested in Thailand in December 2015 and extradited to the US in June 2018. He was sentenced to 20 years in July 2023.

### Asset Recovery

Total cryptocurrency seized: ~173,991 BTC from initial seizures, plus ~69,370 BTC via 2020 civil forfeiture. Forfeiture order: USD 183,961,921.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

The US asserted jurisdiction based on the criminal conduct's effects within the United States and the involvement of US-based infrastructure, despite the server being located in Iceland and the marketplace operating on the Tor network.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Server identification method**: The FBI's account of discovering the server IP via a misconfigured CAPTCHA has been disputed by security researchers.
- **MLAT with Iceland**: The government's initial misstatement about having an MLAT with Iceland raised evidentiary concerns, though the court found no grounds for suppression.
- **Corrupt investigators**: DEA Agent Carl Force and Secret Service Agent Shaun Bridges were convicted of stealing Bitcoin during the investigation, but the court found their misconduct did not affect Ulbricht's conviction.

### Precedent Value

**Landmark.** The case established precedents for: (1) life imprisonment for cybercrime; (2) large-scale cryptocurrency seizure; (3) cross-border dark web server identification and evidence collection; (4) the "successor marketplace" problem requiring sustained enforcement.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2013-10-01 | Ulbricht arrested in San Francisco |
| 2013-10-02 | Criminal complaint unsealed |
| 2014-02-04 | Superseding indictment (S1 14 Cr. 68) |
| 2015-01-13 | Trial begins (Judge Katherine B. Forrest) |
| 2015-02-04 | Guilty verdict on all seven counts |
| 2015-05-29 | Sentenced to life imprisonment; USD 183M forfeiture |
| 2017-05-31 | Second Circuit affirms conviction and sentence |
| 2018-07 | Gary Davis extradited from Ireland |
| 2019-07-19 | Davis sentenced to 78 months |
| 2023-07 | Roger Thomas Clark sentenced to 20 years |
| 2025-01-21 | Ulbricht pardoned by President Trump |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case demonstrated both strengths and limitations of pre-

title: United States v. Wu Haibo et al.
text:
## Summary

United States v. Wu Haibo et al. is the public charging case associated with the 5 March 2025 DOJ action against i-Soon personnel, Chinese law-enforcement officers, and APT27-linked operators. It is one of the clearest examples in the repository of a cybercrime indictment that doubles as strategic attribution.

## Facts

DOJ stated that the defendants participated in computer intrusions conducted both for Chinese security services and for private profit. The public announcement described charges involving computer hacking, wire fraud, identity theft, and related conspiracies and tied the defendants to a larger ecosystem that included i-Soon employees and Chinese state actors.

The indictment was announced alongside Treasury sanctions and a Rewards for Justice offer. That package indicates the U.S. government treated the case not only as a prosecution but also as a broader coercive and signaling action.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Evidence Gathering

The case likely relied on victim and infrastructure evidence from multiple jurisdictions because the announced intrusion campaigns were global in scope. Public materials do not specify MLA channels, but the factual theory almost certainly depended on cross-border digital evidence consolidation.

### Arrest and Extradition

No foreign arrest or extradition step is publicly associated with the case. The defendants are believed to remain in China, making custodial enforcement highly unlikely in the near term.

### Asset Recovery

The public enforcement package emphasized sanctions rather than criminal forfeiture. Treasury measures nonetheless functioned as an asset-denial and compliance-enforcement tool parallel to the indictment.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

U.S. jurisdiction was *likely* grounded in intrusions affecting U.S. victims, U.S.-located infrastructure, and the use of U.S. financial or communications channels tied to the charged offenses.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Attribution without custody:** The case shows how prosecutors can build a detailed federal indictment even when arrest is improbable.
- **State-linked hybrid conduct:** The allegations blur the line between state-directed espionage and profit-seeking cybercrime, complicating ordinary mutual-assistance expectations.

### Precedent Value

The case has *high* precedent value because it demonstrates the practical U.S. model for dealing with foreign state-linked cyber actors: criminal charges, economic sanctions, and public naming rather than near-term trial.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2025-03-05 | DOJ announces indictment in the District of Columbia |
| 2025-03-05 | Treasury sanctions announced in parallel |
| 2025-03-05 | Rewards for Justice offer publicized |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case is *likely* effective as an attribution and deterrence measure even if it does not immediately produce arrests. Its weakness is the absence of any realistic short-term custody path.

## Korean Relevance (?쒓뎅 愿?⑥꽦)

South Korea was not identified as a direct participant in the U.S. charging action. The case is still highly relevant because Korean public and private entities face the same state-linked intrusion patterns that make purely domestic investigation insufficient.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. The public announcement summarizes a broad defendant set, but the exact role of each named actor requires underlying charging documents for full differentiation.
2. Public sources do not specify the principal foreign victim jurisdictions reflected in the indictment.
3. It remains unclear whether any defendants will ever be arrested outside China.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|------|-----|
| 1 | Justice Department Charges 12 Chinese Contract Hackers and Law Enforcement Officers in Global Computer Intrusion Campaigns | DOJ OPA | 2025-03-05 | https://www.justice.gov/opa/pr/justice-department-ch

title: United States v. Yakubets and Turashev (Dridex/Evil Corp)
text:
## Summary

United States v. Yakubets and Turashev is the December 2019 indictment of Maksim V. Yakubets (aka "aqua"), leader of the Evil Corp cybercriminal organization, and Igor Turashev for conspiracy, computer hacking, wire fraud, and bank fraud related to the Dridex (Bugat/Cridex) banking trojan. The Dridex malware infected computers in 40+ countries and caused over USD 100 million in theft.

The case is *almost certainly* the most significant example of the US government's "three-pronged" approach to cybercrime: combining criminal indictment, OFAC economic sanctions (17 individuals, 7 entities), and a USD 5 million reward — the largest cyber reward at the time of announcement.

## Facts

Evil Corp is a Russia-based cybercriminal organization led by Yakubets and operating primarily from Moscow. The Dridex malware evolved through several versions (Cridex, Bugat v4, Dridex/Bugat v5) and spread through phishing emails containing malicious Microsoft Office documents. According to the US Treasury, Yakubets also provided direct assistance to the Russian government's malicious cyber efforts, highlighting the nexus between state-sponsored and criminal cyber operations.

## International Cooperation Elements

### FBI-NCA Joint Investigation

The FBI (Pittsburgh Field Office) and UK National Crime Agency conducted a parallel investigation, with evidence sharing facilitated by the US-UK MLAT.

### Ghinkul Arrest and Extradition

Andrey Ghinkul, a Dridex botnet administrator, was arrested in Cyprus in August 2015, extradited to the US in February 2016, and sentenced to time served in December 2018.

### Technical Disruption

On 13 October 2015, the FBI, NCA, Dell SecureWorks, and Shadowserver Foundation executed a botnet sinkholing operation against the Dridex P2P network.

### Sanctions

OFAC designated 17 individuals and 7 entities associated with Evil Corp under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), freezing all US-jurisdictional assets.

## Legal Analysis

### Precedent Value

**Landmark.** The combined indictment + sanctions + reward approach became a template for subsequent cybercrime enforcement actions, particularly against Russian-origin groups where arrest is not feasible. The OFAC sanctions imposed real economic costs on Evil Corp and created legal complications for any entity interacting with designated individuals.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2015-08 | Ghinkul arrested in Cyprus |
| 2015-10-13 | Ghinkul indictment announced; Dridex botnet sinkholed |
| 2016-02 | Ghinkul extradited to US |
| 2018-12-06 | Ghinkul sentenced to time served |
| 2019-12-05 | Yakubets and Turashev indictment unsealed |
| 2019-12-05 | OFAC sanctions against Evil Corp |
| 2019-12-05 | USD 5 million reward announced |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The FBI-NCA bilateral cooperation is *almost certainly* a model for US-UK cybercrime investigations. However, the principal targets (Yakubets and Turashev) remain at large in Russia, and Evil Corp subsequently pivoted to ransomware operations — an outcome assessment of **partial success**.

## Korean Relevance (한국 관련성)

No Korean involvement identified. However, the sanctions-based enforcement model is relevant as a reference for Korean authorities considering how to apply economic pressure on cybercriminal organizations beyond traditional prosecution.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. Yakubets's alleged ties to Russian government cyber operations raise questions about state protection.
2. Evil Corp's pivot to ransomware (WastedLocker, Hades) after sanctions undermines the effectiveness narrative.
3. The USD 5 million reward has not resulted in Yakubets's arrest as of April 2026.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- US DOJ (OPA / W.D. Pa.), 2019-12-05: Russian National Charged with Decade-Long Series of Hacking and Bank Fraud Offenses Resulting in Tens of Millions in Losses and Second Russian..
- US DOJ (W.D. Pa.), 2015-10-13

title: United States v. YunHe Wang (911 S5 Botnet)
text:
## Summary

United States v. YunHe Wang is the central U.S. criminal case tied to the 911 S5 botnet dismantling announced in May 2024. DOJ stated that Wang operated a large residential proxy service built from millions of compromised devices and enabled a broad range of cyber-enabled offenses worldwide.

## Facts

The indictment was filed in the Southern District of Texas and announced alongside coordinated enforcement actions involving partners in Germany, Singapore, and Thailand. DOJ alleged that 911 S5 compromised more than 19 million IP addresses and generated tens of millions of dollars in criminal proceeds.

The public announcement described 911 S5 as a residential proxy service built from compromised devices rather than a conventional botnet used only for spam or DDoS. That distinction matters because the service allegedly enabled downstream fraud, bomb threats, child exploitation offenses, and sanctions evasion by selling access to infected IP addresses.

## International Cooperation Elements

### Foreign Enforcement Support

The public release attributes the disruption to multinational cooperation among U.S., German, Singaporean, and Thai authorities. That strongly indicates cross-border evidence gathering and operational support rather than a purely domestic U.S. investigation.

### Asset Measures

DOJ paired the indictment with forfeiture allegations, while Treasury announced related sanctions. This combination likely increased the disruption effect beyond the criminal charges alone.

## Legal Analysis

### Jurisdiction

U.S. jurisdiction was *likely* grounded in the service's extensive effects on U.S. victims, the use of U.S.-linked communications and financial channels, and the ability of U.S. authorities to tie criminal proceeds and infrastructure to the charged conduct.

### Key Legal Issues

- **Residential proxy as criminal infrastructure:** The case helps frame botnet-enabled proxy services as prosecutable core infrastructure rather than merely neutral routing tools.
- **Cross-border seizure and arrest coordination:** The prosecution appears to depend on synchronized action across several jurisdictions rather than unilateral U.S. arrest power.

### Precedent Value

The case has *high* practical value because it links a globally distributed proxy-botnet service to a named federal indictment and parallel sanctions, providing a concrete courtroom anchor for a major multinational disruption.

## Proceedings Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2022-2024 | 911 S5 allegedly operates at global scale |
| 2024-05-22 | Federal indictment filed in the Southern District of Texas |
| 2024-05-29 | DOJ publicly announces the case and parallel disruption measures |
| 2024-05-29 | Treasury sanctions and forfeiture-related measures announced in parallel |

## Cooperation Effectiveness

The case suggests the operation was effective because it combined arrest activity, infrastructure disruption, forfeiture allegations, and sanctions in a single public enforcement moment. That bundled approach is *likely* more disruptive than indictment alone.

## Korean Relevance (?쒓뎅 愿?⑥꽦)

South Korea was not identified as a direct participant. The case is still highly relevant because proxy-botnet services of this type can mask fraud and intrusion traffic affecting Korean institutions and users.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

1. Public DOJ materials do not fully describe the extradition or transfer posture following the Singapore arrest activity.
2. The exact split of investigative labor between Germany, Singapore, and Thailand remains undisclosed.
3. Final disposition of the criminal case is not yet reflected in public sentencing materials.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|------|-----|
| 1 | Justice Department Leads Effort Among Multinational Partners to Dismantle World's Largest Botnet | DOJ OPA | 2024-05-29 | https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/justice-department-leads-e

title: Data Sovereignty and Localization Requirements
text:
## Summary

Data sovereignty is the challenge created when data relevant to an investigation is stored, controlled, routed, or legally protected in a jurisdiction different from the investigating authority.

## Cooperation Use

Cybercrime investigations often need provider-held content, subscriber records, traffic data, logs, cloud backups, or payment records. Those records may be physically stored in one state, controlled by a provider in another, and relevant to victims in several more. Data-sovereignty concerns shape whether investigators use MLAT, direct provider requests, CLOUD Act executive agreements, emergency preservation, or local partner action.

## Boundary Notes

Use this challenge page when legal control over data is the bottleneck. Use [[electronic-evidence]] for the evidence type and [[jurisdictional-conflicts]] when overlapping legal demands or blocking laws create direct conflict.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | [[2026-04-29_justice-gov_cloud-act-executive-agreements|Regarding CLOUD Act Executive Agreements]] | U.S. Department of Justice | 2023-10-01 | https://www.justice.gov/es/node/1397306 |
| [2] | [[2022-05-12_coe-int_second-additional-protocol-cets-no-224|Second Additional Protocol (CETS No. 224)]] | Council of Europe | 2022-05-12 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/second-additional-protocol |

title: Jurisdictional Conflicts in Cybercrime
text:
## Summary

Jurisdictional conflicts arise when more than one state has a plausible claim to investigate, prosecute, demand data, restrain assets, or control a suspect in the same cybercrime matter.

## Cooperation Use

Conflicts can involve overlapping victim states, infrastructure states, suspect nationality, provider location, and data-protection law. They can delay evidence access, complicate extradition, or force prosecutors to choose between parallel proceedings and a lead-jurisdiction model. Formal treaties, executive agreements, and coordination bodies reduce but do not remove these conflicts.

## Boundary Notes

Use this page for overlapping authority problems. Use [[data-sovereignty]] when the core issue is data control and [[extraterritorial-jurisdiction]] when the page focuses on one state's legal reach.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | [[2026-04-29_justice-gov_cloud-act-executive-agreements|Regarding CLOUD Act Executive Agreements]] | U.S. Department of Justice | 2023-10-01 | https://www.justice.gov/es/node/1397306 |
| [2] | [[2026-04-17_coe-int_about-the-convention-budapest-convention|About the Convention — Budapest Convention]] | Council of Europe | 2026-04-17 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/the-budapest-convention |

title: Cobalt Strike (Adversary Simulation Tool)
text:
## Summary

Cobalt Strike is a dual-use adversary-simulation framework that appears in the corpus because criminal actors abuse cracked or unauthorized copies as command-and-control tooling. The menu node helps separate the tool from a specific malware family or threat group.

## International-Cooperation Relevance

Operation Morpheus-style records show why a dual-use tool can become a cooperation issue. The legal target is not the legitimate security product; it is the unauthorized infrastructure and criminal use pattern around servers, licenses, payloads, and operators. Investigators need hosting records, server seizures, malware telemetry, and private-sector analysis to distinguish legitimate testing from criminal deployment.

## Menu Use

Use this page when a source explicitly turns on Cobalt Strike infrastructure, beacons, cracked copies, or law-enforcement disruption of unauthorized servers. Use [[malware-ic]] for the broader malware case and [[cybercrime-infrastructure-ic]] when Cobalt Strike is only one component in a larger hosting or loader ecosystem.

## Analytical Use

Within this wiki, **Cobalt Strike (Adversary Simulation Tool)** is a control node for comparing records that describe the analytical vocabulary used to compare operations, cases, and source records. Use the page to separate three questions that often become mixed in operation narratives: what conduct or authority is being discussed, which agency or state actor exercised it, and where the cross-border dependency appears in the evidence chain. That separation makes the menu more useful for comparing takedowns, prosecutions, extradition matters, and assistance requests without treating every related record as the same kind of cooperation event.

## Integrity Notes

The page groups terminology; it is not independent proof that the concept, crime type, mechanism, or framework applied in every linked matter. Operation and case pages remain the controlling records for arrests, seizures, requests, treaty use, and participating agencies. When adding future links, keep the distinction between direct source evidence, inferred analytical classification, and catalog navigation visible in the target page.
