title: Republic of Poland
text:
## Summary

Poland is a European Union member and **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party**. Its cybercrime law enforcement underwent a major institutional reform in 2022 with the creation of the **Central Bureau for Combating Cybercrime** (*Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości*, **CBZC**), a dedicated national cybercrime investigation service within the Polish Police.

Poland participates in EU cybercrime cooperation frameworks through Europol's EC3 and Eurojust.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

Poland's primary cybercrime provisions are contained in the **Criminal Code** (*Kodeks karny*, 1997, amended):
- Art. 267: Unauthorized access / interception
- Art. 268-268a: Data interference
- Art. 269-269b: System interference, computer sabotage

Poland's Criminal Code implements the substantive law requirements of the Budapest Convention following accession.

### Procedural Powers

Under the Code of Criminal Procedure, Polish law enforcement has access to search and seizure, production orders, data preservation, and lawful interception. *Likely* compliant with Budapest Convention procedural requirements.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority for MLAT requests is the **Ministry of Justice** (*Ministerstwo Sprawiedliwości*).

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratification 2015-02-20) [1]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol (CETS 224)]]** — signatory (2022-05-12) [1]

## Key Agencies

- **[[poland-police|Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości (CBZC)]]** — Central Bureau for Combating Cybercrime, established 12 January 2022 [2][3]

## Cooperation Track Record

Poland *likely* has a strong track record in EU joint operations, particularly through Europol EC3. Specific operation participation records require further research.

## Capacity Assessment

With CBZC's establishment in 2022, Poland has *highly likely* strengthened its institutional cybercrime capacity. EU membership and Europol integration support medium-to-high IC responsiveness.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Exact count of CBZC staff and operational footprint not confirmed
- Second Additional Protocol ratification status pending
- Specific operation participation records need source backing

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Chart of Signatures and Ratifications of Treaty 185 | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |
| [2] | About CBZC (Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości) | Polish Police / CBZC | 2022 | https://cbzc.policja.gov.pl/ |
| [3] | Poland establishes Central Bureau to fight cybercrime | CoE Cybercrime | 2022 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/-/poland-central-bureau |

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> Exact ratification date for Second Additional Protocol (CETS 224) should be verified against Council of Europe Treaty Office.

title: Portuguese Republic
text:
## Summary

Portugal is an EU member state and **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party**. Its 2009 Cybercrime Law (*Lei do Cibercrime*, Law 109/2009) is widely cited as a close implementation of the Budapest Convention. The **Polícia Judiciária** (PJ) — Portugal's criminal investigation police — hosts the **Unidade Nacional de Combate ao Cibercrime e Criminalidade Tecnológica (UNC3T)**, which handles most cybercrime investigations including international cooperation.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Lei 109/2009 — Lei do Cibercrime** [1] criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access (Art. 6)
- System interference (Art. 5)
- Data interference
- Computer-related fraud and forgery

The law integrates with the Portuguese Penal Code.

### Procedural Powers

Law 109/2009 also contains procedural provisions including expedited preservation of data, production orders, and interception of content data. *Likely* compliant with Budapest Convention Chapter II procedural requirements.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority for MLAT requests is the **Procuradoria-Geral da República** (Attorney General's Office). Portugal is a party to EU mutual assistance instruments as well.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratification 2010-03-24) [2]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol]]** — signatory (2022-05-12) [2]

## Key Agencies

- **[[portugal-police|Polícia Judiciária — UNC3T]]** — Unidade Nacional de Combate ao Cibercrime e Criminalidade Tecnológica [3]

## Cooperation Track Record

Portugal cooperates through Europol EC3, Eurojust, and the Budapest Convention 24/7 Network. *Likely* participates in major EU joint operations.

## Capacity Assessment

Portugal's PJ and UNC3T represent consolidated institutional cybercrime capacity. The 2009 Cybercrime Law provides a solid legal framework. EU integration supports strong IC.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Specific operation participation records need source backing
- Second Additional Protocol ratification status pending as of 2026

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Lei n.º 109/2009 — Lei do Cibercrime | Assembleia da República | 2009-09-15 | https://dre.pt/dre/detalhe/lei/109-2009-490315 |
| [2] | Chart of Signatures and Ratifications of Treaty 185 | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |
| [3] | Polícia Judiciária — UNC3T | Ministério da Justiça | — | https://www.pj.pt/ |

title: Romania
text:
## Summary

Romania is a major European cybercrime cooperation partner. It is a **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party** since 2004 (one of the earliest) and hosts the **Council of Europe Cybercrime Programme Office ([[c-proc|C-PROC]])** in Bucharest, which runs global capacity-building programmes.

Romania has a dedicated specialized prosecutor service, **DIICOT** (*Direcția de Investigare a Infracțiunilor de Criminalitate Organizată și Terorism*, [[romania-diicot]]), and a police cybercrime service within the Romanian Police's Directorate for Combating Organized Crime ([[romania-police]]).

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Law 161/2003** (*Legea 161/2003*), **Title III** — on Preventing and Combating Cybercrime — was enacted to align Romanian law with the Budapest Convention prior to accession. The Criminal Code Arts. 360-366 later consolidated cybercrime offences [1][2].

### Procedural Powers

Romanian criminal procedure provides for preservation orders, production orders, search and seizure of computer data, and interception. DIICOT has specialized investigative tools for organized cybercrime.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Ministry of Justice** (*Ministerul Justiției*). Romania operates as both sending and receiving state in substantial MLAT volume.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratified 2004-09-12) [3]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol]]** — signatory (2022-05-12) [3]

## Key Agencies

- **[[romania-diicot|DIICOT]]** — Directorate for Investigating Organized Crime and Terrorism (specialized cybercrime prosecutors)
- **[[romania-police|Poliția Română DCCO]]** — Directorate for Combating Organized Crime, cybercrime service
- **[[c-proc|C-PROC]]** — Council of Europe Cybercrime Programme Office (international org, hosted in Bucharest)

## Cooperation Track Record

Romania is *almost certainly* a high-volume cybercrime IC partner. Prior to 2010s it was a significant source country for financial cybercrime targeting Western Europe and North America; Romanian authorities have cooperated extensively with US and EU counterparts in takedowns.

## Capacity Assessment

High institutional capacity (DIICOT, specialized cybercrime judges), EU integration, C-PROC hosting, and experienced prosecutorial practice place Romania in the "high capacity" tier. English proficiency in prosecutorial ranks reported as medium.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Specific operation participation records and MLAT statistics need source backing
- Constitutional court decisions on data retention have affected IC scenarios

## References

| [1] | Law No. 161/2003 (Title III) | Romanian Government | 2003-04-19 | https://legislatie.just.ro/Public/DetaliiDocument/43323 |

| [3] | Chart of Signatures and Ratifications of Treaty 185 | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |

title: Republic of Rwanda
text:
## Summary

Rwanda is a recent **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party**, having deposited its instrument of accession on **10 January 2025** [1]. Rwanda has been an active partner in Council of Europe capacity-building programmes, particularly the **GLACY+** programme run by [[c-proc|C-PROC]].

Rwanda hosts African Union cybersecurity initiatives and is seen as a model East African cybercrime cooperation partner.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Law No. 60/2018 of 22/08/2018 on Prevention and Punishment of Cybercrimes** [2] criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access and interception
- System and data interference
- Computer-related fraud and forgery
- Content-related offences including online grooming

The 2018 Law was drafted with Budapest Convention compatibility in mind.

### Procedural Powers

Search and seizure of electronic evidence, production orders, and preservation orders available under the 2018 Law and general criminal procedure.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Ministry of Justice**.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (acceded 2025-01-10) [1]
- **[[malabo-convention|African Union Malabo Convention]]** — party

## Key Agencies

- **Rwanda National Police Cybercrime Unit**
- **Rwanda Investigation Bureau (RIB)**

## Cooperation Track Record

Rwanda *likely* has a growing cooperation track record through GLACY+, Interpol, and regional East African partnerships.

## Capacity Assessment

Medium institutional capacity with active participation in capacity-building programmes. High English proficiency. Rwanda's recent Budapest Convention accession is expected to improve MLAT workflows.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Specific operation participation records not yet established
- Integration with 24/7 Network pending or in progress following accession

## References

| [1] | Rwanda acceding to the Budapest Convention | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/-/rwanda-acceding-budapest-convention |

title: Republic of Serbia
text:
## Summary

Serbia is a **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party** since 2009 and is *almost certainly* one of the first states to ratify the **Second Additional Protocol (CETS 224)** [1]. Serbia has institutionalized cybercrime enforcement through a **Special Prosecutor's Office for High-Tech Crime** and a specialized department within the Belgrade Higher Court.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

Serbia's 2005 **Law on Organization and Competence of Government Authorities for Combating High-Tech Crime** [2] established the institutional framework. Cybercrime offences are defined in the Serbian **Criminal Code** (Arts. 298-304a covering unauthorized access, data damage, and related conduct).

### Procedural Powers

Standard procedural tools including search, seizure, preservation, and production orders.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Ministry of Justice**.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratified 2009-04-14) [1]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol (CETS 224)]]** — ratified 2023-11-02 (among the earliest ratifiers) [1]

## Key Agencies

- **Special Prosecutor's Office for High-Tech Crime** (Tužilaštvo za visokotehnološki kriminal)
- **Ministry of Interior Cybercrime Department**

## Cooperation Track Record

Serbia cooperates with neighboring Balkans states, EU Member States, and via Europol and Interpol channels. *Likely* strong regional cooperation role.

## Capacity Assessment

Medium capacity; institutional specialization provides focused cybercrime prosecution capability. EU accession process has driven alignment with EU instruments.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Operation participation records need verification
- Second Additional Protocol ratification status confirmed per T-CY news (Serbia was among early ratifiers with Japan and Hungary)

## References

| [1] | Chart of Signatures and Ratifications of Treaty 185 & 224 | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |

title: Republic of Korea (South Korea)
text:
> [!warning] Status — Major reorganization of prosecution system pending (2026-10-02)
> On **2026-10-02** the [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|대검찰청 (Supreme Prosecutors' Office)]] will be **abolished** after 78 years and replaced by two new bodies: **공소청 (Prosecution Service, under 법무부)** for prosecution only, and **중대범죄수사청 / 중수청 (Serious Crimes Investigation Agency, under 행정안전부)** for investigation of six serious-crime categories including **cybercrime**. This is a fundamental restructuring of Korea's criminal justice architecture and will reshape how foreign partners engage with Korea on cybercrime MLAT, extradition, and investigative cooperation. See [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea]] and [[spo-international-cooperation]] for details. Until 2026-09-30 the existing SPO structure remains in effect.
>
> **Budapest Convention status correction:** The prior version of this page stated Korea "acceded in 2024." This is **incorrect**. Korea was *invited* to accede in **February 2023** but accession was stalled because domestic implementing legislation — specifically a 형사소송법 amendment making preservation requests mandatory — had not passed. The amendment **passed the National Assembly on 2025-12-12** and takes effect **2026-07-01**. The Council of Europe Treaty Office chart status dated **2026-04-25** still lists Korea without an accession or entry-into-force date, so Korea is **not yet a party** to the Budapest Convention [15][16][18].

## Summary

South Korea is a **highly capable** partner for international cooperation on cybercrime, with advanced technological infrastructure, specialized cybercrime enforcement units at both national and metropolitan levels, and comprehensive cybercrime legislation. Korea was **invited to accede** to the [[budapest-convention]] in **February 2023** and has been finalizing its accession since: the domestic implementing legislation (a 형사소송법 amendment introducing mandatory electronic-evidence preservation requests) passed the National Assembly on 2025-12-12 and takes effect 2026-07-01. Formal accession may follow, but the Council of Europe Treaty Office chart still recorded no Korean accession or entry-into-force date in its **2026-04-25** status snapshot [15][16][18].

Korea's cybercrime enforcement architecture is undergoing **two major concurrent reorganizations** that will substantially reshape international cooperation by late 2026:
1. **Prosecution system**: [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|대검찰청 abolished]] 2026-10-02, replaced by 공소청 (prosecution) and 중수청 (investigation of six serious-crime categories including cybercrime)
2. **Police cybercrime system**: [[knpa-cyber-bureau|경찰청 사이버수사국 dissolved]] in December 2023; functions currently exercised at 사이버수사심의관 (deputy-director) level within 수사국; a re-establishment of 사이버안전국 has been proposed for 2027

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**정보통신망 이용촉진 및 정보보호 등에 관한 법률 (Information and Communications Network Act):**
Primary cybercrime statute. Criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access to information and communications networks (Art. 48)
- Distribution of malicious programs (Art. 48-2)
- DDoS attacks / interference with service (Art. 48-3)
- Data breach and personal information violations
- Spam and unsolicited communications

**형법 (Criminal Act):**
- Art. 314: Obstruction of business by interference with computer (컴퓨터등 장애 업무방해)
- Art. 316(2): Violation of secrecy of electronic records
- Art. 347-2: Computer fraud (컴퓨터등 사용사기)

**기타 관련 법률:**
- 전기통신사업법 (Telecommunications Business Act)
- 개인정보 보호법 (Personal Information Protection Act)
- 전자금융거래법 (Electronic Financial Transactions Act)

### Procedural Powers

- **압수·수색 (Search and seizure):** 형사소송법 Art. 106, 107 — includes digital evidence; requires warrant
- **통신제한조치 (Interception):** 통신비밀보호법 — requires court order for real-time interception
- **통신사실확인자료 (Traffic data):** 통신비밀보호법 Art. 13 — court order for subscriber/traffic data
- **보전요청 (Preservation):** Available under 국제형사사

title: Kingdom of Spain
text:
## Summary

Spain is a **key EU cybercrime cooperation partner**, with two specialised national cyber investigation bodies operating in parallel: the **Guardia Civil's Grupo de Delitos Telemáticos (GDT)** — founded 1996 as one of Europe's earliest cybercrime units — and the **Policía Nacional's Brigada Central de Investigación Tecnológica (BIT)**. Spain ratified the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] on **3 June 2010** and modified its Criminal Code and Criminal Procedure Act in 2015 to implement Convention requirements.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**Código Penal (Criminal Code):**
- Art. 197 — unlawful access to personal data / computer systems
- Art. 197 bis — illegal access to information systems (post-2015)
- Art. 197 ter — preparatory acts
- Art. 248 — computer fraud (estafa informática)
- Art. 264 — data/system interference and damage
- Art. 264 bis — system interference
- Art. 264 ter — preparatory tools

2015 reform (LO 1/2015) brought the Criminal Code into line with the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] and EU Directive 2013/40/EU on attacks against information systems.

### Procedural Powers

**Ley de Enjuiciamiento Criminal (LECrim)** provides:
- Search and seizure of electronic evidence
- Remote search of computer systems (registro remoto)
- Communications interception (intervención de las comunicaciones)
- Data preservation and production orders

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2010-06-03 | |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022-05-12 | |
| EU EIO | Participating | 2017 | |
| UNTOC | Party | | |
| UNCAC | Party | | |

## Key Agencies

### [[spain-guardia-civil|Guardia Civil — Grupo de Delitos Telemáticos (GDT)]]
- Founded 1996 as Grupo de Delitos Informáticos
- Renamed Departamento de Delitos de Alta Tecnología, then Grupo de Delitos Telemáticos
- Investigation, forensics, R&D, intelligence

### [[spain-national-police|Cuerpo Nacional de Policía — Unidad Central de Ciberdelincuencia (UCC / BIT)]]
- Brigada Central de Investigación Tecnológica
- Parallel national cyber investigative jurisdiction

### Central Authority
- **Subdirección General de Cooperación Jurídica Internacional**, Ministerio de Justicia — MLA central authority under Budapest arts. 24 and 27
- Spain maintains **two 24/7 contact points** — one for each national force

## Cooperation Track Record

Spain is *almost certainly* a major participant in EU-coordinated cybercrime operations via Europol EC3 and Eurojust. *Likely* frequently involved in operations against Spanish-speaking cybercrime networks operating across Latin America.

## Capacity Assessment

- **Rating:** high
- **24/7 availability:** yes (two contact points)
- **Digital forensics:** high
- **English proficiency:** medium

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What is the current division of labour between Guardia Civil GDT and CNP BIT on cybercrime cases?
- Has Spain ratified the Second Additional Protocol as of 2026?

## References

|---|--------|-----------|------|-----|
| 1 | Spain — Octopus Cybercrime Community | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/-/spain |
| 2 | Cybercrime policies/strategies Spain | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/country-wiki-ap/-/asset_publisher/CmDb7M4RGb4Z/content/spain/pop_up |
| 3 | Cybercrime Central Unit of the Guardia Civil | IT Law Wiki | — | https://itlaw.fandom.com/wiki/Cybercrime_Central_Unit_of_the_Guardia_Civil |
| 4 | Treaty 185 signatures chart | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |

title: Swiss Confederation
text:
## Summary

Switzerland is a **high-capability cybercrime cooperation partner**, particularly important for **financial and cryptocurrency investigations** given its role as a global financial hub and the presence of the "Crypto Valley" ecosystem in Zug. Switzerland ratified the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] on **21 September 2011**.

Swiss cybercrime law is embedded within the existing **Swiss Criminal Code (StGB/Code pénal)** and **Swiss Criminal Procedure Code (StPO/CPP)**; no standalone cybercrime statute exists, as the existing codes were deemed sufficient with minor amendments.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**Swiss Criminal Code (StGB):**
- Art. 143 — unauthorised obtaining of data (unbefugte Datenbeschaffung)
- Art. 143bis — unauthorised access to a data processing system (unbefugtes Eindringen in ein Datenverarbeitungssystem)
  - Art. 143bis para. 2 added to implement the Budapest Convention — criminalising making available passwords, programs, or data intended for committing offences
- Art. 144bis — damage to data (Datenbeschädigung)
- Art. 147 — computer fraud (betrügerischer Missbrauch einer Datenverarbeitungsanlage)
- Art. 150 — obtaining services fraudulently

### Procedural Powers

**Swiss Criminal Procedure Code (StPO)** provides search, seizure, preservation, production orders, and telecommunications surveillance.

**BÜPF** (Bundesgesetz betreffend die Überwachung des Post- und Fernmeldeverkehrs) — Federal Act on the Surveillance of Post and Telecommunications governs interception and data retention obligations on providers.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

- **Federal Act on International Mutual Assistance in Criminal Matters (IMAC/IRSG)** — primary MLA statute
- **Central authority:** Federal Office of Justice (Bundesamt für Justiz, BJ)
- Swiss banking secrecy is *no longer* a blanket bar to MLA in criminal matters

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2011-09-21 | |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022-05-12 | |
| UNTOC | Party | | |
| UNCAC | Party | | |

## Key Agencies

### [[switzerland-fedpol|fedpol — Federal Office of Police]]
- Responsible for combating cybercrime, detecting online offences, and coordinating cross-border investigations
- Hosts Switzerland's 24/7 contact point
- NEDIK (Netzwerk Ermittlungsunterstützung Digitale Kriminalität) — Digital Crime Investigation Support Network coordinates cantonal and federal efforts

### [[switzerland-ncsc|National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC)]]
- National CERT and cybersecurity coordination

### Cantonal Police Forces
- Switzerland's federal structure means operational cybercrime investigation is often conducted at cantonal level, with fedpol providing coordination

## Cooperation Track Record

Switzerland is *almost certainly* a frequently-requested MLA partner, particularly for:
- Seizure of assets and cryptocurrency
- Records from Swiss financial institutions
- Servers hosted in Swiss data centres

## Capacity Assessment

- **Rating:** high
- **Digital forensics:** high
- **24/7 availability:** yes
- **English proficiency:** high (among federal officials)

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What is the average MLA response time for Swiss requests under IMAC?
- How does NEDIK coordinate with individual cantonal cybercrime units?

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|------|-----|
| 1 | Switzerland — Octopus Cybercrime Community | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/-/switzerland |
| 2 | Cybercrime policies/strategies Switzerland | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/country-wiki-ap/-/asset_publisher/CmDb7M4RGb4Z/content/switzerland/pop_up |
| 3 | Treaty 185 signatures chart | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |

title: United Republic of Tanzania
text:
## Summary

Tanzania is a **non-party** to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]. Its primary cybercrime legislation is the **Cybercrimes Act, 2015** [1], which has been controversial for including content-related offences that civil society organizations have characterized as restricting free expression.

Tanzania cooperates internationally through **Interpol** and **Commonwealth** channels. Capacity for cross-border cybercrime cooperation is *likely* low due to limited specialized institutional infrastructure.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Cybercrimes Act No. 14 of 2015** [1] criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access and interception
- System and data interference
- Computer fraud and forgery
- Publication of false information (speech-related offences drawing civil society concern)
- Racist and xenophobic content

### Procedural Powers

The Act grants police powers to search and seize, compel production of data, and access computer systems. Real-time interception requires judicial authorization.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority for mutual legal assistance is the **Ministry of Constitutional and Legal Affairs**. Tanzania has MLA arrangements through the Commonwealth Harare Scheme and bilateral agreements.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — non-party [2]
- **[[malabo-convention|African Union Malabo Convention]]** — non-party (as of last check)

## Key Agencies

- **Tanzania Police Force Cyber Crime Unit**
- **Tanzania Communications Regulatory Authority (TCRA)**

## Cooperation Track Record

Limited public record of major joint cybercrime operations. Tanzania participates in Interpol Africa initiatives including [[africa-cyber-surge-ii]] (as a participating country).

## Capacity Assessment

Low institutional cybercrime capacity. English proficiency is high (English is an official language). Limited specialized digital forensics infrastructure.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Malabo Convention status requires verification
- Extent of participation in Interpol Africa cybercrime operations

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Cybercrimes Act No. 14 of 2015 | Parliament of Tanzania | 2015-04-25 | https://www.parliament.go.tz/ |
| [2] | Parties/Observers to the Budapest Convention | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/parties-observers |

title: Kingdom of Thailand
text:
## Summary

Thailand is a **non-party** to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] but has substantive cybercrime legislation under the **Computer Crime Act B.E. 2550 (2007)**, as amended in 2017 (B.E. 2560). The Act has been widely criticized by civil society organizations for provisions that criminalize content considered harmful to national security or public order, which are distinct from conventional cybercrime offences.

The **Cyber Crime Investigation Bureau (CCIB)** ([[thailand-royal-police]]) of the Royal Thai Police handles most cybercrime investigations.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Computer Crime Act B.E. 2550 (2007)** [1] criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access to computer systems
- Interception
- Data and system interference
- Distribution of obscene/false content (controversial provisions)

**B.E. 2560 (2017) amendments** [2] expanded surveillance authorities and added additional offences. Civil society organizations have expressed concerns about speech restrictions.

### Procedural Powers

Authorities have powers to access computer data, seize equipment, and compel disclosure. Speed of cross-border cooperation varies.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Office of the Attorney General** (*สำนักงานอัยการสูงสุด*). Thailand is a party to the ASEAN Treaty on Mutual Legal Assistance in Criminal Matters.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — non-party [3]
- **ASEAN MLAT** — party
- **UN Convention against Cybercrime (2024)** — status to be verified

## Key Agencies

- **[[thailand-royal-police|Cyber Crime Investigation Bureau (CCIB)]]** — Royal Thai Police cyber unit
- **Ministry of Digital Economy and Society (MDES)** — policy and infrastructure

## Cooperation Track Record

Thailand cooperates via Interpol, ASEAN, and bilateral channels. Notable cooperation in takedown of domestic call-center scam operations and pig-butchering fraud networks based in Thailand and bordering Special Economic Zones.

## Capacity Assessment

Medium institutional capacity. Thailand has established dedicated cybercrime units. MLAT response times are variable. English proficiency in specialized units is adequate for international engagement.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Thailand is not currently a Budapest Convention party — whether accession is under consideration unclear
- Human rights concerns about Computer Crime Act provisions affect how some Western states handle MLAT requests from Thailand

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Computer Crime Act B.E. 2550 (2007) — English translation | Thailand Law Online | 2007 | https://www.samuiforsale.com/law-texts/computer-crime-act.html |
| [2] | 2017 amendments to Computer Crime Act (English summary) | Baker McKenzie | 2017 | https://www.bakermckenzie.com/en/ |
| [3] | Parties/Observers to the Budapest Convention | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/parties-observers |

title: Togolese Republic
text:
## Summary

Togo is a **non-party** to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] but is an early party to the **[[malabo-convention|African Union Malabo Convention]]** on Cyber Security and Personal Data Protection (ratified 18 November 2016). Togo has enacted comprehensive cybersecurity legislation and was announced in 2019 as the host of the **African Union Cybersecurity Centre** in Lomé.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Law 2018-026 on Cybersecurity and Combating Cybercrime** [1] defines cybercrime offences including unauthorized access, data interference, and system interference. Additional provisions cover electronic fraud and child protection.

### Procedural Powers

Togolese criminal procedure provides for search and seizure of electronic evidence and production orders, with judicial authorization for interception.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Ministry of Justice**. Togo participates in ECOWAS regional cooperation and bilateral MLATs.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — non-party [2]
- **[[malabo-convention|African Union Malabo Convention]]** — party (2016-11-18) [3]
- **ECOWAS Directive on Cybercrime** — participating member state

## Key Agencies

- **Agence Nationale de la Cybersécurité (ANCy)** — National Cybersecurity Agency
- **Togolese Police Cybercrime Unit**

## Cooperation Track Record

Limited public record. Togo has participated in Interpol Africa operations including [[africa-cyber-surge-ii]].

## Capacity Assessment

Low institutional capacity but improving through regional initiatives. The hosting of the AU Cybersecurity Centre is expected to drive capacity development.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Implementation status of AU Cybersecurity Centre at Lomé
- Togo's current engagement with GLACY+ and C-PROC capacity building

## References

title: Ukraine
text:
## Summary

Ukraine is a **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party** since 2005 and has been one of the most operationally active eastern European cybercrime cooperation partners. The **Cyberpolice of Ukraine** ([[ukraine-police]]) — Department of Cyber Police within the National Police of Ukraine — leads cybercrime investigations.

Despite the ongoing Russian invasion (since February 2022), Ukraine has continued to participate in multinational cybercrime operations including those targeting ransomware infrastructure. Ukrainian law enforcement has made notable arrests of suspected ransomware affiliates.

> [!caution] Operational sensitivity
> Details of active cooperation during wartime may involve operational security considerations. Information in this profile is drawn from public sources only.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

Ukraine's **Criminal Code** Arts. 361-363-1 criminalize unauthorized access, data interference, and distribution of malicious software [1]. Additional provisions address computer fraud and forgery.

The **Law on Cybersecurity of Ukraine** (2017) [2] established a national cybersecurity framework including responsibilities for State Service of Special Communications and Information Protection (SSSCIP).

### Procedural Powers

Ukrainian criminal procedure provides for search and seizure of electronic evidence, data preservation, production orders, and interception.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Office of the Prosecutor General** (*Офіс Генерального прокурора*).

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratified 2005-09-10) [3]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol]]** — signatory (2022-05-12) [3]

## Key Agencies

- **[[ukraine-police|Cyberpolice of Ukraine]]** — Department of Cyber Police, National Police of Ukraine
- **Security Service of Ukraine (SBU)** — counter-intelligence and national security cyber matters

## Cooperation Track Record

Ukraine has participated in multiple major international cybercrime operations including actions against REvil/Sodinokibi and other ransomware groups. It is *highly likely* to have strong bilateral cooperation with US and EU partners.

## Capacity Assessment

Despite wartime conditions, Ukraine maintains substantial cybercrime investigative capacity. English proficiency in specialized cyber units is high. Digital forensics capacity is well-developed.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Operational impact of the war on specific MLAT timelines unclear
- Extent of cooperation with Russia (formally hostile state) ceased after 2014 / 2022
- Cross-border cooperation logistics may involve alternative channels during wartime

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Criminal Code of Ukraine — Section XVI Cyber Offences | Verkhovna Rada | 2001 | https://zakon.rada.gov.ua/laws/show/2341-14 |
| [2] | Law on Basic Principles of Cybersecurity of Ukraine (2017) | Verkhovna Rada | 2017-10-05 | https://zakon.rada.gov.ua/laws/show/2163-19 |
| [3] | Chart of Signatures and Ratifications of Treaty 185 | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |

title: United Kingdom
text:
## Summary

The United Kingdom is one of the **most active** countries in international cybercrime cooperation. The **National Crime Agency (NCA)**, through its National Cyber Crime Unit (NCCU), led [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos]] against the LockBit ransomware group — *almost certainly* the most high-profile ransomware takedown to date. The UK is a **Five Eyes** member, a party to the [[budapest-convention]], and holds the world's first **CLOUD Act executive agreement** with the United States (2019).

Post-Brexit, the UK has had to navigate new cooperation arrangements with the EU, but operational cooperation on cybercrime has *likely* remained strong through the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement and bilateral channels.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**Computer Misuse Act 1990 (CMA):**
- Unauthorized access to computer material (§ 1)
- Unauthorized access with intent to commit further offences (§ 2)
- Unauthorized acts with intent to impair operation of computer (§ 3)
- Making, supplying or obtaining articles for use in offences (§ 3A, added 2006)
- Unauthorized acts causing serious damage (§ 3ZA, added 2015)

**Fraud Act 2006:**
- Broadly covers cyber-enabled fraud (phishing, BEC, online scams)

**Serious Crime Act 2015:**
- Participation in organized crime groups, including cybercrime syndicates

**Investigatory Powers Act 2016 (IPA):**
- Comprehensive surveillance framework; interception warrants, data retention notices, equipment interference
- International cooperation provisions for responding to foreign requests

### Procedural Powers

- **Search and seizure:** Police and Criminal Evidence Act 1984 (PACE); warrants from magistrates
- **Production orders:** PACE Schedule 1; Terrorism Act 2000
- **Data retention:** IPA 2016; retention notices to providers for up to 12 months
- **Interception:** IPA 2016; requires warrant from Secretary of State
- **Equipment interference:** IPA 2016; remote access to devices (hacking) for law enforcement

### Mutual Legal Assistance

- **Central authority:** UK Home Office (incoming requests); Attorney General's Office (outgoing requests)
- **Crime (International Co-operation) Act 2003:** Domestic legislation implementing MLA obligations
- **CLOUD Act Agreement with US (2019):** First bilateral executive agreement; allows direct data requests to US providers
- **Post-Brexit EU cooperation:** UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement (2021) includes MLA provisions; no longer party to European Arrest Warrant (EAW), uses surrender arrangements instead

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> Verify the current status of UK-EU operational cooperation agreements post-Brexit, including Europol working arrangement details.

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2011 | |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022 | |
| UNTOC (Palermo Convention) | Party | 2006 | |
| UNCAC | Party | 2006 | |
| US-UK MLAT | In force | 1996 | |
| US-UK CLOUD Act Agreement | In force | 2019 | First CLOUD Act executive agreement globally |

The UK participates in the Budapest Convention **[[24-7-network]]** and the **G7 24/7 Cyber Network**.

## Key Agencies

### National Crime Agency (NCA) — National Cyber Crime Unit (NCCU)
See [[uk-nca]] for full profile.
- Lead UK agency for serious and organised cybercrime
- Led [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos]] against LockBit
- Participates in Europol JITs and INTERPOL operations
- Houses the UK's international liaison network for cybercrime

### Metropolitan Police Service — Cyber Crime Unit
- London-based cyber investigations
- Supports NCA on major operations

### GCHQ / National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC)
- Signals intelligence and cyber security
- Provides technical capabilities and threat intelligence
- NCSC is the public-facing cyber security authority

## Cooperation Track Record

### As Requesting State

title: United States of America
text:
## Summary

The United States holds the **highest centrality** in the international cybercrime cooperation network. As home to the world's largest technology companies (Google, Meta, Microsoft, Apple, etc.), the US is *almost certainly* the most frequently requested country for cross-border digital evidence. The [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Cyber Division]] and [[us-doj|Department of Justice]] lead or participate in the majority of major international cybercrime operations, and US federal prosecutors have brought more international cybercrime cases than any other jurisdiction.

The US legal framework combines the **Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA)** as the primary cybercrime statute, the **CLOUD Act** (2018) as a mechanism for streamlining cross-border data access, and an extensive network of 30+ bilateral **Mutual Legal Assistance Treaties (MLATs)**.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA), 18 U.S.C. § 1030 (1986, amended):**
- Unauthorized access to protected computers (§ 1030(a)(2))
- Damage to protected computers (§ 1030(a)(5))
- Trafficking in passwords (§ 1030(a)(6))
- Extortion involving computers (§ 1030(a)(7)) — covers ransomware

**Wire Fraud, 18 U.S.C. § 1343:**
- Broadly applied to cyber-enabled financial fraud (BEC, phishing, etc.)

**Identity Theft, 18 U.S.C. § 1028/1028A:**
- Aggravated identity theft adds mandatory 2-year consecutive sentence

**Economic Espionage Act, 18 U.S.C. § 1831-1839:**
- State-sponsored cyber espionage prosecutions

**CLOUD Act (Clarifying Lawful Overseas Use of Data Act), 2018:**
- Allows US law enforcement to compel US-based providers to produce data stored abroad
- Enables bilateral executive agreements for reciprocal direct access
- *Highly likely* the most significant legislative development for cross-border evidence access since the Budapest Convention

### Procedural Powers

- **Search and seizure:** Fourth Amendment protections; warrants required for content data (Carpenter v. United States, 2018)
- **Stored Communications Act (SCA), 18 U.S.C. § 2701-2712:** Framework for compelling subscriber data, traffic data, and content from providers
- **Pen register / trap-and-trace (18 U.S.C. § 3121-3127):** Real-time collection of metadata
- **Wiretap Act (Title III), 18 U.S.C. § 2510-2522:** Real-time content interception; requires court order
- **Preservation orders (18 U.S.C. § 2703(f)):** 90-day preservation requests to providers; commonly used in IC context

### Mutual Legal Assistance

- **Central authority:** [[us-doj|Department of Justice]], Office of International Affairs (OIA)
- **30+ bilateral MLATs** in force
- **Budapest Convention** Chapter IV provides additional multilateral MLA basis
- **CLOUD Act executive agreements:** Bilateral agreements enabling direct law enforcement-to-provider requests (first agreement: US-UK, 2019)
- **Average processing time:** *Likely* 180+ days for standard MLAT requests; significantly faster for urgent requests and CLOUD Act pathways

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> Verify the current number of CLOUD Act executive agreements in force and the complete list of US bilateral MLATs relevant to cybercrime.

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2006 | Among the first ratifiers |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022 | Pending ratification |
| UNTOC (Palermo Convention) | Party | 2005 | |
| UNCAC | Party | 2006 | |
| US-Korea MLAT | In force | 2000 | |
| US-UK MLAT | In force | 1996 | |
| US-Germany MLAT | In force | 2010 | |
| US-Netherlands MLAT | In force | 1983 | |
| US-Australia MLAT | In force | 1999 | |
| US-Japan MLAT | In force | 2006 | |

The US participates in the **G7 24/7 Cyber Network** and the Budapest Convention **24/7 Network** ([[24-7-network]]).

## Key Agencies

### Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) — Cyber Division
See [[fbi-cyber-division]] fo

title: Republic of Zambia
text:
## Summary

Zambia is a **non-party** to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]. Its primary cybercrime legislation is the **Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act No. 2 of 2021** [1], enacted during a politically contentious period. The Act has been criticized by civil society for provisions deemed to restrict online speech, though it does include standard cybercrime offences.

Zambia cooperates through Interpol, SADC (Southern African Development Community), and Commonwealth channels.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act No. 2 of 2021** [1]:
- Unauthorized access and interception
- System and data interference
- Computer fraud and forgery
- Content-related offences

### Procedural Powers

The Act authorizes search and seizure of computer data, production orders, interception (with judicial or ministerial authorization), and preservation requests.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Ministry of Justice**. Zambia's MLA arrangements use the **Mutual Legal Assistance in Criminal Matters Act** framework and the **Commonwealth Harare Scheme**.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — non-party [2]
- **African Union Malabo Convention** — status unclear

## Key Agencies

- **Zambia Police Service Cybercrime Unit**
- **Zambia Information and Communications Technology Authority (ZICTA)**

## Cooperation Track Record

Zambia has participated in Interpol Africa-focused cybercrime operations including [[africa-cyber-surge-ii]].

## Capacity Assessment

Low institutional capacity. High English proficiency (English is the official language). Digital forensics infrastructure limited.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Malabo Convention ratification status requires verification
- Extent of participation in GLACY+ or other capacity building programmes

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act No. 2 of 2021 | Parliament of Zambia | 2021-03-23 | https://www.parliament.gov.zm/node/8756 |
| [2] | Parties/Observers to the Budapest Convention | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/parties-observers |

title: Banking Trojan Operations
text:
## Summary

Banking-trojan cooperation cases focus on malware designed to steal online-banking credentials, manipulate sessions, or support fraudulent transfers. These cases frequently combine cyber intrusion evidence with financial-crime evidence. The malware operator, botnet administrator, money mule recruiter, cash-out layer, victim bank, and hosting provider may all sit in different countries.

## Cooperation Pattern

GozNym, Bugat/Dridex, Zeus, and SpyEye records show why banking-trojan cases often require several cooperation tracks at once. Technical disruption can neutralize botnet infrastructure, but prosecution usually also needs victim-bank records, account-opening evidence, payment traces, and evidence tying defendants to malware administration or mule management. Public-private support matters because banks and security companies often detect command-and-control infrastructure and money-mule patterns before a formal action day.

## Menu Use

Use this page where the malware's primary purpose is online-banking theft or unauthorized funds transfer. Use [[malware-ic]] for broader malware families and [[money-mule-networks]] when the source mainly documents recruitment, account control, or laundering after the initial credential theft. The distinction prevents the same operation from being reduced to either a purely technical botnet case or a purely financial-fraud case.

## Analytical Use

Within this wiki, **Banking Trojan Operations** is a control node for comparing records that describe the offense pattern, evidence sources, and cross-border investigative pressure points. Use the page to separate three questions that often become mixed in operation narratives: what conduct or authority is being discussed, which agency or state actor exercised it, and where the cross-border dependency appears in the evidence chain. That separation makes the menu more useful for comparing takedowns, prosecutions, extradition matters, and assistance requests without treating every related record as the same kind of cooperation event.

## Integrity Notes

The page groups terminology; it is not independent proof that the concept, crime type, mechanism, or framework applied in every linked matter. Operation and case pages remain the controlling records for arrests, seizures, requests, treaty use, and participating agencies. When adding future links, keep the distinction between direct source evidence, inferred analytical classification, and catalog navigation visible in the target page.

title: Business Email Compromise (BEC) — International Cooperation Perspective
text:
## Summary

Business Email Compromise (BEC) is a cyber-enabled financial fraud scheme in which criminals impersonate business executives, vendors, or trusted entities via email to trick victims into transferring funds to accounts under the criminals' control. BEC is *almost certainly* one of the highest-loss cybercrime types globally, with the FBI reporting USD 2.9 billion in US losses alone in 2023. From an international cooperation perspective, BEC presents unique challenges due to the rapid cross-border movement of stolen funds and the involvement of transnational criminal organizations, particularly West African groups such as Black Axe.

## Criminalization Landscape

BEC is broadly criminalized worldwide under general fraud, wire fraud, and cybercrime statutes. The crime itself is often prosecuted under:
- **Fraud/wire fraud statutes** (e.g., US 18 USC 1343)
- **Computer fraud provisions** (unauthorized access to email systems)
- **Money laundering statutes** (for the movement of stolen funds)
- **Organized crime provisions** (when linked to criminal syndicates)

## Typical IC Scenarios

### Scenario 1: CEO Fraud / Executive Impersonation
**Pattern:** Criminals compromise or spoof executive email accounts, instructing financial officers to make urgent transfers to accounts in foreign jurisdictions.
**Example:** [[franco-israeli-ceo-fraud|Franco-Israeli CEO Fraud]] — EUR 38 million stolen from single company, laundered through EU/China/Israel accounts

### Scenario 2: West African Organized Crime BEC Networks
**Pattern:** Large-scale BEC operations by organized crime groups (Black Axe) spanning dozens of countries, with proceeds laundered through complex international banking networks.
**Example:** [[operation-jackal|Operation Jackal]] (103 arrests, 21 countries) and [[operation-jackal-iii|Jackal III]] (~300 arrests, 21 countries)

### Scenario 3: INTERPOL Multi-Crime Operations Targeting BEC
**Pattern:** BEC addressed as one of multiple financial fraud types within large-scale INTERPOL operations.
**Example:** [[operation-haechi-iv|HAECHI IV]] (3,500 arrests, BEC as one of 7 crime types), [[operation-haechi-vi|HAECHI VI]] (USD 439M recovered)

### Scenario 4: Rapid Fund Interception (I-GRIP)
**Pattern:** INTERPOL's I-GRIP mechanism used for rapid cross-border BEC fund recovery.
**Example:** [[operation-haechi-vi|HAECHI VI]] Korea-UAE sub-operation recovered USD 3.91M via I-GRIP; [[operation-first-light-2024|First Light 2024]] Spain intercepted USD 331K BEC fraud

## Key Operations and Precedents

### BEC-Focused Operations

| Operation | Date | Coordinator | Countries | Arrests | Seized | Target |
|-----------|------|-------------|-----------|---------|--------|--------|
| [[franco-israeli-ceo-fraud]] | 2022-2023 | Europol | 7 | 8 | EUR 5.5M | Franco-Israeli CEO fraud network |
| [[operation-jackal]] | May 2023 | INTERPOL | 21 | 103 | EUR 2.15M | Black Axe (BEC/financial fraud) |
| [[operation-jackal-iii]] | Apr-Jul 2024 | INTERPOL | 21 | ~300 | USD 3M | Black Axe (BEC/financial fraud) |
| [[operation-sentinel-africa]] | Oct-Nov 2025 | INTERPOL | 19 | 574 | USD 3M | BEC, ransomware across Africa |

### BEC as Component of Broader Operations

| Operation | Date | BEC Role | Total Arrests | Total Seized |
|-----------|------|----------|---------------|-------------|
| [[operation-haechi-iv]] | Jul-Dec 2023 | 1 of 7 crime types; 75% of cases (with investment/e-commerce fraud) | 3,500 | $300M |
| [[operation-haechi-v]] | Jul-Nov 2024 | 1 of 7 crime types | 5,500+ | $400M+ |
| [[operation-first-light-2024]] | Mar-May 2024 | I-GRIP BEC interceptions | 3,950 | $257M |
| [[operation-haechi-vi]] | Apr-Aug 2025 | Korea-UAE BEC recovery; Thailand BEC | - | $439M |

## Black Axe and BEC

The **Black Axe** criminal network and similar West African organized crime groups are identified as responsible for the majority of the world's cyber-enabled financial fraud, with BEC as a primary crime type:

- Estimated **30,000 members** in dozen

title: CSAM (Child Sexual Abuse Material) — International Cooperation Perspective
text:
## Summary

CSAM international-cooperation cases involve online distribution, production, possession, streaming, or platform facilitation of child sexual abuse material where offenders, victims, platforms, payment systems, or hosting infrastructure cross borders.

## Cooperation Use

Operation Orion International, Operation Stream/Kidflix, and Operation Cyber Guardian show three recurring patterns: victim identification across countries, platform or server takedown, and coordinated arrests based on shared intelligence. These cases often require rapid provider data, device forensics, cryptocurrency or payment tracing, child-protection coordination, and careful handling of victim-identifying material.

## Boundary Notes

Use this page where CSAM is the principal crime type. Use [[dark-web-ic]] when the record is mainly about darknet marketplace infrastructure, [[electronic-evidence]] for provider/device evidence, and [[interpol-icse-database]] where the source specifically concerns victim-identification data sharing.

title: Cybercrime Forum Operations
text:
## Summary

Cybercrime forum cases target online communities that broker malware, stolen credentials, payment-card data, hacking services, tutorials, or access to compromised systems. The cooperation challenge is different from a single-victim intrusion case. Investigators must attribute screen names to people, preserve private-message and transaction records, coordinate arrests before administrators warn members, and handle evidence from servers, payment systems, and undercover accounts.

## Cooperation Pattern

Darkode, Infraud, and Operation Talent show three useful patterns. Darkode was an invitation-only forum case with international undercover and takedown work. Infraud was treated as a transnational criminal organization with defendants and infrastructure distributed across countries. Operation Talent reflects the newer model of marketplace and forum disruption where domains, servers, and user databases become evidence for later actions. In each pattern, timing matters because a forum can migrate quickly once administrators detect law-enforcement interest.

## Menu Use

Use this page when the source focuses on a forum, marketplace community, or criminal service board. Use [[dark-web-ic]] where the main fact is Tor or darknet marketplace access, and use [[cybercrime-infrastructure-ic]] where the primary target is hosting, proxying, VPN, botnet, or malware delivery infrastructure rather than the social marketplace layer.

## Analytical Use

Use the page to separate three questions that often become mixed in operation narratives: what conduct or authority is being discussed, which agency or state actor exercised it, and where the cross-border dependency appears in the evidence chain. title: Cybercrime Infrastructure Disruption
text:
## Summary

Cybercrime infrastructure disruption covers actions against the services that make other crimes scalable: botnet loaders, proxy networks, bulletproof VPNs, credential shops, hosting clusters, malware-delivery domains, and command-and-control systems. The target is often not a single criminal group. It is an enabling service used by many customers, affiliates, or downstream actors.

## Cooperation Pattern

Operation Endgame, VPNLab, DoubleVPN, and the 911 S5 botnet action show the common sequence. Investigators map the infrastructure, identify legal control points, prepare seizure or disruption orders in several jurisdictions, and coordinate an action day that prevents administrators from moving domains, servers, or customer data. Evidence value is high because seized customer records can generate follow-on cases against ransomware groups, fraud actors, malware operators, or access brokers.

## Menu Use

Use this page when the source is mainly about infrastructure-as-a-service for cybercrime. If the source is about a specific malware family, use [[malware-ic]]. If it is about DDoS-for-hire, use [[ddos-ic]]. If it is about a forum where criminals trade services, use [[cybercrime-forum-ic]]. The infrastructure page should remain the cross-cutting menu node for services that support multiple criminal markets.

## Analytical Use

Within this wiki, **Cybercrime Infrastructure Disruption** is a control node for comparing records that describe the offense pattern, evidence sources, and cross-border investigative pressure points. Use the page to separate three questions that often become mixed in operation narratives: what conduct or authority is being discussed, which agency or state actor exercised it, and where the cross-border dependency appears in the evidence chain. title: Cyberstalking / Online Coercion
text:
## Summary

Cyberstalking covers repeated online or electronic communications, threats, harassment, coercion, impersonation, and related misuse of digital platforms against specific victims. In the international-cooperation corpus it is usually not a large multinational takedown category, but it still matters for evidence and classification because offenders often use platform accounts, cloud services, messaging providers, anonymization tools, or out-of-district infrastructure.

## Cooperation Pattern

The linked cyberstalking sources show a victim-centered investigative pattern. Investigators need to preserve account data, attribute accounts to a person, connect online conduct to real-world threats or coercion, and protect victims while a case is pending. Cross-border cooperation becomes relevant when the service provider, suspect, victim, or data store is outside the investigating district or country. In those settings, the legal route may be a domestic warrant to a provider, a direct provider request, an emergency disclosure request, or formal mutual assistance.

## Menu Use

Use this category for stalking, coercive harassment, sextortion-like harassment, and threat campaigns directed at identifiable victims. Use [[extortion-ic]] when the dominant feature is a payment demand, [[online-fraud-ic]] when deception for money is central, and [[csam-ic]] when production, distribution, or possession of abuse material is the core offense.

## Analytical Use

Within this wiki, **Cyberstalking / Online Coercion** is a control node for comparing records that describe the offense pattern, evidence sources, and cross-border investigative pressure points. Use the page to separate three questions that often become mixed in operation narratives: what conduct or authority is being discussed, which agency or state actor exercised it, and where the cross-border dependency appears in the evidence chain. That separation makes the menu more useful for comparing takedowns, prosecutions, extradition matters, and assistance requests without treating every related record as the same kind of cooperation event.

## Integrity Notes

The page groups terminology; it is not independent proof that the concept, crime type, mechanism, or framework applied in every linked matter. Operation and case pages remain the controlling records for arrests, seizures, requests, treaty use, and participating agencies. When adding future links, keep the distinction between direct source evidence, inferred analytical classification, and catalog navigation visible in the target page.

title: DDoS Operations in International Cooperation
text:
## Summary

DDoS international-cooperation cases usually involve services, infrastructure, customers, and victims spread across multiple jurisdictions. The operational target is often a booter or stresser platform rather than a single attack event. This means investigators need to preserve provider data, seize domains or servers, identify paying customers, and notify victims quickly enough to keep the service from reconstituting.

## Cooperation Pattern

The 2016 international DDoS-for-hire sweep shows the older pattern: arrests and interviews of users, disruption of service providers, and public messaging aimed at deterrence. Later Operation PowerOFF sources show a more industrialized model in which law enforcement repeatedly seizes booter domains, replaces them with seizure banners, and uses customer records for follow-on prevention or prosecution. In both patterns, attribution is only one part of the case. Payment trails, account records, domain registration data, hosting logs, and victim impact are usually needed to make the enforcement action usable in court.

## Menu Use

Use this crime-type page when the source record is about DDoS extortion, booter services, stresser marketplaces, or DDoS-for-hire infrastructure. Use [[cybercrime-infrastructure-ic]] for broader infrastructure takedowns where DDoS is only one service among many, and use [[extortion-ic]] when the dominant conduct is ransom demand or coercion rather than traffic flooding.

## Analytical Use

Within this wiki, **DDoS Operations in International Cooperation** is a control node for comparing records that describe the offense pattern, evidence sources, and cross-border investigative pressure points. Use the page to separate three questions that often become mixed in operation narratives: what conduct or authority is being discussed, which agency or state actor exercised it, and where the cross-border dependency appears in the evidence chain. title: Hacking / Computer Intrusion — International Cooperation Perspective
text:
## Summary

Hacking and computer intrusion cover unauthorized access to computers, networks, accounts, devices, and data. In international-cooperation analysis this category includes financially motivated intrusion, contractor or state-directed intrusion, credential theft, vulnerability exploitation, and infrastructure abuse used to hide the actor's location.

## Cooperation Pattern

The cooperation problem depends on the actor model. Criminal intrusion cases may progress through provider records, seized servers, exchange records, extradition, and coordinated searches. State-directed or state-sheltered cases are harder: public charges may identify actors and infrastructure, but custody and evidence collection can be blocked by the suspect's location or by the sponsoring state's non-cooperation. Proxy-service takedowns add a second pattern, where the immediate target is infrastructure used by many intruders rather than one intrusion crew.

## Menu Use

Use this category when unauthorized access is the core conduct. Use [[malware-ic]] when the record focuses on malware deployment or botnet infrastructure, [[cybercrime-infrastructure-ic]] when the main target is a criminal enabling service, and [[data-sovereignty]] or [[jurisdictional-conflicts]] where the central issue is legal conflict over data access rather than the intrusion itself.

## Analytical Use

Within this wiki, **Hacking / Computer Intrusion — International Cooperation Perspective** is a control node for comparing records that describe the offense pattern, evidence sources, and cross-border investigative pressure points. Use the page to separate three questions that often become mixed in operation narratives: what conduct or authority is being discussed, which agency or state actor exercised it, and where the cross-border dependency appears in the evidence chain. That separation makes the menu more useful for comparing takedowns, prosecutions, extradition matters, and assistance requests without treating every related record as the same kind of cooperation event.

title: Illegal IPTV and Streaming Piracy Operations in International Cooperation
text:
## Summary

Illegal IPTV and streaming piracy operations cover enforcement actions against services that capture, redistribute, resell, or broker access to protected live and on-demand audiovisual content without authorization. The conduct is cyber-enabled because the offending infrastructure usually depends on servers, control panels, reseller portals, credentials, payment channels, and cross-border hosting arrangements.

## Cooperation Pattern

The expanded IPTV set shows several recurring cooperation patterns. Eurojust-supported European actions such as Operation Perfect Storm, the Spanish pay-TV streaming network takedown, Mobdro, and Operation Taken Down use cross-border search, seizure, EIO, MLA, and Europol analytical support to disrupt distributed streaming infrastructure. Operation 404 shows a non-European umbrella model led by Brazil with U.S., UK, Latin American, and private-sector IP partners.

## Analytical Use

Use this category when the operation target is an illegal IPTV, pirate streaming, or audiovisual-piracy infrastructure service rather than a conventional malware, ransomware, dark-web market, or CSAM platform. If a source frames the conduct mainly as malware delivery, fraud, or money laundering, keep those as additional wiki context but export only this `illegal-iptv-ic` node for the five-category SNA subset.

title: Malware Operations in International Cooperation
text:
## Summary

Malware operations in this wiki cover botnets, loaders, banking trojans, infostealers, remote-access tools, and malware-enabled proxy services where cross-border coordination is necessary to identify operators, seize infrastructure, notify victims, or neutralize command-and-control systems. The cooperation problem is technical and legal at the same time: investigators need lawful access to servers and domains, while defenders need enough telemetry to prevent reinfection or migration to replacement infrastructure.

## Cooperation Pattern

Emotet, QakBot, and Operation Endgame illustrate the recurring pattern. Authorities coordinate search and seizure steps across countries, technical partners help map the malware ecosystem, and judicial or prosecutorial channels prepare the action-day package. In mature takedowns, the goal is not only to arrest operators. It is also to interrupt delivery chains, redirect or seize domains, preserve evidence, and create follow-on leads for affiliates, customers, or money-laundering services.

## Menu Use

Use this page when the principal target is malware deployment or malware infrastructure. Use [[banking-trojan-ic]] when the malware is specifically tied to online-banking theft, [[ransomware-ic]] when encryption and extortion dominate the record, and [[cybercrime-infrastructure-ic]] when the source is mainly about hosting, proxy, VPN, marketplace, or access-broker infrastructure that supports multiple crime types.

## Analytical Use

Within this wiki, **Malware Operations in International Cooperation** is a control node for comparing records that describe the offense pattern, evidence sources, and cross-border investigative pressure points. Use the page to separate three questions that often become mixed in operation narratives: what conduct or authority is being discussed, which agency or state actor exercised it, and where the cross-border dependency appears in the evidence chain. That separation makes the menu more useful for comparing takedowns, prosecutions, extradition matters, and assistance requests without treating every related record as the same kind of cooperation event.

## Integrity Notes

The page groups terminology; it is not independent proof that the concept, crime type, mechanism, or framework applied in every linked matter. Operation and case pages remain the controlling records for arrests, seizures, requests, treaty use, and participating agencies. When adding future links, keep the distinction between direct source evidence, inferred analytical classification, and catalog navigation visible in the target page.

title: Money Laundering (Cryptocurrency-Enabled) — International Cooperation Perspective
text:
## Summary

Money laundering — particularly **cryptocurrency-enabled money laundering** through mixing services, illicit exchanges, and obfuscation tooling — is a cross-cutting cybercrime enabler that converts the proceeds of nearly every other cybercrime category (ransomware, online fraud, drug trafficking, weapons trafficking, BEC) into spendable value. It is *almost certainly* the most internationally interconnected category of cybercrime activity, because illicit cryptocurrency flows by their nature cross every border with each on-chain hop.

This page focuses on the **international cooperation** dimension: how states and EU agencies coordinate to dismantle laundering infrastructure, freeze cryptocurrency, and prosecute the operators of mixers, swap services, and informal value-transfer networks.

## Criminalization Landscape

- **Broadly criminalized**: Money laundering is criminalized in nearly every jurisdiction, typically under standalone statutes (US 18 U.S.C. § 1956, EU 6th AML Directive, etc.).
- **Definition variation**: Whether *operating* a mixing service (as distinct from using one) is itself criminal varies. The US has prosecuted mixer operators under unlicensed money transmission and conspiracy theories; some EU states require a predicate offence linkage.
- **Crypto-specific gaps**: Regulatory perimeters around crypto-asset service providers (CASPs) under MiCA (EU) and FinCEN (US) are still maturing, leaving informal mixers in legal grey zones in some jurisdictions.

## Typical IC Scenarios

1. **Mixer takedown**: Authorities in the country hosting the mixer's infrastructure coordinate with destination-of-funds jurisdictions to seize servers and freeze cryptocurrency.
2. **Cross-jurisdictional asset tracing**: Multiple countries' financial intelligence units share blockchain analytics and bank account information.
3. **Operator extradition**: Mixer/exchange operators arrested in one country are extradited to a country where prosecutorial theory is more developed (often the US).

## Cooperation Pathways

- **[[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]]** provides cryptocurrency tracing and analytical support to EU member-state investigations.
- **[[eurojust|Eurojust]]** coordinates judicial cooperation, including for non-EU partners (e.g., Switzerland) under bilateral agreements.
- **[[mutual-legal-assistance|MLAT]]** processes are invoked for compelling production from exchanges and freezing assets in non-cooperative jurisdictions.
- **Bilateral DE-CH cooperation** (as in the [[de-ch-crypto-mixer-takedown-2025|December 2025 mixer takedown]]) is *highly likely* a routine pattern, given Switzerland's role as a global financial hub and Germany's role as a major EU enforcement actor.

## Key Operations and Precedents

| Operation | Year | Countries | Outcome |
|-----------|------|-----------|---------|
| [[de-ch-crypto-mixer-takedown-2025]] | 2025 | DE, CH | EUR 25M+ seized, 3 servers down |
| [[cryptex-pm2btc-sanctions]] | 2024 | US-led | Sanctions and indictments |
| [[2bagoldmule-qqaazz]] | 2020 | EU + US | Money mule network dismantled |

## Challenges

- **Speed of crypto flows**: Funds can be moved in seconds across many wallets, outpacing MLA timelines.
- **Mixer deniability**: Operators routinely claim "no knowledge" of customers' criminal proceeds.
- **Jurisdiction shopping**: Mixer hosts are frequently relocated across friendly jurisdictions in response to enforcement pressure.
- **Privacy coin obfuscation**: Monero and similar privacy-preserving chains complicate tracing.

## Korean Context (한국 상황)

[[south-korea|South Korea]]'s position is *likely* significant given its large cryptocurrency exchange market (Upbit, Bithumb) and historical exposure to North Korean DPRK laundering. The Korean Financial Intelligence Unit (KoFIU) cooperates internationally on suspicious cryptocurrency transactions. No direct Korean involvement is documented in the [[de-ch-crypto-mixer-takedown-2025|December 2025 DE-CH mixer takedown]].

##

title: Online Fraud — International Cooperation Perspective
text:
## Summary

Online fraud encompasses a broad spectrum of cyber-enabled financial crimes, including voice phishing (보이스피싱), romance scams, business email compromise (BEC), investment fraud, online gambling fraud, and e-commerce fraud. From an international cooperation perspective, online fraud is *almost certainly* the highest-volume cybercrime type requiring cross-border cooperation, affecting millions of victims annually across all regions.

INTERPOL's HAECHI series of operations has been the primary international enforcement framework for online fraud, with [[operation-haechi-v|Operation HAECHI V]] (2024) setting records: **5,500+ arrests** and **USD 400 million+** seized across **40 countries**.

## Criminalization Landscape

Online fraud is broadly criminalized worldwide under various statutes:
- **Fraud/deception statutes:** Most jurisdictions have general fraud provisions covering online variants
- **Cybercrime statutes:** Many jurisdictions have specific computer fraud provisions
- **Financial crime statutes:** Money laundering and proceeds-of-crime provisions frequently apply

## Typical IC Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Large-Scale Multi-Country Enforcement Sweep
**Pattern:** INTERPOL-coordinated operations targeting multiple fraud types simultaneously across many countries.
**Example:** [[operation-haechi-v|Operation HAECHI V]] — 40 countries, 5,500+ arrests, USD 400M+ seized

### Scenario 2: Regional Enforcement Operation
**Pattern:** International-regional partnership targeting fraud networks in a specific region.
**Example:** [[operation-serengeti|Operation Serengeti]] — INTERPOL-AFRIPOL, 19 African countries, 1,006 arrests

## Key Operations and Precedents

| Operation | Date | Countries | Arrests | Seized | Focus |
|-----------|------|-----------|---------|--------|-------|
| HAECHI III | 2022 | 30 | 975 | ~$130M | Cyber-enabled financial crime | [^haechi3]
| [[operation-jackal]] | 2023 | 21 | 103 | EUR 2.15M | Black Axe BEC/financial fraud |

[^haechi3]: > [!warning] Source needed — HAECHI III statistics (975 arrests, ~$130M) are not from a collected source. The HAECHI IV press release references a "260% arrest increase over HAECHI III" but does not provide explicit HAECHI III totals.
| [[operation-haechi-iv]] | 2023 | 34 | 3,500 | $300M | Expanded scope, 7 crime types |
| [[operation-jackal-iii]] | 2024 | 21 | ~300 | $3M | Black Axe escalation |
| [[operation-first-light-2024]] | 2024 | 61 | 3,950 | $257M | Online scams, I-GRIP |
| [[operation-haechi-v]] | 2024 | 40 | 5,500+ | $400M+ | Record HAECHI operation |
| [[operation-serengeti]] | 2024 | 19 | 1,006 | - | Africa-focused |
| [[operation-haechi-vi]] | 2025 | 40 | - | $439M | New recovery record |
| [[operation-sentinel-africa]] | 2025 | 19 | 574 | $3M | BEC/ransomware in Africa |

## Korean Context (한국 상황)

Korea is *almost certainly* one of the most heavily affected countries by voice phishing (보이스피싱), which has been a major law enforcement priority for the [[knpa-cyber-bureau|KNPA Cyber Bureau]]. Korea's leadership of the HAECHI series reflects this domestic priority translated into international cooperation:

- Korea led all five HAECHI operations
- In HAECHI V, Korea and China jointly dismantled a voice phishing syndicate causing **USD 1.1 billion** in losses to **1,900+ victims**
- Voice phishing schemes targeting Korean victims frequently originate from China and Southeast Asia, making international cooperation essential

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What is the total global annual loss from online fraud?
- How effective are asset seizures at deterring online fraud networks?
- What is the recidivism rate for online fraud suspects after arrest?

## References

| # | Title | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|-------|----------|------|-----|
| [1] | INTERPOL financial crime operation makes record 5,500 arrests — Operation HAECHI V | INTERPOL | 2024-12-02 | [원본](https://www.interpol.int/News-and-Events/News/2024/INTERPOL-financial-crime-operation-makes
