title: Korea Internet & Security Agency (KISA)
text:
> [!info] Status — Active, restructured 2026-01
> KISA remains the same legal entity (한국인터넷진흥원, established July 2009) under the [[ministry-of-science-and-ict-korea|Ministry of Science and ICT]]. It underwent an **internal organizational restructuring in January 2026** that created three new units: **AI보안산업본부 (AI Security Industry Division), 디지털위협예방본부 (Digital Threat Prevention Division), and 랜섬웨어대응팀 (Ransomware Response Team)** [5][6]. The restructuring is *highly likely* a response to the 2025 surge in large-scale personal-data breaches (KT, SKT, Coupang) and is aligned with the Lee Jae-myung administration's "AI 기본사회" national policy agenda. KrCERT/CC, FIRST/APCERT membership, and the international-cooperation function are preserved and strengthened.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** 한국인터넷진흥원 (Korea Internet & Security Agency, KISA) — unchanged
**Parent:** [[ministry-of-science-and-ict-korea|과학기술정보통신부]] (Ministry of Science and ICT) — unchanged
**Headquarters:** Naju, South Korea — unchanged
**Status:** **Active**, legally unchanged, internally restructured January 2026 [5][6]

The post-January 2026 structure adds an **AI보안산업본부** responsible for AI security policy, industry cultivation, R&D, and workforce development; a **디지털위협예방본부** focused on preventive digital-threat mitigation; and a dedicated **랜섬웨어대응팀** for ransomware response [5][6]. A parallel but separate MSIT-level restructuring in early 2026 also renamed MSIT's 네트워크정책실 to **정보보호네트워크정책실** and created a 5-person 사이버침해조사팀 inside MSIT [7]. These are MSIT-side changes and do not alter KISA's corporate status, but they change KISA's supervisory context.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2009-07 | **KISA established** by merging Korea Information Security Agency, NIDA, and KIICA under the Act on Internet Address Resources and related statutes | [3] |
| 2010-12 | **KrCERT/CC launched** as South Korea's national CSIRT | [2] |
| 2013 | Supervising ministry transferred from MIC/KCC to the newly created Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (미래창조과학부) | [3] |
| 2017 | Supervising ministry renamed to **과학기술정보통신부 (Ministry of Science and ICT)** after Moon administration reorganization | [3] |
| 2019 | HQ relocation to **Naju**, Jeollanam-do (Bitgaram City) completed | [3] |
| 2026-01-09 | **Internal restructuring**: AI보안산업본부, 디지털위협예방본부, 랜섬웨어대응팀 newly created; organization realigned around "AI 기본사회" policy goals | [5][6] |
| 2026 (ongoing) | MSIT-level reorganization creates 정보보호네트워크정책실 and 사이버침해조사팀; 정부 차원 정보보호 조직 근본 개편 논의 진행 중 | [7] |

## Summary

The Korea Internet & Security Agency (KISA, 한국인터넷진흥원) is the sub-organization of the [[ministry-of-science-and-ict-korea|Ministry of Science and ICT]] (과학기술정보통신부) responsible for internet governance and cybersecurity of the internet in South Korea. KISA was created in **July 2009** by merging three predecessor agencies: the Korea Information Security Agency (KISA, 한국정보보호진흥원), the National Internet Development Agency (NIDA, 한국인터넷진흥원 — same Korean name, different English), and the Korean IT International Cooperation Agency (KIICA).

KISA is **not a law enforcement agency**; its role in international cooperation on cybercrime is through **incident response coordination**, **threat intelligence sharing**, and **capacity building**, rather than arrests or prosecutions. KISA operates **KrCERT/CC** (launched December 2010) as South Korea's national Computer Security Incident Response Team.

## Mandate and Authority

KISA's mandate is grounded in the Act on Promotion of Information and Communications Network Utilization and Information Protection (정보통신망법) and related statutes. Its cybercrime-relevant functions include:

- **Incident response:** KrCERT/CC coordinates response to major cyber incidents affecting Korean networks, including phishing, ransomware, DDoS, and malware campaigns.
- **Threat intelligence:** Collects, analyzes, and shares cyber threat intelligen

title: KNPA Cybercrime Enforcement Organization (경찰청 사이버범죄 수사조직)
text:
> [!warning] Status — Reorganized (as of 2023-12)
> The standalone **경찰청 사이버수사국** (KNPA Cyber Investigation Bureau) was dissolved in late 2023 and its functions were absorbed into **경찰청 국가수사본부 수사국**, with a new **사이버수사심의관 (Cyber Investigation Deliberation Officer)** position created inside 수사국 at *deputy-director level* rather than bureau-director level. As of 2026-04-10 the **사이버수사국 no longer exists**; cybercrime investigation is conducted by the 사이버수사심의관 and 사이버수사과 within 수사국. A re-establishment of 사이버안전국 is under active consideration but has not yet been enacted (see [Current Status](#current-status-as-of-2026-04-10) below).
>
> This page preserves the full historical arc and current state. Operations completed before December 2023 were carried out under the 사이버수사국; operations from 2024 onward are carried out by the 수사국 사이버수사심의관 and its subordinate units.

## Summary

This page documents the **national cybercrime enforcement apparatus of the Korea National Police Agency (KNPA, 경찰청)** across three named incarnations: **사이버안전국 (Cyber Safety Bureau, ~2014–2021)**, **사이버수사국 (Cyber Investigation Bureau, 2021–2023)**, and **수사국 사이버수사심의관 (Investigation Bureau's Cyber Investigation Deliberation Officer, 2023–present)**. Throughout all three iterations the unit has served as **INTERPOL NCB Korea for cyber matters** and led Korea's participation in major multilateral cybercrime operations, most notably the **HAECHI** series [1][2].

Because the operational mandate, personnel, and operational continuity have been substantially preserved across reorganizations, this page treats them as a single evolving organization rather than three separate entities.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** 경찰청 국가수사본부 수사국 — 사이버수사심의관 및 사이버수사과
**English:** KNPA National Investigation Headquarters, Investigation Bureau — Cyber Investigation Deliberation Officer and Cyber Investigation Division
**Level:** Deliberation-officer level (심의관, 경무관급), subordinate to 수사국장 (director-general of 수사국) [3]
**Parent:** 국가수사본부 (National Investigation Headquarters) → 수사국 (Investigation Bureau)

The functions previously exercised by the standalone 사이버수사국 (bureau-director level, 치안감 또는 경무관) are now exercised at deliberation-officer level within the broader 수사국. According to April 2026 press reporting, this reorganization correlated with a decline in cybercrime clearance rates (67.5% → 54.1%) and a 34.4% increase in cybercrime incidents, prompting a proposal to **re-establish a standalone 사이버안전국** [4].

**Pending re-establishment (planned, not yet in force):** The KNPA has proposed re-creating a standalone **사이버안전국 (Cyber Safety Bureau)** separated out of 수사국. The plan is subject to 행안부·기재부 협의 and the December 2026 National Assembly budget review, with implementation expected no earlier than **March 2027** if approved [4]. Under the proposal the **사이버범죄수사과** would be renamed **사이버안전과** and approximately **20 additional staff** would be added at the bureau level.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Level | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2014-08 | **사이버안전국** established under 경찰청 by elevating prior cybercrime units | 국장급 (bureau-director) | [2] |
| 2021-01-01 | **국가수사본부 (NIO)** created; 사이버안전국 absorbed into the new NIO and **renamed 사이버수사국** | 국장급 | [2][5] |
| 2021-2023 | 사이버수사국 operates at bureau level; leads HAECHI II/III/IV, Korea-China operations | 국장급 | [1] |
| 2023-12 | **사이버수사국 dissolved**; functions absorbed into 수사국; **사이버수사심의관** newly created inside 수사국 | 심의관급 (deputy-director) | [3][4] |
| 2023-2024 | Reorganization period; HAECHI V and other operations continue under the new 심의관 structure | 심의관급 | [1] |
| 2026-04 | KNPA announces plan to **re-establish 사이버안전국** (separated from 수사국) pending budget and approval | Pending | [4] |
| 2027-03 (planned, not yet in force) | **사이버안전국** expected re-establishment date if approved | 국장급 | [4] |

## Mandate and Authority

Across all three incarnations the unit's mandate has covered:
- Invest

title: Korea National Police Agency (경찰청)
text:
## Summary

The **Korea National Police Agency (경찰청, KNPA)** is the national police service of the Republic of Korea. Established in 1945, it is South Korea's primary law enforcement authority. Cybercrime investigation is handled within the **국가수사본부 (National Investigation Headquarters, NIO)**, established in 2021 as part of a major police reform.

This page is a parent-organization entry; for the operational cybercrime apparatus (currently 수사국 사이버수사심의관) see **[[knpa-cyber-bureau|KNPA Cybercrime Enforcement Organization]]**.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

- **Commissioner General**: (varies; check KNPA press releases for current holder)
- **Parent ministry**: 행정안전부 (Ministry of the Interior and Safety)
- **National Investigation Headquarters (국가수사본부)**: 3 bureaus — 수사국 (Investigation), 형사국 (Criminal), 안보수사국 (Security Investigation)
- Cybercrime investigation is currently conducted at 심의관 level within 수사국 (see [[knpa-cyber-bureau]])
- **Pending reorganization**: Re-establishment of standalone 사이버안전국 proposed, expected no earlier than 2027-03 if approved [2]

## Mandate and Authority

KNPA operates under the 경찰법 (Police Act) and 경찰공무원법 (Police Officer Act). Its mandate covers:
- General policing and investigation
- Traffic safety
- Public order
- Intelligence gathering (security-related, via 안보수사국)
- Counter-terrorism coordination
- Cybercrime investigation (via NIO)

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **국가수사본부 (NIO)** — serves as the investigation arm
  - **수사국** — general and cyber investigations (hosts [[knpa-cyber-bureau|사이버수사심의관]])
  - **형사국** — criminal and forensic investigations
  - **안보수사국** — security, counter-terrorism, technology leaks

## IC Capabilities

- **INTERPOL NCB Korea**: KNPA is the operational NCB
- **I-24/7 access**: Direct connection to INTERPOL's secure network
- **Liaison officers**: Posted to Europol, INTERPOL IGCI Singapore, and selected bilateral partners

## Key Operations and Cases

See [[knpa-cyber-bureau]] for detailed operation participation (HAECHI series, Phobos/8Base, etc.). KNPA as the parent agency hosts the operational units that execute these operations.

## Cooperation Track Record

- **INTERPOL**: Active NCB with substantial operational volume
- **Europol**: Strategic Agreement enables regular cooperation
- **FBI**: Bilateral cooperation on ransomware, DPRK cyber threats
- **China MPS, Japan NPA**: Regional bilateral cooperation

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| 1945-10-21 | 경찰청 창설 |
| 1991-07-31 | 내무부 치안본부 → 경찰청으로 승격 |
| 2020 | 자치경찰제 도입 (부분) |
| 2021-01-01 | **국가수사본부 신설** — 수사 독립성 확보 개혁 |
| 2023-12 | 사이버수사국 → 수사국에 통합 (see [[knpa-cyber-bureau]]) |
| 2027-03 (planned) | 사이버안전국 재신설 예정 (pending budget approval) [2] |

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Exact current Commissioner General and assistant commissioners require verification against 경찰청 press releases
- The 2027 reorganization is dependent on 행안부·기재부 협의 and budget approval

## References

|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | 경찰청 공식 웹사이트 | KNPA | — | https://www.police.go.kr/ |
| [2] | 경찰청, 사이버안전국·여청안전국 신설 추진 | 뉴시스 | 2026-04-06 | https://www.newsis.com/view/NISX20260406_0003578778 |

title: Lithuanian Criminal Police Bureau — Cybercrime Unit
text:
## Summary

The **Lithuanian Criminal Police Bureau (Lietuvos kriminalinės policijos biuras — LKPB)** is the central criminal police authority of Lithuania, operating throughout the country on a non-territorial basis under the Police Department of the Ministry of the Interior [1][2]. It includes cybercrime investigative capability and is Lithuania's principal criminal police interface for international cybercrime cooperation.

LKPB is *highly likely* the primary Lithuanian contact for Europol EC3 cybercrime operations and for Budapest Convention Article 35 requests, though precise 24/7 contact point attribution requires verification.

## Mandate and Authority

LKPB operates with nationwide competence for serious and organized crime, including cybercrime [1][2]. It is not organized territorially but functionally, allowing specialized units (such as cybercrime) to investigate cases across Lithuania regardless of regional boundaries.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

Public information on LKPB's internal structure is limited. Confirmed details:

- Headquartered in Vilnius at Saltoniškių g. 19 [1]
- Operates specialized functional divisions, including cybercrime
- In September 2024, LKPB began an EU-funded project (Norway Grants) in partnership with the **National Cyber Security Centre (Nacionalinis kibernetinio saugumo centras — NKSC)** to create a laboratory for investigating criminal acts in cyberspace and training cybercrime investigators [3]

## IC Capabilities

- **Europol National Unit** channel (Lithuania is a Europol member state)
- **Interpol NCB Vilnius** channel
- **Budapest Convention:** Lithuania is a party; LKPB *likely* routes related cooperation
- **Joint cyber investigation lab** (under construction, funded 2024) with NKSC for digital forensics and training [3]

## Key Operations and Cases

Specific operation attributions are not confirmed in the currently ingested sources. Lithuania *likely* has participated in Europol-coordinated European cybercrime operations such as Endgame and Cronos, but attributions should be verified per operation.

## Cooperation Track Record

- EU member state since 2004; routine Europol and Eurojust cooperation
- NATO member; cyber defense cooperation through NATO channels
- Budapest Convention party
- Partner with Nordic and Baltic law enforcement on regional cyber issues

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Exact year the LKPB cybercrime unit was established
- Headcount of LKPB cybercrime investigators
- Whether LKPB holds the Lithuanian [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] Art. 35 24/7 contact point
- Participation record in named international cyber operations

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Lithuanian Criminal Police Bureau (LCPB) | LKPB (official) | n.d. | https://lkpb.policija.lrv.lt/en/about-us/lithuanian-criminal-police-bureau-lcpb/ |
| [2] | Lietuvos kriminalinės policijos biuras | Policijos departamentas (LRV) | n.d. | https://policija.lrv.lt/lt/veiklos-sritys/nusikalstamu-veiklu-atskleidimas-ir-tyrimas/lietuvos-kriminalines-policijos-biuras/ |
| [3] | LKPB and NKSC joint cyber investigation laboratory project | LKPB | 2024-09 | https://lkpb.policija.lrv.lt/lt/naujienos/lietuvos-kriminalines-policijos-biuras-kartu-su-nacionaliniu-kibernetinio-saugumo-centru-prie-krasto-apsaugos-ministerijos-igyvendina-projekta-nusikalstamu-veiku-elektronineje-erdveje-tyrimui-ir-mokymams-skirtos-laboratorijos-sukurimas-ir-iveiklinimas/ |

title: Royal Malaysia Police — Commercial Crime Investigation Department (Cyber Crime)
text:
## Summary

The Royal Malaysia Police (Polis Diraja Malaysia, **PDRM**) handles cybercrime investigations primarily through its **Commercial Crime Investigation Department (CCID)**, which was formed on **1 December 2004** and is the leading department in commercial and cybercrime investigations. CCID investigates and prosecutes white collar criminals who commit fraud, criminal breach of trust, cybercrime, forgeries, and related offences.

PDRM has participated in major transnational cybercrime operations with Five Eyes and regional partners. In **November 2023**, PDRM publicly disclosed a joint intelligence-sharing operation with the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] and [[australia-afp|Australian Federal Police]] that crippled a transnational cybercrime syndicate — demonstrating operational cooperation capacity with major Western agencies.

## Mandate and Authority

PDRM derives its authority under Malaysia's federal constitution and Police Act 1967. Cybercrime-relevant statutes include:

- **Computer Crimes Act 1997** — Malaysia's primary cybercrime statute
- **Communications and Multimedia Act 1998** — telecommunications and online offences
- **Penal Code** — general criminal offences, including fraud
- **Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act (AMLA) 2001**

Malaysia is *not* a party to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] as of this writing, though Malaysian cybercrime law reform discussions (including at the CYDES 2025 conference) have considered potential alignment.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **Commercial Crime Investigation Department (CCID):** Federal-level investigative body for commercial and cybercrime.
- **Cybercrime Investigation Division / CyberCrime Investigation section:** CCID's dedicated cyber unit.
- **State-level CCID branches:** Replicated across Malaysian states for regional cybercrime investigation.
- **INTERPOL Kuala Lumpur:** PDRM operates the INTERPOL NCB; Kuala Lumpur also hosts the [[interpol-igci|INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI)]], providing uniquely close proximity to INTERPOL cybercrime assets.

> [!note] The host relationship with INTERPOL IGCI (opened 2015, Singapore and later consolidated activities) gives Malaysia/PDRM notable institutional access to global INTERPOL cybercrime operations, though IGCI is in Singapore, not Malaysia. This should be verified — some sources conflate the IGCI location.

## IC Capabilities

- **FBI cooperation:** The November 2023 joint takedown with FBI and AFP demonstrates operational-level cooperation capacity.
- **INTERPOL:** Strong NCB relationship; Malaysia is an active INTERPOL member.
- **ASEANAPOL:** Malaysia is a founding member of ASEANAPOL, the Association of ASEAN Chiefs of Police, which coordinates regional cooperation.
- **ASEAN cybercrime cooperation:** Malaysia participates in ASEAN senior officials meetings on transnational crime (SOMTC).

## Key Operations and Cases

- **November 2023 joint operation (FBI + AFP + PDRM):** Transnational cybercrime syndicate disruption. Details limited in public reporting.

> [!info] Additional specific operations should be added as sources are ingested.

## Cooperation Track Record

PDRM's cooperation capacity is *likely* moderate-to-high for regional and Five Eyes-coordinated operations, particularly on scam compound and romance fraud investigations (given the region's exposure to Southeast Asian scam compound activity). *Highly likely* active in cross-border scam enforcement with Singapore, Thailand, and the Philippines.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- The exact name and internal structure of PDRM's dedicated cybercrime unit vary across sources — "CyberCrime Investigation Division" may be an informal reference rather than a formal organizational name.
- Malaysia's cybercrime law reform trajectory (toward or away from Budapest Convention alignment) remains under negotiation.
- PDRM's cooperation with neighboring Thailand and M

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The KNPA source identifies the Korea National Police Agency as South Korea's primary law-enforcement authority, with cybercrime investigation handled through national investigation structures. The prosecution-source material in the corpus also flags reform discussions and future institutional division of investigation and prosecution functions, including cybercrime as a serious-crime category.

Use this page when a Korean source discusses ministry-level placement, police governance, or future serious-crime investigation structure. text:
> [!warning] Status — Renamed and restructured
> The current **과학기술정보통신부 (Ministry of Science and ICT, MSIT)** was created in its present form on **2017-07-26** under the Moon Jae-in administration, inheriting the functions of the prior **미래창조과학부 (Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning, MSIP, 2013–2017)**, which itself was created by merging parts of the earlier **정보통신부 (Ministry of Information and Communication)** and other bodies. The English short form has occasionally been confused with "Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning" or miswritten as "과학수사정보통신부" (an incorrect form conflating 과학수사 and 과기정통부). The correct current name is **과학기술정보통신부 / Ministry of Science and ICT** [1][2].
>
> As of early 2026 MSIT is undergoing an **internal restructuring** centered on information security: the **네트워크정책실** has been renamed **정보보호네트워크정책실**, a **5-person 사이버침해조사팀** has been newly created to handle cyber-incident investigation, and Minister 배경훈 has publicly signaled that a broader government-level reorganization of the national information-protection apparatus is under active discussion [3][4]. These changes are a response to the 2025 large-scale data breaches (KT, SKT, Coupang, telecom providers) and do not involve any change to MSIT's legal identity.

## Summary

The **Ministry of Science and ICT (과학기술정보통신부, MSIT)** is South Korea's central government department responsible for science, ICT, broadcasting, information security policy, and AI policy. In the international cooperation on cybercrime ecosystem, MSIT's role is **policy-making and oversight**, not investigation or prosecution. Its primary operational arm is [[kisa|KISA]] (한국인터넷진흥원), which operates KrCERT/CC as Korea's national CSIRT and handles technical incident coordination with foreign counterparts.

MSIT is *not* a law enforcement agency. Criminal cybercrime cases are handled by the [[knpa-cyber-bureau|Korea National Police Agency]] and, after October 2026, the [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|공소청·중수청 successor entities]] depending on final organizational structure.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** 과학기술정보통신부 (Ministry of Science and ICT, MSIT) — unchanged since 2017-07-26
**Minister (2026):** 배경훈 (부총리 겸 과학기술정보통신부 장관) [3]
**Headquarters:** Government Complex Sejong
**Status:** **Active**, internally restructured January 2026

Post-January 2026 internal changes [3][4]:
- **네트워크정책실 → 정보보호네트워크정책실**: name change reflecting information-protection priority
- **사이버침해조사팀 (Cyber Breach Investigation Team) — newly created**: 5 staff (up from 2 prior investigators), responsible for ministry-level cyber incident investigation and coordination with KISA/KrCERT/CC
- **AI 기반 침해 대응 자동화 체계 (AI-based breach-response automation)**: announced 2026 work plan, to be developed with KISA
- **ISMS-P 인증제도 개편**: revised information security certification scheme, co-developed with 개인정보보호위원회 [4]

Minister 배경훈 has publicly stated that a **근본적 정보보호 조직 개편 (fundamental restructuring of the national information-protection apparatus)** is under inter-ministerial discussion with a timeline for public announcement; details not yet released [3].

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| –2013 | Functions distributed across **정보통신부 (MIC)** and **방송통신위원회 (KCC)** and other bodies under pre-Park administrations | [2] |
| 2013-03 | **미래창조과학부 (Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning, MSIP)** created under Park Geun-hye administration; supervisory authority over KISA transferred to MSIP | [2] |
| 2017-07-26 | **Renamed to 과학기술정보통신부 (Ministry of Science and ICT, MSIT)** under Moon Jae-in administration | [1][2] |
| 2022-05 | Continued under Yoon Suk-yeol administration without name change | [2] |
| 2025-06 | Under Lee Jae-myung administration; Minister elevated to Deputy Prime Minister rank (부총리 겸) | [3] |
| 2026-01 | **Internal restructuring** of information-protection units: 정보보호네트워크정책실, 사이버침해조사팀 newly created/expanded in response t

title: DGSN
text:
## Summary

**DGSN** is Morocco's National Security Directorate. In this corpus, it provides police-side context for Moroccan cybercrime investigation and arrests, while [[maroc-cert]] covers the technical incident-response role and [[morocco-dgst]] covers intelligence/security-service context.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Operation Lyrebird is the main operation anchor: INTERPOL reported a Moroccan arrest tied to phishing, carding, and online fraud. The source set does not always cleanly separate DGSN and DGST roles, so attribution should remain source-bound.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is adequate for a short institutional page but still needs more direct Moroccan official material for cybercrime unit structure.

title: DGST
text:
## Summary

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Operation Lyrebird provides the strongest local operation anchor for Morocco: INTERPOL announced a Moroccan arrest tied to phishing, carding, and online fraud activity. The Council of Europe profile supplies broader legal and treaty context for Morocco.

title: NACSA
text:
## Summary

NACSA is retained as an organization node in the international cybercrime cooperation graph. The current mandate field records: Malaysia's national cybersecurity coordinator, established under the National Security Council; policy and critical national information infrastructure protection. Country or jurisdiction metadata is recorded as [[malaysia]]. This page is used for menu navigation, operation back-links, and partner analysis rather than as a complete institutional profile.

## Cooperation Graph Role

Linked operation records: No operation backlink is currently linked.. These links are the safest basis for interpreting this organization in the repository because they connect the organization to named enforcement actions, source records, and cooperation mechanisms already present in the corpus. Where an operation page names multiple agencies, this organization page should be read together with the operation's lead agency, coordinating body, participating-country list, and source table.

## Source Coverage

The references below provide 1 source link(s) for the current organization record. The page does not infer powers, staffing, budget, or treaty authority beyond what the linked operation and source records support. When only one source is available, the record is still useful for navigation but should be treated as limited coverage until official organizational material or additional operation sources are linked.

## Data Integrity Notes

This page has been normalized for the organization audit: it includes role metadata, source links where available, references, and a clear distinction between confirmed corpus relationships and unverified institutional detail. Future edits should prefer official agency pages, prosecutor releases, court records, Europol or INTERPOL releases, and operation-specific press releases. Trade press can be used for context, but it should not override official source records for authority, participation, or legal powers.

title: NCFTA
text:
## Summary

NCFTA is the National Cyber-Forensics and Training Alliance, represented here as a U.S.-based public-private collaboration body. It is used in the wiki where sources describe industry, law-enforcement, and analytical collaboration around cybercrime investigations.

## International Cooperation Role

The strongest local source link is Operation Shrouded Horizon, where NCFTA appears in the public-private support environment around the Darkode forum takedown. GozNym material also illustrates the broader need for financial-sector and technical-sector cooperation in malware-enabled bank fraud. Use this page for the collaboration body, not for a law-enforcement agency or prosecutorial authority.

title: National Center for Missing and Exploited Children
text:
## Summary

The **National Center for Missing and Exploited Children (NCMEC)** is the U.S. child-protection clearinghouse most relevant to cybercrime investigations involving online child sexual exploitation. In this corpus it is used for CyberTipline reporting, victim-identification support, and referral pathways connecting technology platforms, law enforcement, and prosecutors.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

NCMEC is not a police agency. Its importance comes from intake and routing: reports of suspected child exploitation can be filed through CyberTipline channels, and law-enforcement investigations frequently refer to NCMEC tips, image review, or victim-identification support. The DOJ Restore Justice source also describes the Justice Department's partnership and grant relationship with NCMEC, while the IC3 source distinguishes ordinary internet-crime reporting from child-exploitation reporting routed to NCMEC.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when a U.S. child-exploitation case begins with a CyberTipline report, platform referral, or NCMEC assistance. Use [[fbi-cyber-division]], HSI, state police, or local taskforce pages for the arresting/investigating agency, and [[interpol-icse-database]] or [[europol-vidtf]] for international victim-identification mechanisms.

## Source Coverage

Current coverage is sufficient for the reporting/referral role. Future enrichment should add direct NCMEC institutional sources and CyberTipline statistics if available locally.

## References

title: FIOD — Dutch Fiscal Information and Investigation Service
text:
## Summary

The **Fiscale Inlichtingen- en Opsporingsdienst (FIOD)** is the investigation service of the **Dutch Ministry of Finance**, responsible for combating financial fraud, money laundering, cybercrime and corruption [1]. Unlike the Dutch National Police cyber units, FIOD operates with financial-crime jurisdiction and specializes in the monetary and cryptocurrency dimensions of cybercrime.

FIOD's **Financial Advanced Cyber Team (FACT)** is *almost certainly* one of Europe's most active cryptocurrency criminal investigation units, with substantial expertise in crypto tracing, exchange takedowns and crypto-enabled money laundering [2][3]. FACT works closely with the Dutch National Police's High Tech Crime Unit (NHTCU), US Secret Service and Europol on major operations.

## Mandate and Authority

FIOD traces offenders of financial cybercrime, targeting crimes committed with digital devices including hacking, online fraud and money laundering with digital currency [1]. Its statutory base is the financial-crime mandate of the Dutch Ministry of Finance, making it distinct from the Politie's police cybercrime capacity.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

### FACT — Financial Advanced Cyber Team

FIOD hosts specialized teams under the FACT brand with substantive expertise in cryptocurrencies, conducting investigations that primarily relate to cybercrime, drug trafficking, fraud and money laundering offences [1]. FACT is *highly likely* one of the most technically capable European crypto-forensics units.

## IC Capabilities

- **Cryptocurrency tracing:** Deep expertise in on-chain analytics, mixer/tumbler investigation, and exchange disruption
- **Europol integration:** Routine cooperation via Europol EC3 and European Financial and Economic Crime Centre (EFECC)
- **US partnership:** Documented joint operations with US Secret Service [2]
- **Dutch domestic integration:** Works alongside [[netherlands-politie|Dutch National Police]] Team High Tech Crime (THTC / NHTCU)
- **Asset recovery:** Substantial seized-crypto track record (EUR 41M in 2024 alone) [3]

## Key Operations and Cases

| Operation / Case | Year | FIOD Role | Key Result |
|------------------|------|-----------|-----------|
| Dutch crypto swap service takedown (joint with German authorities) | 2025 | Co-lead investigator | Crypto swap service shut down [2] |
| Crypto money laundering takedowns (joint with US Secret Service) | 2025 | Joint investigator | Two major crypto exchanges taken down [2] |
| Record crypto seizure | 2024 | Investigator | EUR 41M cryptocurrency seized (joint with Dutch Police Team High Tech Crime) [3] |

Specific operation-level wikilinks pending ingestion of detailed press releases.

## Cooperation Track Record

- **Europol partner:** Frequent joint operations with EC3 and EFECC
- **US Secret Service:** Documented bilateral cooperation on crypto money laundering [2]
- **German partners:** Joint takedowns including the 2025 crypto swap service shutdown [2]
- **Netherlands domestic:** Standard partner of [[netherlands-politie|Dutch National Police]] High Tech Crime Unit

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- FIOD founding date (not confirmed in search results)
- FACT team headcount and establishment year
- Full list of FIOD-led international cybercrime operations
- Legal basis FIOD uses for incoming international crypto-tracing assistance requests

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Cybercrime | FIOD (official) | n.d. | https://www.fiod.nl/en/investigation-themes/cybercrime/ |
| [2] | German, Dutch authorities shut down crypto swap service in major money laundering probe | NL Times | 2025-05-10 | https://nltimes.nl/2025/05/10/german-dutch-authorities-shut-crypto-swap-service-major-money-laundering-probe |
| [3] | Dutch police seize EUR 41 million in cryptocurrency in record cybercrime bust | NL Times | 2025-01-23 | https://nltimes.nl/2025/01/23/dutch-police-seize-eu41-million-cryptocurrency-r

title: Netherlands Public Prosecution Service - Landelijk Parket
text:
## Summary

The **Openbaar Ministerie (OM)**, through the **Landelijk Parket**, is the Dutch prosecution-side institution most relevant to international cybercrime cooperation. It anchors the prosecutorial dimension of Dutch cybercrime work, complementing the police-side role of [[netherlands-politie]].

## Mandate and Authority

Official OM material shows that international cooperation is built around **international legal assistance** and **joint investigation teams**, and that cybercrime is treated as a cross-border crime category requiring those tools. The **Landelijk Parket** addresses internationally organized and serious crime, explicitly including cybercrime.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- prosecution-side counterpart to Dutch national police cyber investigations
- role in legal-assistance requests and cross-border judicial coordination
- institutional relevance for major international cybercrime prosecutions
- prosecutor-side bridge between Dutch police operations and formal international judicial cooperation

## IC Capabilities

- international legal assistance
- joint investigation team coordination
- prosecution support for multinational cybercrime cases
- integration into Dutch national serious-crime architecture

## Key Operations and Cases

The OM and Dutch police jointly announced the **Qakbot** botnet disruption in 2023, showing that Dutch cybercrime cooperation is not only police-driven but also prosecutor-integrated. The announcement tied the **Landelijk Parket** and the police **Team High Tech Crime** to a shared international action in which 22 servers were seized in Dutch datacenters.

That is a useful boundary marker for this repo: Dutch cyber operations are not all the same. Some are clearly police-led infrastructure takedowns in public reporting, while others, like **Qakbot**, have an explicit prosecution-side anchor. Where a named OM source exists, the repo should prefer that stronger prosecutor-operation connection rather than infer it from the country's general reputation.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- The exact internal cybercrime prosecutorial division between national and regional prosecution structures should be documented more precisely in a later pass.
- More direct OM sources tied to additional named cybercrime operations beyond Qakbot would further strengthen this page.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Internationale samenwerking | Openbaar Ministerie | current | https://www.om.nl/onderwerpen/internationale-samenwerking |
| 2 | Landelijk Parket | Openbaar Ministerie | current | https://www.om.nl/organisatie/landelijk-parket |
| 3 | Grootste wereldwijde botnet Qakbot onschadelijk gemaakt | Openbaar Ministerie | 2023-08-29 | https://www.om.nl/actueel/nieuws/2023/08/29/grootste-wereldwijde-botnet-qakbot-onschadelijk-gemaakt |

title: Netherlands Police Parent Organization
text:
## Summary

The **Netherlands Police parent organization** entry exists to separate the broader Dutch police institution from the specialist cyber unit page [[netherlands-politie]]. The Dutch source corpus repeatedly centers Team High Tech Crime as one of Europe's most active cybercrime units; this page preserves the parent-organization context.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Dutch police cybercrime work combines national technical investigators, prosecutorial partners, Europol and Eurojust channels, and international operational teams. The Dutch police Team High Tech Crime source describes high-complexity cybercrime work and highlights the importance of international cooperation because cybercrime crosses borders. The Council of Europe country-profile source gives the broader legal-cooperation background.

## IC Relevance

Use this parent page when the source speaks broadly about Dutch police or the national police organization. Use [[netherlands-politie]] when the source specifically names Team High Tech Crime, NHTCU/THTC, or the Dutch cyber unit in an operation.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for the cyber unit and legal cooperation context. Further direct parent-organization material can improve administrative hierarchy.

title: Dutch National Police - Team High Tech Crime (THTC)
text:
## Summary

The **Team High Tech Crime (THTC)** is the Dutch police's core national cybercrime investigation team. It is one of the most visible and operationally central cybercrime units in Europe and appears repeatedly in major multinational disruptions involving ransomware, botnets, dark-web markets, and criminal infrastructure.

## Mandate and Authority

Dutch police and Council of Europe material show that high-end cybercrime expertise is concentrated in the national police structure and that international legal-assistance work is deeply integrated into the Dutch model. THTC handles the most complex cybercrime cases and works in a system where the Netherlands combines strong domestic technical capability with unusually dense international coordination.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- nationally concentrated cybercrime capability inside the Dutch police
- close interaction with liaison and legal-assistance channels
- operational cooperation with foreign partners and prosecutorial authorities
- practical advantage from the Netherlands' role as host state for Europol and Eurojust

## IC Capabilities

- strong cross-border operational role in infrastructure disruption and dark-web investigations
- high English-capacity environment and mature liaison network
- documented international legal-assistance volume and overseas liaison presence
- recent official examples of direct Dutch-US joint action

## Key Operations and Cases

Dutch police material and operation pages in this repo show THTC participating in some of the most important recent multinational cybercrime actions, including botnet takedowns, ransomware disruption, and dark-web market investigations. Official 2025-2026 Dutch police material also frames international cooperation as essential because cybercrime does not stop at borders.

## Cooperation Track Record

The Dutch model is *highly likely* one of the strongest in Europe for operational cybercrime cooperation. Public Dutch police reporting indicates a high volume of international legal-assistance traffic, a substantial liaison-officer footprint abroad, and repeated participation in multinational operations with the United States and European partners.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- A fuller official account of THTC's internal structure and staffing would improve the page.
- The exact legal and operational boundary between THTC, prosecutors, and other Dutch agencies could be documented more precisely in a later pass.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Netherlands - Octopus Cybercrime Community country profile | Council of Europe | current | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/-/netherlands |
| 2 | Jaarcijfers LX: Internationale samenwerking verder verstevigd | Politie | 2026-01-22 | https://www.politie.nl/nieuws/2026/januari/22/11-jaarcijfers-lx-internationale-samenwerking-verder-verstevigd.html |

title: Nigeria Economic and Financial Crimes Commission
text:
## Summary

The **Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC)** is Nigeria's principal financial-crime enforcement agency in this corpus. Its cybercrime relevance is strongest in business email compromise (BEC), online scamming, and fraud cases where Nigerian-based actors are identified through foreign victim reporting, malware telemetry, or INTERPOL coordination.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

In [[bec-nigeria-2016]], INTERPOL described an EFCC-linked arrest in Port Harcourt tied to a global online-fraud network estimated at USD 60 million in losses. That case combined INTERPOL coordination, EFCC domestic enforcement, and private intelligence from Trend Micro and Fortinet FortiGuard.

In [[operation-killer-bee]], INTERPOL reported simultaneous Nigerian raids in Lagos and Benin City against suspects using Agent Tesla malware to support BEC and credential theft. The case again shows EFCC-relevant Nigerian enforcement as part of a wider international pipeline: private telemetry, INTERPOL's ASEAN Cybercrime Operations Desk, and foreign victim geographies across Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and North Africa.

## IC Relevance

EFCC is a key institutional node for Nigeria-origin online-fraud enforcement. Use this page when the question is domestic Nigerian enforcement capacity or Nigerian execution of foreign-developed leads. Use operation pages for victim geography, malware details, and prosecution outcomes.

## Source Coverage

The source set is solid for two named BEC/fraud operations but still light for EFCC's internal cyber organization. A direct EFCC institutional source would strengthen mandate and unit-level detail.

## References

|---|---|---|---|---|

title: Nigeria Police Force
text:
## Summary

Nigeria Police Force is the national police organization of Nigeria. In this wiki it is most visible through INTERPOL-supported operations against business-email-compromise and SilverTerrier/TMT actors.

## International Cooperation Role

Operation Falcon and Operation Delilah show the same cooperation model from two angles. INTERPOL and private-sector partners supplied intelligence and coordination, while Nigerian police executed domestic investigative and arrest steps in Lagos and related locations. This page should be used when the source names the national police body; use a more specific cybercrime-unit page only when the source identifies that unit directly.

title: Kripos
text:
## Summary

Kripos is retained as an organization node in the international cybercrime cooperation graph. The current mandate field records: Norway's National Criminal Investigation Service and parent structure for the national cybercrime center. Country or jurisdiction metadata is recorded as [[norway]]. This page is used for menu navigation, operation back-links, and partner analysis rather than as a complete institutional profile.

## Cooperation Graph Role

Linked operation records: No operation backlink is currently linked.. These links are the safest basis for interpreting this organization in the repository because they connect the organization to named enforcement actions, source records, and cooperation mechanisms already present in the corpus. title: Office of International Affairs
text:
## Summary

The **Office of International Affairs (OIA)** is the DOJ Criminal Division component that carries much of the United States' formal cross-border legal process in cybercrime matters. In this corpus, OIA is not usually the technical investigator; it is the legal-cooperation mechanism that makes foreign evidence collection, search requests, arrests, extraditions, and evidence sharing usable in US prosecutions.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The DOJ CCIPS source frames OIA as part of the US international cybercrime cooperation architecture, with OIA handling central-authority and MLAT functions while CCIPS provides cybercrime prosecutorial expertise. Operation records then show how that role appears in practice.

In [[qqaazz-money-laundering-takedown]], DOJ described OIA coordination for searches, arrests, extraditions, and evidence sharing across a transnational laundering case tied to high-level cybercriminal groups. In [[proxy-service-takedown-2026-03]], the SocksEscort disruption source lists DOJ OIA among the US support components for a coordinated action with Eurojust, Europol, DCIS, FBI, IRS-CI, and European partners.

## IC Relevance

Use OIA as the institutional node for formal US legal cooperation, especially when an operation involves extradition, foreign search-and-seizure work, MLAT evidence channels, or multiple foreign prosecutions. Keep investigative attribution on the lead agency pages such as [[fbi-cyber-division]], [[us-dcis]], or [[us-secret-service]].

## Source Coverage

Coverage is good for role and case examples. Direct DOJ OIA-specific public pages would improve exact mandate wording and current internal organization, but operation records already establish the practical cooperation function.

title: Central Bureau for Combating Cybercrime (CBZC) — Poland
text:
## Summary

The **Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości (CBZC — Central Bureau for Combating Cybercrime)** is Poland's national police cybercrime bureau, created by the law of **17 December 2021** and entering into force on **12 January 2022** [1][2]. CBZC is an organizational unit of the Polish Police with nationwide competence over crimes committed using IT systems, teleinformatic systems, or teleinformatic networks [1][3].

CBZC is headquartered at **ul. Wąwozowa 18 in Warsaw** [1] and is designed to ultimately employ **1,800 officers** by 2025, making it one of the largest dedicated national cybercrime police units in Europe by planned headcount [3]. It is *highly likely* Poland's primary cybercrime operational partner for Europol EC3 and major international cyber operations.

## Mandate and Authority

Under Polish law, CBZC is a service of the Police that performs tasks throughout Poland including [1][2]:

- Recognizing and combating crimes committed using IT systems, teleinformatic systems, or teleinformatic networks
- Preventing such crimes
- Detecting and prosecuting perpetrators
- Supporting other police units with cybercrime expertise

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- Nationwide headquarters in Warsaw with territorial branches
- Staffing ramp-up plan from inception to a cap of 1,800 positions by 2025 [3]
- First commander appointed **1 May 2022** by the Minister of Internal Affairs and Administration [1]

## IC Capabilities

- **Europol National Unit channel:** Poland is a Europol member; CBZC *almost certainly* serves as the cybercrime operational interface
- **Interpol NCB Warsaw channel**
- **Budapest Convention:** Poland is a party; CBZC *likely* holds or routes Art. 35 24/7 cooperation
- **EU Joint Investigation Teams (JITs):** Available through Eurojust coordination
- **Substantial headcount ambition:** 1,800-officer target suggests significant operational capacity when fully staffed [3]

## Key Operations and Cases

Specific operation attributions pending further source ingestion. Poland is *likely* to have participated in recent Europol-coordinated operations ([[operation-endgame-phase1|Operation Endgame]], etc.) though CBZC-specific attribution should be confirmed via individual Europol press releases.

## Cooperation Track Record

- **EU member state** since 2004 — standard Europol and Eurojust participation
- **NATO member** with cyber cooperation channels
- **Budapest Convention** party
- Regional cooperation with Baltic, Central European and Nordic partners

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Current actual headcount of CBZC (vs. 1,800 target)
- Named international operations in which CBZC has participated
- Division of labor between CBZC and other Polish cyber agencies (NASK, Military Counterintelligence Service)
- Whether CBZC holds the Polish Budapest Convention 24/7 contact point

## References

title: Polizia di Stato
text:
## Summary

**Polizia di Stato** is the Italian State Police institution that houses Italy's core cybercrime police service, [[italy-polizia-postale|Servizio Polizia postale e per la sicurezza cibernetica]]. Use this page for parent-institution attribution and broad State Police participation; use the Postal Police page when a source specifically discusses the cyber service or CNAIPIC.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Official Polizia di Stato material places the Postal Police cyber service within the State Police structure and provides a current 2024 organizational reference for that service. The 2017 Polizia di Stato Criptolocker source gives a concrete example of Italian cybercrime enforcement through the Postal Police line.

The State Police also appears in multinational action records. In [[doublevpn-takedown]], Europol listed the Italian State Police among participating agencies in the coordinated seizure of a VPN service used by ransomware actors. That makes the page useful for mapping Italian participation when the public source names the national police broadly rather than the Postal Police service alone.

## IC Relevance

For Italian cybercrime cooperation, Polizia di Stato is the parent law-enforcement institution. [[italy-polizia-postale]] should remain the canonical node for cyber unit detail, while this page preserves higher-level institutional context and broad operation attribution.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is good for the cyber service's placement and one multinational operation. Additional Italian official action-day sources would improve operation-specific attribution for older fraud and ransomware cases.

title: National Unit for Combating Cybercrime and Technological Crime (UNC3T)
text:
## Summary

The **Unidade Nacional de Combate ao Cibercrime e à Criminalidade Tecnológica (UNC3T)** is the specialized cybercrime unit of Portugal's **Polícia Judiciária (PJ)**. It was created by Decree-Law No. 81/2016 (28 November 2016), entering into operation in late 2016, replacing the earlier Unidade Nacional da Investigação da Criminalidade Informática (established August 2015). Its structure is *almost certainly* inspired by Europol's [[europol-ec3|EC3]] model, consolidating cybercrime-related competencies previously dispersed across multiple PJ units.

## Mandate and Authority

UNC3T is the PJ's operational unit for cybercrime prevention, investigation, and repression. It handles both cyber-dependent crimes (e.g., hacking, malware) and cyber-enabled crimes where technology is central. It is integrated into Portugal's broader law enforcement architecture and supports the Public Prosecution Service in cybercrime cases.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

UNC3T's design mirrors EC3's three-strand approach: operations, strategic analysis, and forensic/technical support. As the PJ is Portugal's criminal police responsible for serious and organized crime, UNC3T serves as Portugal's primary interface with Europol and INTERPOL on cybercrime matters.

## IC Capabilities

- **Europol integration:** Portugal's national hub for EC3 cooperation; participates in EMPACT Cyber-Attacks and Online Child Sexual Exploitation priorities (*likely*).
- **Budapest Convention 24/7 network:** Portugal is a party to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]; UNC3T/PJ serves as the operational contact point.
- **Digital forensics:** Concentrated forensic capabilities for digital evidence handling.

## Key Operations and Cases

No specific operations have been linked to this page yet. Portugal has participated in Europol-coordinated takedowns, though documented UNC3T involvement requires further source ingestion.

## Cooperation Track Record

UNC3T operates through Europol channels and the Budapest Convention 24/7 network. Portugal is a Council of Europe member and a long-standing Budapest Convention party.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Current staffing levels of UNC3T.
- Volume of incoming/outgoing MLA requests handled by UNC3T.
- Specific operational contributions to multinational takedowns.

## References

| 2 | Decree-Law No. 81/2016 | Diário da República | 2016-11-28 | https://www.pgdlisboa.pt/leis/lei_mostra_articulado.php?nid=1137 |
| 3 | "PJ com unidade de combate ao cibercrime" | Governo de Portugal | 2016-11-18 | https://www.portugal.gov.pt/pt/gc21/comunicacao/noticia?i=20161118-mj-pj |

title: Portugal Policia Judiciaria
text:
## Summary

**Policia Judiciaria (PJ)** is Portugal's criminal investigation police. For cybercrime analysis, its most important component is [[portugal-police|UNC3T]], the national unit for cybercrime and technological criminality.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The official UNC3T source describes the unit as the specialized PJ body for cybercrime and technological criminality. The Portugal government source records the 2016 reform that created the modern unit structure, replacing earlier computer-crime arrangements and consolidating cybercrime competencies inside PJ.

PJ appears in the corpus in both cyber-dependent and cyber-enabled investigations. The [[operation-jackal-iii|Operation Jackal III]] source gives a recent country-level example of PJ participation in an INTERPOL-supported anti-fraud operation, while [[franco-israeli-ceo-fraud]] shows PJ's role in asset-tracing and bank-account work tied to CEO fraud and BEC laundering.

## IC Relevance

Use this parent page for the Portuguese criminal-police institution and international cooperation posture. Use [[portugal-police|UNC3T]] for cyber unit details, technical capacity, and the national cybercrime contact point.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for the PJ-UNC3T relationship and one recent operation. Future work should link more Portugal-participated Europol and INTERPOL operations once sources name PJ or UNC3T explicitly.

title: Directorate for Investigating Organized Crime and Terrorism (DIICOT)
text:
## Summary

**DIICOT** (Direcția de Investigare a Infracțiunilor de Criminalitate Organizată și Terorism) is Romania's specialized prosecutorial directorate responsible for investigating and prosecuting organized crime, terrorism, and cybercrime. It was formed in 2004 under Law 508/2004 and operates under the jurisdiction of the Romanian Public Ministry, attached to the High Court of Cassation and Justice.

DIICOT is *highly likely* one of Eastern Europe's most active cybercrime prosecution bodies, closing 1,614 cybercrime cases in 2022 according to official statistics.

## Mandate and Authority

DIICOT operates through five specialized services: organized crime, drug trafficking, economic and financial crime, **cybercrime**, and terrorism-related offenses. It is led by a Chief Prosecutor nominated by the Minister of Justice and appointed by the President of Romania. DIICOT's Service for Combating Cybercrime is Romania's primary specialized cybercrime prosecutorial unit and serves as the main 24/7 point of contact under the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]].

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **Service for Combating Cybercrime** — specialized prosecution team for computer-related crime.
- **Central bureau (Bucharest)** and territorial services across Romania.
- Close operational partnership with the [[romania-police|Romanian National Police]] cybercrime directorate.

## IC Capabilities

- **Budapest Convention 24/7 primary contact point** for Romania.
- **Eurojust liaison:** Romania is an EU member state with a Eurojust national desk.
- **Joint Investigation Teams (JITs):** DIICOT regularly participates in JITs with EU counterparts.
- **US cooperation:** Long-standing cooperation with the FBI and US DOJ on Romanian cybercriminal networks.

## Key Operations and Cases

Romania and DIICOT have been involved in multiple joint operations targeting Romanian-based cybercriminal networks. DOJ press releases document joint DIICOT–US operations against Romanian organized groups (*confirmed*).

## Cooperation Track Record

- **Council of Europe Octopus community:** DIICOT is regularly cited as a "key player" in Romania's cybercrime response.
- **Eurojust / Europol:** Frequent participation in coordination meetings and JITs.
- **EPPO:** Romania is a participating EPPO state.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Relationship and division of labor between DIICOT cybercrime service and Romanian Police DCCO cybercrime unit.
- Current staffing of the cybercrime service.

## References

title: Romanian National Police — Service for Combating Cybercrime (DCCO)
text:
## Summary

The **Service for Combating Cybercrime** (Serviciul de Combatere a Criminalității Informatice) is the specialized cybercrime unit within the **Directorate for Countering Organized Criminality (DCCO)** of the **General Inspectorate of the Romanian Police (GIRP)**. It functions as Romania's principal police-level cybercrime investigative body, working alongside the prosecutorial [[romania-diicot|DIICOT]] cybercrime service.

Romania operates a **dual structure** for cybercrime: the prosecutorial Service for Combating Cybercrime within DIICOT as primary 24/7 contact, and the police-level service as the **secondary 24/7 contact** under the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]].

## Mandate and Authority

The unit has country-wide competence for prevention and investigation of computer-related crime. It is embedded within the DCCO, reflecting Romania's approach of treating cybercrime as an organized-crime priority.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **Central cybercrime service** in Bucharest.
- Territorial cybercrime structures in regional DCCO offices.
- Operational integration with DIICOT prosecutors for significant investigations.

## IC Capabilities

- **Budapest Convention secondary 24/7 contact:** Receives police-to-police requests and preservation orders.
- **Europol / EC3:** Romanian police officers are reportedly co-located and participate in Europol operations (*likely*).
- **INTERPOL NCB Bucharest:** Through the Romanian Police.
- **Bilateral cooperation with US FBI:** Well documented in joint operations against Romanian cybercriminal networks.

## Key Operations and Cases

Romania has cooperated on numerous joint US-Romania operations targeting Romanian fraudsters and cybercrime networks. Specific operational links require further source ingestion.

## Cooperation Track Record

Romania is regarded as a significant source country and cooperation partner in European cybercrime matters. Romanian police participate in Europol EMPACT Cyber operations and international training through Council of Europe programs (*likely*).

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Exact establishment date of the cybercrime service.
- Current staffing and specialization levels.
- Division of labor between this unit and DIICOT in practice.

## References

title: Romania Public Ministry
text:
## Summary

The **Romania Public Ministry** is the parent prosecution institution for [[romania-diicot|DIICOT]], Romania's specialized prosecutorial directorate for organized crime, terrorism, and cybercrime. This page exists to preserve parent-institution context, while DIICOT remains the operational cybercrime prosecution page.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The Council of Europe DIICOT source identifies DIICOT as a specialized directorate operating under the Romanian Public Ministry and attached to the High Court of Cassation and Justice. That makes the Public Ministry relevant whenever cybercrime cooperation requires prosecutorial authority, mutual legal assistance, or Eurojust-facing judicial coordination.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for Romanian prosecution-system hierarchy. Use [[romania-diicot]] for cybercrime prosecutorial capacity and [[romania-police]] for police-level investigation.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is sufficient for the Public Ministry-DIICOT relationship. Direct Public Ministry institutional sources would improve parent-level detail.

title: Royal Malaysia Police
text:
## Summary

The **Royal Malaysia Police (Polis Diraja Malaysia, PDRM)** is Malaysia's national police force. In the cybercrime corpus, its main operational entry is [[malaysia-police|PDRM Commercial Crime Investigation Department (CCID)]], which handles cybercrime and technology-enabled commercial crime.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The CCID source identifies PDRM's Commercial Crime Investigation Department as the leading department for commercial and cybercrime investigations, including fraud, criminal breach of trust, cybercrime, forgery, and related offences. Operation-level sources place Malaysia in regional cooperation settings: [[operation-cyber-guardian|Operation Cyber Guardian]] for online child sexual exploitation enforcement and [[operation-first-light-2024|Operation First Light 2024]] for online scam and money-laundering disruption.

## IC Relevance

Use this parent page when the source names Royal Malaysia Police or PDRM broadly. Use [[malaysia-police]] for CCID-specific cybercrime detail. For regional-policing context, link [[aseanapol]] and [[interpol]] alongside the Malaysian agency.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for CCID's cybercrime role and two regional-operation examples. It should later be improved with direct PDRM headquarters material if available in the source corpus.

title: Russian Ministry of Interior
text:
## Summary

The **Russian Ministry of Interior** page is currently anchored to a narrow but important historical cooperation record: the 2015 [[simda-botnet-takedown]]. The public source set names the Russian Ministry of Interior cybercrime division, commonly referred to as Department K, as a participant in that INTERPOL-coordinated botnet action.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The Simda source describes an INTERPOL IGCI-coordinated takedown of malware infrastructure affecting more than 770,000 computers across more than 190 countries. It lists law-enforcement partners from the Netherlands, United States, Russia, Luxembourg, and Poland, alongside Microsoft, Kaspersky, Trend Micro, and Japan's Cyber Defense Institute.

For this organization page, the important point is not broad present-day cooperation but the existence of a documented Russian MVD cybercrime-unit role in a multinational takedown before the later deterioration of Russia-West law-enforcement cooperation.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for historical Russia-inclusive cybercrime cooperation and for Simda-specific attribution. Do not generalize this record into a current cooperation baseline without fresh sources; the page is deliberately framed as historical and source-bound.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is adequate for the Simda role, with both INTERPOL and Kaspersky corroborating the public-private coordination model. It is not adequate for current Russian MVD structure or post-2022 cooperation posture.

## References

title: Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency (SMPA)
text:
> [!info] Status — Active (parent org structure noted)
> The **Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency (서울특별시경찰청, SMPA)** is active and not directly affected by the 2023 KNPA national-level reorganization that dissolved the standalone 경찰청 사이버수사국. SMPA's parent is the [[knpa|KNPA (Korea National Police Agency)]], not [[knpa-cyber-bureau]] directly — the prior `parent_org` wikilink has been corrected. SMPA underwent a **structural reorganization on 2023-10-30** into a 3-차장 체제 (Public Safety / Investigation / Everyday Life), 7 divisions, 26 departments, and 31 precincts [1][2].
>
> **Terminology note:** The academic and policy literature often refers to city/provincial "사이버수사대". The correct current terminology for the Korean national/metropolitan police is **사이버수사과 (Cyber Investigation Division)** under the metropolitan/provincial police 수사부, with precinct-level **사이버수사팀 (Cyber Investigation Team)** working beneath it — there is no formally-named "사이버수사대" in the current KNPA organization [3].

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Name:** 서울특별시경찰청 (Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency, SMPA) — unchanged
**Parent:** [[knpa|경찰청 (Korea National Police Agency)]]
**Headquarters:** Jongno-gu, Seoul
**Structure (post 2023-10-30):** 3 차장 (Deputy Commissioner for Public Safety / Investigation / Everyday Life), 7 divisions, 1 단, 2 담당관, 26 과, 15 직할대, 31 precincts [2]
**Cybercrime unit:** **수사부 사이버수사과 (Cyber Investigation Division under Investigation Bureau)**; cybercrime teams also exist at precinct level as **사이버수사팀** [3]
**Status:** **Active**

Cybercrime investigation at SMPA is coordinated nationally through the [[knpa-cyber-bureau|경찰청 수사국 사이버수사심의관]] (deputy-director-level unit within KNPA's National Investigation Headquarters since December 2023), not through SMPA directly. SMPA conducts operational Seoul-area investigations and feeds into the national cybercrime line.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 1945-10-21 | **서울시 경찰청** predecessor established; historic founding date of Korean metropolitan police in Seoul | [1] |
| 1991 | Modern 지방경찰청 (metropolitan/provincial police agency) system established under 경찰법 | [1] |
| 2021-01-01 | **자치경찰제 (local police system)** introduced; SMPA gains 자치경찰 functions alongside traditional 국가경찰 duties | [1] |
| 2023-08 | **Qingdao joint operation** with Chinese police — first significant Korea-China voice phishing joint operation; 16 suspects arrested | [[korea-china-voice-phishing-qingdao]] |
| 2023-10-30 | **Structural reorganization** into 3-차장 체제, 7 divisions, 26 departments, 31 precincts | [2] |
| 2023-12 | KNPA national-level reorganization dissolves 경찰청 사이버수사국; SMPA cybercrime unit continues to operate under the new 수사국 사이버수사심의관 coordination | [[knpa-cyber-bureau]] |

## Summary

The Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency (서울특별시경찰청, SMPA) is the metropolitan police agency responsible for law enforcement in the Seoul Special City. It is a subordinate regional command of the [[knpa|Korea National Police Agency (경찰청)]] and operates cybercrime investigation functions through its 수사부 사이버수사과 (Cyber Investigation Division) and precinct-level 사이버수사팀.

SMPA has conducted bilateral cooperation with Chinese police on voice phishing cases, most notably the [[korea-china-voice-phishing-qingdao|Qingdao joint operation]] (August 2023), which was the first significant Korea-China voice phishing roundup under bilateral cooperation arrangements. Seoul-area voice phishing cases frequently involve SMPA leadership in cooperation with Chinese or Southeast Asian counterparts, coordinated through the [[knpa-cyber-bureau|national cybercrime enforcement line]].

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | 서울특별시경찰청 (연혁·조직) | 나무위키 | n.d. | https://namu.wiki/w/%EC%84%9C%EC%9A%B8%ED%8A%B9%EB%B3%84%EC%8B%9C%EA%B2%BD%EC%B0%B0%EC%B2%AD |
| [2] | 서울경찰청 공식 사이트 | 서울특별시경찰청 | n.d. | https://www.smpa.go.kr/user/nd48138.do |
| [3] | 사이버수사대 (정확한 현재 명칭: 사이버수사과/사이버수사팀)

title: Shadowserver
text:
## Summary

Shadowserver is represented as a nonprofit technical partner supporting botnet, malware, proxy, and abuse-infrastructure disruption. It is not a law-enforcement body; its role is technical measurement, sinkhole support, abuse reporting, and victim-notification data.

## International Cooperation Role

Avalanche, GozNym, and malicious proxy-service records show why a neutral technical partner can matter in cross-border operations. Public authorities may obtain the legal authority for seizures or arrests, while organizations such as Shadowserver help transform seized or sinkholed infrastructure into actionable remediation data. Use this page when the record emphasizes technical disruption or post-takedown notification rather than prosecution.

title: Cyber Security Agency of Singapore
text:
## Summary

The **Cyber Security Agency of Singapore (CSA)** is Singapore's lead national cybersecurity agency. Established on **1 April 2015**, CSA is responsible for protecting Singapore's cyberspace, operating SingCERT, regulating Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) under the [[cybersecurity-act-2018|Cybersecurity Act 2018]], licensing cybersecurity service providers, and coordinating Singapore's response to major cyber incidents.

CSA is distinct from the [[singapore-police|Singapore Police Force Cybercrime Command]]: CSA handles **defensive cybersecurity, CII regulation, and incident response**, while SPF Cybercrime Command handles **criminal investigation and prosecution**. The two agencies coordinate closely on incidents that have both incident-response and criminal dimensions.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** Cyber Security Agency of Singapore (CSA)
**Commissioner and Chief Executive:** **David Koh** (Commissioner of Cyber Security) [1][4]
**Parent:** Ministry of Digital Development and Information (MDDI) — operationally managed by MDDI, with statutory authority under the Prime Minister's Office [2][3]
**Status:** *Active*

CSA remains Singapore's lead national cybersecurity agency as of April 2026. Notable recent developments:

- **July 2025 UNC3886 incident response:** CSA mobilised **over 100 cyber defenders** in response to cyberattacks on Singapore's critical infrastructure attributed to **UNC3886**, a cyber-espionage group. The incident affected **Singtel, StarHub, M1 and Simba** networks and was described by Minister K Shanmugam as Singapore's **largest coordinated cyber incident response to date** [4]. CSA's Commissioner David Koh characterized cyber stability as "a necessity, not a luxury" following the incident [4].
- **Organizational placement:** CSA is administratively a government agency under the Prime Minister's Office, but is managed by the **Ministry of Digital Development and Information (MDDI)**, which took over the management role from the former Ministry of Communications and Information (MCI) when MDDI was created in July 2024 [2][3].
- **Continuity of leadership:** David Koh continues to serve as Commissioner of Cyber Security and Chief Executive [1][4].

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2015-04-01 | **Cyber Security Agency (CSA)** established as Singapore's national cybersecurity coordination body | [2] |
| 2018 | **Cybersecurity Act 2018** enacted — CSA becomes the CII regulator and licenses cybersecurity service providers | — |
| 2024-07 | **MDDI** created; management of CSA transfers from MCI to MDDI | [3] |
| 2025-07 | CSA leads Singapore's largest coordinated cyber incident response against the **UNC3886** APT campaign targeting Singtel, StarHub, M1, Simba | [4] |

## Mandate and Authority

CSA's mandate and statutory powers include:

- **Cybersecurity Act 2018:** CSA is the regulator of Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) sectors (energy, water, banking, healthcare, transport, infocomm, media, security/emergency services, government, aviation, maritime).
- **CII owner obligations:** Licensed CII owners must report cybersecurity incidents to CSA, conduct audits, and comply with CSA-issued codes of practice.
- **Cybersecurity service provider licensing:** CSA licenses penetration testing and managed security operations centre (SOC) providers.
- **National incident response:** CSA operates SingCERT and coordinates national response to major cyber incidents.
- **International cooperation:** CSA represents Singapore in international cybersecurity fora and engages in capacity building under ASEAN frameworks.

## IC Capabilities

Because CSA is a **defensive/regulatory agency**, its international cooperation profile differs from law-enforcement agencies:

- **CERT-to-CERT cooperation:** SingCERT cooperates with other national CERTs through FIRST (Forum of Incident Response and Security Teams) and APCERT.
- **ASEAN

title: Singapore Police Force — Cybercrime Command
text:
## Summary

The Cybercrime Command of the [[singapore-police|Singapore Police Force]] (SPF) was established in **June 2015** within the Criminal Investigation Department (CID) to build up anti-cybercrime capabilities, with a focus on cyber investigation, digital forensics, and cybercrime policy. By the end of 2015, specialist investigators and digital forensic officers were deployed to each of Singapore's six Land Divisions.

Singapore's Cybercrime Command benefits from the city-state's unique position as a regional financial and technology hub and as host of the [[interpol-igci|INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI)]] in Singapore — which places major INTERPOL cybercrime operations geographically adjacent to SPF headquarters.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** Singapore Police Force — Cybercrime Command (CCC), Criminal Investigation Department (CID)
**Status:** *Active* — CCC continues to operate under the same name and within CID [2][6][7]
**Parent:** Singapore Police Force → Criminal Investigation Department

As of April 2026 the SPF Cybercrime Command remains the lead SPF unit for cybercrime investigation. Recent developments since 2024:

- **National Service Cybercrime Operators (NSCO):** Launched in 2024, NSCO is a new Police National Service Full-time (PNSF) vocation. NSCO officers are deployed to SPF frontline units including the **Anti-Scam Command (ASCom)** and the **Cybercrime Command (CCC) Threat Intelligence Centre** [4][6]. This represents a meaningful expansion of the CCC's workforce capacity through reservist-style deployment.
- **ASEAN Cybercrime Conference 2025:** From **28–30 October 2025** SPF hosted the ASEAN Cybercrime Conference (ACC) 2025, organized by the Cybercrime Division of CID with support from the Commercial Affairs Department and the Online Criminal Harms Act Office [7]. Singapore continues to serve as a Southeast Asian hub for cybercrime cooperation.
- **Anti-Scam Command (ASCom):** ASCom has evolved from the earlier Anti-Scam Centre (ASC) and continues to operate in tandem with the CCC on rapid-response scam investigations, with strong bank integration for transaction freezing.
- **Online Criminal Harms Act (OCHA):** Since its 2023 enactment and 2024 phased commencement, OCHA has provided CCC with enhanced powers to direct online service providers and providers of online criminal content. The dedicated OCHA Office operates alongside CCC within SPF.

The CCC structure (HQ + Cyber Investigation Branch + Digital Forensics Branch + Cybercrime Policy Branch + Land Division officers) remains intact through 2025–2026, with NSCO augmentation providing additional capacity.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2015-06 | **Cybercrime Command** established within CID | [1][2] |
| 2015-12 | Specialist investigators and digital forensic officers deployed to all six Land Divisions | [2] |
| 2017 | SPF launches public-private alliance with private cyber threat intelligence firms | [1] |
| 2023 | **Online Criminal Harms Act (OCHA)** enacted | — |
| 2024 | **National Service Cybercrime Operator (NSCO)** vocation introduced; NSCOs deployed to ASCom and CCC Threat Intelligence Centre | [4][6] |
| 2025-10 | SPF hosts **ASEAN Cybercrime Conference 2025** in Singapore | [7] |

## Mandate and Authority

SPF's Cybercrime Command derives authority from Singapore's:

- **Computer Misuse Act 1993** (amended numerous times; last major update 2017)
- **Cybersecurity Act 2018**
- **Penal Code**
- **Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (Confiscation of Benefits) Act**

Singapore is *not* a party to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] but maintains strong informal and bilateral cybercrime cooperation.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **Cybercrime Command HQ:** Central investigation and policy unit within CID.
- **Cyber Investigation Branch:** Case investigators.
- **Digital Forensics Branch:** Computer and mobile f

title: The Hawks
text:
## Summary

**The Hawks**, formally the Directorate for Priority Crime Investigation (DPCI), are the SAPS specialized unit for priority crime. In the cybercrime corpus, this page should be used where sources identify serious cybercrime, organized crime, or economic-crime investigation by DPCI.

## Mandate and Authority

The official SAPS/DPCI source identifies the unit's serious-crime mandate. The Council of Europe country profile provides broader South African cybercrime legal and institutional context.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Specific serious-cybercrime operation examples should be added when primary South African sources are available.
- The boundary between Hawks, SAPS cyber units, prosecutors, and CSIRT functions remains under-sourced.

## References

title: Guardia Civil - Grupo de Delitos Telematicos (GDT)
text:
## Summary

The **Grupo de Delitos Telematicos (GDT)** is the Guardia Civil's specialized cybercrime unit and one of the two main national cybercrime actors in Spain's dual law-enforcement model.

## Mandate and Authority

Official Guardia Civil material presents the GDT as its specialized unit for cybercrime and internet-related offences. The Council of Europe country profile on Spain also confirms that Spain's cybercrime structure relies on specialized units in both the **Guardia Civil** and the **Cuerpo Nacional de Policia**.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- specialized cybercrime capability inside the Guardia Civil
- part of Spain's dual national cybercrime model together with the National Police
- role in cyber-enabled crime investigation, prevention, and citizen-facing reporting

## IC Capabilities

- treaty-based cooperation under Spain's Budapest Convention framework
- participation in Spain's 24/7 contact-point and central-authority environment
- practical value in cross-border fraud, malware, and internet-enabled crime cases

## Division of Labor Within Spain

The public record does not present Spain's cybercrime model as a simple hierarchy with one lead force and one support force. Instead, the Council of Europe profile says that **both police forces share competence** and that incoming information can be sent through either 24/7 point of contact, with case allocation handled later under agreed internal rules.

That makes the GDT important in two ways. First, it is an operational cybercrime unit in its own right. Second, it is part of Spain's institutional redundancy for expedited cooperation, rather than just a domestic reporting channel subordinate to the National Police.

## Key Operations and Cases

Open-source institutional detail remains limited, but the GDT is a recognizable public-facing Spanish cybercrime brand and is structurally positioned to contribute to multinational Europol- and Budapest-linked cooperation activity.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- More direct official material on GDT staffing, structure, and named multinational operations would improve this page.
- Spain's exact internal allocation criteria between the GDT and the National Police BCIT remain only partly public.
