title: AlphaBay, the Largest Online “Dark Market,” Shut Down
text:
## Source Summary

On 20 July 2017, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of California announced the shutdown of AlphaBay and disclosed that Alexandre Cazes, the alleged creator and administrator of the marketplace, had already been charged by indictment in Fresno federal court on 1 June 2017. DOJ stated that the indictment charged racketeering conspiracy, narcotics distribution conspiracy and substantive distribution counts, identity-theft conspiracy, access-device-fraud conspiracy, device-making-equipment trafficking, and money-laundering conspiracy. DOJ also stated that Thai authorities arrested Cazes on 5 July 2017 on behalf of the United States, and that a separate civil forfeiture complaint was filed on 19 July 2017 against assets tied to Cazes and his wife in Thailand, Cyprus, Liechtenstein, and Antigua and Barbuda. Millions of dollars in cryptocurrency and luxury real estate were targeted as forfeitable AlphaBay proceeds. The prosecution did not proceed to adjudication because Cazes died in Thai custody on 12 July 2017 before extradition. Even so, the case remains central to international-cooperation analysis because it tied together a U.S. indictment, a Thailand arrest, cross-border asset preservation, and operational support from European and Canadian partners.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2017-07-20_edca_cazes-alphabay-indictment.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Andromeda Botnet Dismantled in International Cyber Operation
text:
## Source Summary

In late November to early December 2017, an international law enforcement operation dismantled the Andromeda botnet infrastructure. The FBI, German Central Criminal Investigation Office in Luneburg, Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3), the Joint Cybercrime Action Task Force (J-CAT), and Eurojust coordinated with 17 countries and private sector partners including Microsoft to neutralize the botnet. One suspect was arrested in Belarus. The operation disrupted servers and domains used for malware distribution through the Andromeda malware family.

title: Thirty-Six Defendants Indicted For Alleged Roles In Transnational Criminal Organization Responsible For More Than $530 Million In Losses From Cybercrimes
text:
## Source Summary

On 7 February 2018, a nine-count superseding indictment was unsealed by a Las Vegas, Nevada, grand jury charging 36 defendants for their alleged roles in the Infraud Organization, a transnational cybercrime enterprise that caused more than USD 530 million in actual losses and intended losses exceeding USD 2.2 billion. The Infraud Organization was created in October 2010 by Svyatoslav Bondarenko (aka "Obnon," "Rector," "Helkern"), 34, of Ukraine, as the premier destination for "carding" — purchasing retail items with counterfeit or stolen credit card information. Operating under the slogan "In Fraud We Trust," the organization had 10,901 registered members at its peak. Key defendants included: Sergey Medvedev (aka "Stells," "segmed"), co-founder and administrator, of Russia; and 34 additional defendants from multiple countries. Charges included racketeering conspiracy (RICO), identity theft, wire fraud, and computer fraud.

## Relevance to IC

title: Mastermind Behind EUR 1 Billion Cyber Bank Robbery Arrested in Spain

## Source Summary

The Spanish National Police, in cooperation with Europol, the FBI, Romanian police, Belarusian police, and Taiwanese authorities, arrested the mastermind behind the Carbanak and Cobalt malware campaigns in Spain. The suspect led a cybercrime group that had used Carbanak, Cobalt, and Anunak malware to steal over EUR 1 billion from more than 100 financial institutions across 40+ countries since 2013. Private cybersecurity firms including Kaspersky contributed to the investigation.

title: Irish Man Who Helped Operate The “Silk Road” Website Sentenced In Manhattan Federal Court To Over Six Years In Prison
text:
## Source Summary

The Silk Road takedown was the seizure and shutdown of the pioneering dark web drug marketplace on 2 October 2013, accompanied by the arrest of its creator and operator, Ross William Ulbricht (alias "Dread Pirate Roberts" or "DPR"), by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] in San Francisco. Silk Road, which operated on the Tor anonymity network from approximately February 2011, had facilitated an estimated USD 1.2 billion in illegal transactions -- primarily narcotics -- serving over 100,000 buyers through thousands of vendors worldwide. The FBI seized approximately 173,991 bitcoins (then worth ~$33.6 million) and the Silk Road server infrastructure hosted in Iceland.

title: Treasury Sanctions Evil Corp, the Russia-Based Cybercriminal Group Behind Dridex Malware
text:
## Source Summary

The Dridex/Evil Corp disruption and prosecution was a **multi-year international law enforcement operation** targeting the Dridex banking trojan (also known as Bugat and Cridex) and its operators within the Russia-based cybercriminal organization **Evil Corp**. The operation unfolded in two major phases: (1) the **October 2015 technical disruption and arrest** of botnet administrator Andrey Ghinkul, conducted jointly by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] and [[uk-nca|UK National Crime Agency (NCA)]]; and (2) the **December 2019 indictment, sanctions, and reward** targeting Evil Corp leader **Maksim Yakubets** and deputy **Igor Turashev**, coordinated between the [[us-doj|US DOJ]], [[us-treasury|US Treasury (OFAC)]], and NCA.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2019-12-05_home-treasury-gov_treasury-sanctions-evil-corp-the-russia-based-cybercriminal-group-behind-dridex.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Cyber Command, Microsoft take action against Trickbot
text:
## Source Summary

The TrickBot disruption was a multi-pronged effort in October 2020 involving both U.S. military cyber operations and a private-sector-led legal action to disable one of the world's largest and most persistent botnets. U.S. Cyber Command conducted offensive operations to interfere with TrickBot's command-and-control (C2) infrastructure, while Microsoft, acting under a court order from the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia, took down 120 of 128 identified TrickBot servers in the first week. The urgency was driven by concerns that TrickBot-deployed ransomware could disrupt U.S. election infrastructure ahead of the November 2020 presidential election. While the initial disruption was temporary — TrickBot operators eventually rebuilt portions of their infrastructure — the operation was followed by a sustained law enforcement campaign that produced arrests and indictments through 2024, including the arrest of developer Vladimir Dunaev in [[south-korea|South Korea]] and the indictment of nine Russian nationals.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2020-10-12_cyberscoop-com_cyber-command-microsoft-take-action-against-trickbot.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: OFFICIALS ANNOUNCE INTERNATIONAL OPERATION TARGETING TRANSNATIONAL CRIMINAL ORGANIZATION QQAAZZ THAT PROVIDED MONEY LAUNDERING SERVICES TO HIGH-LEVEL CYBERCRIMINALS
text:
## Source Summary

On 15 October 2020, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Western District of Pennsylvania announced the unsealing of an indictment charging 14 members of the transnational QQAAZZ organization. DOJ said a related 2019 indictment had charged five additional QQAAZZ members and that one more conspirator had previously been arrested by criminal complaint in the United States, bringing the total number of charged defendants to 20. DOJ described QQAAZZ as a professional money-laundering network that provided services to high-level cybercriminal groups, including actors connected to malware families such as Dridex, Trickbot, and GozNym. According to the press release and indictment summary, QQAAZZ maintained hundreds of bank accounts and shell companies across multiple countries, laundered tens of millions of dollars in stolen funds, and advertised itself on Russian-speaking cybercrime forums as a global cash-out service. The case is notable because it combined a U.S. indictment with parallel prosecutions and searches in Europe. DOJ reported more than 40 house searches in Latvia, Bulgaria, the United Kingdom, Spain, and Italy, and said prosecutions were initiated in the United States, Portugal, Spain, and the United Kingdom. The Office of International Affairs coordinated searches, arrests, extraditions, and evidence sharing.

title: An update on disruption of Trickbot
text:
## Source Summary

The TrickBot disruption was a multi-pronged effort in October 2020 involving both U.S. military cyber operations and a private-sector-led legal action to disable one of the world's largest and most persistent botnets. U.S. Cyber Command conducted offensive operations to interfere with TrickBot's command-and-control (C2) infrastructure, while Microsoft, acting under a court order from the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia, took down 120 of 128 identified TrickBot servers in the first week. The urgency was driven by concerns that TrickBot-deployed ransomware could disrupt U.S. election infrastructure ahead of the November 2020 presidential election. While the initial disruption was temporary — TrickBot operators eventually rebuilt portions of their infrastructure — the operation was followed by a sustained law enforcement campaign that produced arrests and indictments through 2024, including the arrest of developer Vladimir Dunaev in [[south-korea|South Korea]] and the indictment of nine Russian nationals.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2020-10-20_blogs-microsoft-com_an-update-on-disruption-of-trickbot.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: DarkMarket: world's largest illegal dark web marketplace taken down
text:
## Source Summary

DarkMarket was, at the time of its shutdown on 11 January 2021, the world's largest illegal darknet marketplace by user count. The takedown was led by the cybercrime unit of the Koblenz Public Prosecutor's Office and [[germany-bka|Germany's Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA)]], with coordination through [[europol-ec3|Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]]. The operation resulted in the arrest of a 34-year-old Australian national near the German-Danish border, the seizure of more than 20 servers in [[moldova|Moldova]] and [[ukraine|Ukraine]], and the recovery of intelligence on approximately 500,000 users and 2,400 vendors. The intelligence gathered from DarkMarket's infrastructure directly enabled [[operation-dark-huntor|Operation Dark HunTOR]] nine months later, which produced 150 arrests across nine countries.

## Relevance to IC

title: Emotet Botnet Disrupted in International Cyber Operation
text:
## Source Summary

On 27 January 2021, the Department of Justice announced the disruption of the Emotet botnet, described by Europol as "the world's most dangerous malware," in an internationally coordinated operation known as Operation LadyBird. ### Legal Action (US Component) The FBI obtained a court-authorized operation in the Middle District of North Carolina. Foreign law enforcement, working in collaboration with the FBI, replaced Emotet malware on servers in their jurisdictions with a law enforcement-created file designed to sever communication between infected computers and the botnet's operators. The remediation ran from approximately 25 January through 25 April 2021.

Official FBI and Europol statements describe it as the largest law-enforcement operation ever mounted against encrypted criminal communications.

title: FBI’s Encrypted Phone Platform Infiltrated Hundreds of Criminal Syndicates; Result is Massive Worldwide Takedown
text:
## Source Summary

On 8 June 2021, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Southern District of California announced the unsealing of a federal indictment charging 17 foreign nationals with distributing ANOM encrypted devices and services to criminal syndicates worldwide. DOJ described the indictment as the charging core of Operation Trojan Shield, the FBI-led operation in which the Bureau secretly operated the ANOM platform while collecting more than 27 million messages. According to DOJ, the defendants were charged with racketeering conspiracy for marketing and distributing thousands of encrypted communication devices to transnational criminal organizations. The same announcement linked the indictment to more than 500 arrests during the initial takedown wave and to coordinated enforcement activity across Australia, Europe, and other regions. Subsequent DOJ releases show that the criminal case matured into guilty pleas and sentencings after extraditions from multiple countries. The case is therefore especially important for international-cooperation analysis because the underlying ANOM collection depended on an international cooperation agreement with a third country for message collection and on later extradition pipelines from countries including Colombia, Finland, and the Netherlands.

## Relevance to IC

title: Ukrainian Cyber Criminal Extradited For Decrypting The Credentials Of Thousands Of Computers Across The World And Selling Them On A Dark Web Website
text:
## Source Summary

On 8 September 2021, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the extradition of Glib Oleksandr Ivanov-Tolpintsev from Poland. DOJ said he was charged with conspiracy, trafficking in unauthorized access devices, and trafficking in computer passwords in a case tied to the xDedic Marketplace. According to DOJ, Ivanov-Tolpintsev used automated tools to decrypt the credentials of thousands of compromised computers around the world and sold those credentials on a dark-web marketplace. The later sentencing announcement stated that he received four years in prison and forfeited USD 82,648. The case is useful because it shows that xDedic enforcement extended to credential suppliers who monetized access at scale and were extradited from third countries.

## Relevance to IC

title: Four Men Federally Charged In Transnational Wire Fraud And Identity Theft Conspiracy
text:
## Source Summary

On 15 September 2021, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the unsealing of two indictments charging Olufemi Odedeyi, Ibrahim Davies, Adetunji Adejumo, and Ibrahim Jinadu with conspiracy to commit wire fraud, theft of public money or property, and aggravated identity theft. DOJ's later xDedic roundup linked this tax-fraud conspiracy to access and PII obtained from xDedic-compromised servers. DOJ said Odedeyi and Davies were apprehended in London by the United Kingdom's National Extradition Unit and detained pending extradition to the United States. The scheme allegedly involved unauthorized access to U.S. business servers, theft of U.S. residents' identifying information, and false tax filings seeking millions of dollars in refunds. The case is important because it shows the U.K. extradition side of the xDedic-enabled tax-fraud pipeline, complementing the later U.S.-based sentencing of Adejumo and Jinadu.

## Relevance to IC

title: Department of Justice Announces Results of Operation Dark HunTor
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Dark HunTOR was a coordinated international law enforcement action announced on 26 October 2021 that resulted in the arrest of 150 suspected dark web drug traffickers across nine countries. The operation seized approximately EUR 26.7 million (~USD 31 million) in cash and cryptocurrency, 234 kg of drugs, and 45 firearms. Coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]] and [[eurojust|Eurojust]], Dark HunTOR is almost certainly the largest follow-on enforcement action stemming from a single dark web marketplace seizure, building directly on the January 2021 takedown of [[darkmarket-takedown|DarkMarket]].

## Relevance to IC

title: International effort takes down VPN service, VPNLab, used for criminal activity
text:
## Source Summary

On 17 January 2022, law enforcement authorities from 10 countries, coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]] and supported by [[eurojust|Eurojust]], seized or disrupted the 15 servers hosting VPNLab.net, a [[bulletproof-hosting|bulletproof VPN service]] that had been used by cybercriminals to facilitate ransomware deployment, malware distribution, and other illicit activities since 2008. The operation was led by [[germany-lka|Hannover Police Department (Polizeidirektion Hannover)]] under the EMPACT security framework priority on "Cybercrime — Attacks Against Information Systems." The service had been linked to at least 150 ransomware attacks causing financial damages of at least EUR 60 million. No arrests were made during the action day, but seized customer data was expected to support follow-on investigations.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2022-01-18_cyberscoop-com_international-effort-takes-down-vpn-service-vpnlab-used-for-criminal-activity.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Russian National Indicted in East Texas for Cyber Hacking Enterprise
text:
## Source Summary

On 22 March 2022, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of Texas announced that a federal grand jury had returned an indictment against Igor Dekhtyarchuk, a Russian national, for operating "Marketplace A," a cybercriminal carding shop that allegedly sold thousands of stolen login credentials, personally identifiable information, and authentication tools. The indictment was returned on 16 March 2022. According to the indictment, Marketplace A claimed to have sold access to more than 48,000 compromised email accounts and more than 39,000 compromised online accounts while averaging roughly 5,000 daily visitors. The FBI's online covert employee made 13 undercover purchases between March and July 2021 from the Eastern District of Texas, obtaining access devices for 131 victim accounts. The case is highly relevant for international-cooperation analysis because the investigation reportedly involved the FBI Legal Attaché office in Riga and the State Police of Latvia, which likely reflects a mix of formal and informal cross-border investigative support even though the public release does not specify the precise legal mechanism.

## Relevance to IC

title: Operation HAECHI III - INTERPOL Arrested 1000 Cyber Criminals & Seized $130 Million
text:
## Source Summary

Operation HAECHI-III was the third in INTERPOL's annual HAECHI series of operations targeting cyber-enabled financial crime. Running from **28 June to 23 November 2022**, it was coordinated by INTERPOL's Financial Crime and Anti-Corruption Centre (IFCACC) and involved **30 countries** acting through their respective INTERPOL National Central Bureaus (NCBs). The operation resulted in **975 arrests**, the resolution of **1,600+ cases**, and the seizure/interception of approximately **USD 130 million** in virtual assets and illicit funds.

title: Moldovan National And Technical Mastermind Of xDedic Marketplace Extradited From Spain
text:
## Source Summary

On 22 November 2022, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the arrest and extradition from Spain of Alexandru Habasescu, whom DOJ described as the lead developer and technical mastermind of the xDedic Marketplace. DOJ said Habasescu was charged in a superseding indictment with conspiracy to commit access-device fraud and substantive access-device-fraud counts. The public xDedic materials connect this case directly to the January 2019 marketplace seizure. DOJ described xDedic as a dark-web platform that sold compromised server credentials and U.S. residents' personal information and said the site offered more than 700,000 compromised credentials, including at least 150,000 in the United States. According to the Department, the investigation involved cooperation with Belgium, Ukraine, Europol, the Dutch National Police, Germany's BKA, and Spain for the extradition phase. DOJ's later victim-services page states that Habasescu was ultimately sentenced to 41 months' imprisonment. The case therefore serves as a strong judicial anchor for the xDedic takedown because it links the marketplace seizure to a named administrator, extradition, conviction, and sentence.

## Relevance to IC

title: Nigerian National Sentenced To More Than Six Years In Federal Prison For International Tax Fraud Scheme
text:
## Source Summary

On 5 December 2022, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the sentencing of Allen Levinson, formerly known as Allen Ameh, to 78 months in prison for wire-fraud conspiracy tied to an international tax-fraud scheme. DOJ's later xDedic summary identified Levinson as a prolific xDedic Marketplace buyer. According to DOJ, Levinson purchased access to U.S.-based Certified Public Accounting firms through xDedic and used the information obtained from those servers to file hundreds of false tax returns. The scheme attempted to obtain more than USD 63 million in fraudulent refunds, and the IRS actually paid more than USD 5.5 million. Levinson was arrested in the United Kingdom in December 2020, extradited to the United States, and pleaded guilty in February 2022. The case is a strong example of how xDedic access sales translated into downstream tax and identity fraud prosecuted in U.S. federal court.

## Relevance to IC

title: Franco-Israeli gang behind EUR 38 million CEO fraud busted
text:
## Source Summary

Europol supported the dismantling of a Franco-Israeli criminal network that employed business email compromise (BEC) and CEO fraud attacks to divert corporate payments to accounts under the threat actors' control. In one case, the fraudsters stole approximately EUR 38 million from a single company within days. The investigation, led by France, involved five action days between January 2022 and January 2023 across France and Israel. Eight suspects were arrested (six in France, two in Israel), including the group leader based in Israel. The criminal proceeds were laundered through a pre-existing money laundering scheme involving multiple bank accounts in the EU, China, and Israel. Authorities seized approximately EUR 5.5 million in total across multiple countries. - **Operation name:** (Unnamed; Franco-Israeli CEO Fraud investigation) - **Date:** Action days between January 2022 and January 2023

title: Russian Malware Developer Arrested And Extradited To The United States
text:
## Source Summary

On 22 February 2023, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the arrest and extradition of Russian national Dariy Pankov from the Republic of Georgia. DOJ said Pankov was charged with conspiracy, access-device fraud, and computer fraud in connection with his role in the xDedic Marketplace ecosystem. According to DOJ, Pankov developed a malware tool called `NLBrute`, which decrypted login credentials and enabled access to tens of thousands of compromised computers worldwide. He then sold those credentials, including through the xDedic Marketplace, and allegedly generated more than USD 358,000 in criminal proceeds. The case is important because it links the xDedic operation not only to administrators but also to a major supplier-side actor. DOJ later reported that Pankov pleaded guilty and was sentenced to 60 months' imprisonment.

## Relevance to IC

title: Foreign National Sentenced for Victimizing U.S. Persons Through Cyber-Enabled Fraud Schemes
text:
## Source Summary

On 27 March 2023, the Department of Justice announced that Solomon Ekunke Okpe, a Nigerian national, had been sentenced to four years and one month in prison for his role in a long-running cyber-enabled fraud conspiracy. DOJ also noted that co-conspirator Johnson Uke Obogo had been sentenced one week earlier, on 20 March 2023, to one year and one day in prison in the same District of Arizona case. According to court documents summarized by DOJ, the conspiracy ran from December 2011 to January 2017 and used a mix of business email compromise, work-from-home fraud, check-cashing fraud, romance scams, and credit-card fraud to target victims in Arizona and elsewhere in the United States. DOJ stated that the scheme was intended to cause more than USD 1 million in losses. The case is particularly useful for international-cooperation analysis because Okpe and Obogo were arrested abroad at U.S. request, in Malaysia and the United Kingdom respectively, and extradited to Arizona in 2022. DOJ further stated that the Office of International Affairs provided significant assistance throughout the investigation and extradition process, making the international dimension explicit rather than merely inferred.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2023-03-27_doj_okpe-obogo-bec-sentencing.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Jackal from 15 to 29 May 2023, mobilizing law enforcement across 21 countries against Black Axe and similar West African organized crime groups responsible for the majority of the world's cyber-enabled financial fraud. 103 arrests, 1,110 suspects identified, 208 bank accounts blocked, EUR 2.15 million seized/frozen.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation Jackal
- **Timeframe:** 15-29 May 2023
- **Countries:** 21 (Argentina, Australia, Belgium, Brazil, Canada, Cote d'Ivoire, France, Germany, Indonesia, Ireland, Italy, Malaysia, Netherlands, Nigeria, Portugal, South Africa, Spain, Switzerland, UAE, UK, US)
- **Arrests:** 103
- **Suspects identified:** 1,110
- **Bank accounts blocked:** 208
- **Assets seized:** EUR 2.15 million
- **Crime types:** BEC, romance scams, money laundering, credit card fraud, advance payment scams
- **Target:** Black Axe and similar West African organized crime groups

## Relevance

First dedicated INTERPOL operation targeting Black Axe. 21-country scope across five continents demonstrates global nature of BEC enforcement.

title: Closing ranks on West African organized crime: more than EUR 2 million seized in Operation Jackal
text:
## Source Summary

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Jackal from 15 to 29 May 2023, mobilizing police forces, financial crime units, and cybercrime agencies across 21 countries to deliver a targeted strike against Black Axe and similar West African organized crime groups. The operation resulted in 103 arrests, the identification of 1,110 suspects, the blocking of 208 bank accounts, and the seizure or freezing of EUR 2.15 million. Black Axe is a violent mafia-style gang renowned for cyber-enabled financial fraud, in particular business email compromise (BEC) schemes, romance scams, inheritance scams, credit card fraud, tax fraud, advance payment scams, and money laundering. - **Operation name:** Operation Jackal - **Date:** 15-29 May 2023

## Relevance to IC

title: Myanmar Rebel Offensive Helps China's Cybercrime Crackdown
text:
## Source Summary

The Myanmar-Kokang Cyber Scam Compound Crackdown was a Chinese-led cross-border law enforcement campaign, beginning in September 2023, aimed at dismantling massive cyber fraud operations based in the Kokang Self-Administered Zone (SAZ) of northern Myanmar's Shan State. The crackdown was catalyzed by China's Ministry of Public Security (MPS) issuing arrest warrants for scam kingpins and was dramatically accelerated by **Operation 1027** — a military offensive launched on October 27, 2023 by the Three Brotherhood Alliance (a coalition of ethnic armed organizations including the MNDAA) that physically overran the scam compound territories.

title: U.S.-Based Conspirators Sentenced To Prison For International Tax Scheme
text:
## Source Summary

On 29 November 2023, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the sentencing of Adetunji Adejumo and Ibrahim Jinadu for conspiracy to commit wire fraud in an international tax-fraud scheme. DOJ's xDedic roundup later linked this case to access and PII obtained from xDedic-compromised servers. According to DOJ, international conspirators obtained unauthorized access to U.S. business servers, stole U.S. residents' identifying information, and used that information to file false tax forms seeking millions of dollars in refunds. Adejumo and Jinadu, who were based in the United States, handled fraud proceeds directed to prepaid debit cards and bank accounts they controlled. DOJ reported that Adejumo received 33 months and Jinadu 27 months. The case is significant because it shows how xDedic access was converted into an organized refund-fraud pipeline involving both foreign and U.S.-based actors.

## Relevance to IC

title: USD 300 million seized and 3,500 suspects arrested in international financial crime operation - Operation HAECHI IV
text:
## Summary

Operation HAECHI IV, the fourth iteration of Korea-initiated HAECHI series, ran July to December 2023 across 34 countries. ~3,500 arrests, USD 300 million seized ($199M fiat, $101M virtual assets), 82,112 bank accounts blocked. Targeted voice phishing, romance scams, investment fraud, BEC, e-commerce fraud and more. 260% arrest increase over HAECHI III.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation HAECHI IV
- **Timeframe:** July – December 2023
- **Countries:** 34
- **Arrests:** ~3,500
- **Assets seized:** USD 300 million (USD 199M fiat + USD 101M virtual)
- **Bank accounts blocked:** 82,112
- **Crime types:** Voice phishing, romance scams, online sextortion, investment fraud, BEC, e-commerce fraud, money laundering
- **Emerging threats:** AI-generated voice cloning, fraudulent NFTs

## Relevance

Fourth HAECHI iteration showing massive scale increase. Cross-validated against HAECHI V data: consistent series growth (34 countries/3,500 arrests → 40 countries/5,500+ arrests).

title: Russian TrickBot malware dev sentenced to 64 months in prison
text:
## Source Summary

The TrickBot disruption was a multi-pronged effort in October 2020 involving both U.S. military cyber operations and a private-sector-led legal action to disable one of the world's largest and most persistent botnets. U.S. Cyber Command conducted offensive operations to interfere with TrickBot's command-and-control (C2) infrastructure, while Microsoft, acting under a court order from the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia, took down 120 of 128 identified TrickBot servers in the first week. The urgency was driven by concerns that TrickBot-deployed ransomware could disrupt U.S. election infrastructure ahead of the November 2020 presidential election. While the initial disruption was temporary — TrickBot operators eventually rebuilt portions of their infrastructure — the operation was followed by a sustained law enforcement campaign that produced arrests and indictments through 2024, including the arrest of developer Vladimir Dunaev in [[south-korea|South Korea]] and the indictment of nine Russian nationals.

## Relevance to IC

title: Law enforcement disrupt world's biggest ransomware operation - Operation Cronos (LockBit)
text:
## Summary

Official Europol press release announcing the disruption of the LockBit ransomware operation through **Operation Cronos Phase 1** on 20 February 2024. This was a multi-country enforcement action involving 10 countries and 12 agencies, led by the [[uk-nca|UK National Crime Agency]] and coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]] and [[eurojust|Eurojust]].

The source provides detailed operational metrics including arrests, server seizures, indictments, cryptocurrency account freezes, and the release of free decryption tools. It describes the coordination mechanisms used (SIENA messaging, operational meetings, technical sprints) and lists all participating agencies.

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official agency press release) |
| Credibility | 2 (confirmed — official source) |
| Topic Relevance | 95/100 |
| Data Completeness | 100/100 |
| Verification | Cross-validated against Phase 3 (Oct 2024); no contradictions |

## Key Metrics

- **Arrests:** 2 (Poland, Ukraine)
- **Servers seized:** 34
- **Indictments:** 5 (French and US authorities)
- **Cryptocurrency accounts frozen:** 200+
- **Participating countries:** 10 (+ 4 supporting)
- **Participating agencies:** 12

title: Law enforcement disrupt world's biggest ransomware operation - Operation Cronos (LockBit)
text:
## Source Summary

On 20 February 2024, law enforcement agencies from 10 countries disrupted the LockBit ransomware operation in a joint action known as **Operation Cronos**, led by the UK's National Crime Agency and coordinated by Europol and Eurojust. LockBit had been the most deployed ransomware variant worldwide since 2022, having emerged in late 2019. The operation resulted in the seizure of 34 servers across 8 countries, 2 arrests, 5 indictments, the freezing of over 200 cryptocurrency accounts, and the identification of 14,000 rogue accounts for removal. Free decryption tools were made available via the "No More Ransom" portal. - **Operation name:** Operation Cronos - **Date:** 20 February 2024

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2024-02-20_europol_operation-cronos-lockbit.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Zambia arrests 77 people in swoop on 'scam' call centre
text:
## Source Summary

In **April 2024**, Zambian authorities raided the **Golden Top Support Services** call center in Roma, a suburb of Lusaka, resulting in **77 arrests** — including 22 Chinese nationals (who subsequently pleaded guilty in June 2024), 1 Cameroonian, and 54 Zambian youths aged 20-25 (Source: [[2024-06-05-therecord-zambia-golden-top-guilty-pleas]], [[2024-04-15-bitdefender-zambia-golden-top-arrests]]). The raid was led by Zambia's **Drug Enforcement Commission (DEC)** in coordination with Zambia Police, immigration, and anti-terrorism unit. Seizures included vehicles, two firearms, 97 desktop PCs, 13,000+ SIM cards, 11 SIM boxes, and 6 properties.

## Relevance to IC

title: 911 S5 Botnet Dismantled and Its Administrator Arrested in Coordinated International Operation
text:
## Source Summary

The Department of Justice announced on May 29, 2024 that a federal grand jury in the Southern District of Texas had indicted YunHe Wang for creating and operating the 911 S5 residential proxy botnet. DOJ described 911 S5 as the world's largest botnet, with more than 19 million IP addresses compromised in nearly 200 countries. According to the release, Wang allegedly sold access to infected devices through the 911 S5 service, enabling fraud, bomb threats, child exploitation offenses, financial theft, and export-control evasion. The operation involved coordinated investigative and seizure activity with partners in Germany, Singapore, Thailand, and the United States. - Cross-border evidence and enforcement support from Germany, Singapore, and Thailand

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2024-05-29_opa_yunhe-wang-911-s5-indictment.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Largest ever operation against botnets hits dropper malware ecosystem - Operation Endgame
text:
## Summary

Official Europol press release announcing **Operation Endgame Phase 1**, the largest-ever international operation against botnets. Conducted between 27 and 29 May 2024 and coordinated from [[europol-ec3|Europol]]'s headquarters, the operation targeted dropper malware infrastructure that enabled ransomware deployment.

The source describes the coordination model (command post with 20+ officers from 4 countries at Europol HQ), lists all participating and supporting countries, and details the operational results. It confirms the link between dropper malware infrastructure and at least 15 ransomware groups.

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official agency press release) |
| Credibility | 2 (confirmed — official source) |
| Topic Relevance | 95/100 |
| Data Completeness | 100/100 |
| Verification | Cross-validated against Phase 2 (May 2025); no contradictions |

## Key Metrics

- **Arrests:** 4 (1 Armenia, 3 Ukraine)
- **Servers taken down:** 100+
- **Domains seized:** 2,000+
- **Cryptocurrency (key suspect):** EUR 69 million+
- **Participating countries:** 6 (+ 7 supporting)
- **Participating agencies:** 15

title: Largest ever operation against botnets — Operation Endgame
text:
## Source Summary

Ransomware is *almost certainly* the single most significant cybercrime type driving international cooperation in the 2020s. Ransomware attacks encrypt victims' data and demand payment (typically in cryptocurrency) for decryption keys. The Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) model, where developers provide tools to affiliates in exchange for a share of ransom payments, has created a complex criminal ecosystem that spans multiple jurisdictions and requires coordinated international responses.

## Relevance to IC

title: USD 257 million seized in global police crackdown against online scams - Operation First Light 2024
text:
## Summary

Operation First Light 2024 was a coordinated global crackdown on online scams from March to May 2024 across 61 countries. 3,950 arrests, 14,643 additional suspects identified, USD 257 million seized, 6,745 bank accounts frozen. Bolstered by AFRIPOL, ASEANAPOL, GCCPOL, and Europol. Utilized INTERPOL's I-GRIP mechanism for cross-border fund recovery.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation First Light 2024
- **Timeframe:** March – May 2024
- **Countries:** 61
- **Arrests:** 3,950
- **Additional suspects:** 14,643
- **Assets seized:** USD 257 million ($135M fiat, $2M crypto, ~$120M physical assets)
- **Bank accounts frozen:** 6,745
- **Crime types:** Phishing, investment fraud, fake online shopping, romance scams, impersonation scams

## Relevance

Part of longstanding First Light series. 61-country scope is one of the broadest IC operations. I-GRIP mechanism demonstrated for rapid cross-border fund interception. Namibia trafficking rescue highlights intersection of online scams and forced labor.

title: Texas Man Sentenced to 9 Months in Federal Prison for Operating Website that Offered Computer Attack Services
text:
## Source Summary

On 15 July 2024, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Central District of California announced that Jeremiah Sam Evans Miller had been sentenced to nine months in federal prison for operating the DDoS-for-hire service RoyalStresser.com, previously known as Supremesecurityteam.com. Miller had pleaded guilty on 6 April 2023 to conspiracy and substantive Computer Fraud and Abuse Act charges. The case formed part of Operation PowerOFF, the multinational campaign against booter and stresser services. DOJ stated that RoyalStresser was one of the services targeted in the December 2022 enforcement wave in which prosecutors in Los Angeles and Alaska charged six defendants and authorities seized 48 related domains. Although the prosecution itself was domestic, the case is highly relevant to international-cooperation analysis because DOJ expressly placed it within Operation PowerOFF, an operation involving the FBI, UK National Crime Agency, Dutch Police, and other foreign partners. The public record therefore supports the inference that Miller's case was one prosecutorial output of a broader coordinated cross-border infrastructure-disruption effort.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2024-07-15_cdca_miller-poweroff-sentencing.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Two Foreign Nationals Plead Guilty to Participation in LockBit Ransomware Group
text:
## Source Summary

On 18 July 2024, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of New Jersey announced that Ruslan Magomedovich Astamirov and Mikhail Vasiliev had pleaded guilty in Newark federal court to participation in the LockBit ransomware group. DOJ stated that Astamirov pleaded guilty to conspiracy to commit computer fraud and abuse and conspiracy to commit wire fraud, while Vasiliev pleaded guilty to conspiracy to commit computer fraud and abuse, intentional damage to a protected computer, transmission of a threat, and conspiracy to commit wire fraud. DOJ linked the guilty pleas directly to the February 2024 international disruption of LockBit under Operation Cronos. The release stated that LockBit had attacked more than 2,500 victims in at least 120 countries and that Astamirov alone derived at least USD 1.9 million in ransom payments. Vasiliev was alleged to have deployed LockBit against victims in the United States, the United Kingdom, and Switzerland and was arrested in Canada before extradition to the United States. The case is a strong operation-to-case bridge for Operation Cronos because it turns the multinational infrastructure disruption into individualized guilty pleas, extradition history, and forfeiture admissions. It also makes the international dimension unusually explicit by naming foreign victim jurisdictions and foreign arrest cooperation.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2024-07-18_nj_lockbit-astamirov-vasiliev-pleas.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Nigerian National Sentenced To More Than 12 Years In Federal Prison For Cyber Scams
text:
## Source Summary

On 23 July 2024, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the sentencing of Bamidele Omotosho to 12 years and 7 months in prison for conspiracy to commit wire fraud, conspiracy to commit money laundering, and computer intrusion. DOJ stated that Omotosho and his coconspirators purchased stolen access credentials and PII on xDedic and then used that data to run multiple fraud schemes. According to DOJ, the Omotosho-led group committed at least six different fraudulent schemes totaling more than USD 7.5 million in intended losses and more than USD 2 million in actual losses. The court ordered restitution of USD 2,051,108.70. The case is one of the strongest downstream-harm examples in the xDedic ecosystem because it ties marketplace purchases to a broad bundle of real-world frauds and a substantial prison term.

## Relevance to IC

title: xDedic Marketplace
text:
## Source Summary

The Department of Justice's xDedic victim-services page identifies `United States v. T'Andre McNeely, et al., 8:21-CR-00101-KM-SPF` as one of the cases that grew out of the xDedic Marketplace investigation. The page lists judgments for T'Andre McNeely, Michael Carr, and Brandon Williams. According to the official summary, McNeely pleaded guilty on 14 April 2022, Carr pleaded guilty on 19 April 2022, and Williams pleaded guilty on 29 March 2022. DOJ reports that McNeely and Carr each received 78-month prison sentences, while Williams received 12 months and 1 day. Although the victim-services page is concise, it is sufficient to establish that xDedic enforcement extended to downstream buyers and fraud actors, not only to marketplace administrators and malware suppliers.

## Relevance to IC

title: INTERPOL operation strikes major blow against West African financial crime - Operation Jackal III
text:
## Summary

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Jackal III from 10 April to 3 July 2024 across 21 countries on five continents, targeting online financial fraud and the West African organized crime syndicates behind it, particularly Black Axe. Approximately 300 arrests, 400+ additional suspects identified, 720+ bank accounts blocked, USD 3 million seized.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation Jackal III
- **Timeframe:** 10 April – 3 July 2024
- **Countries:** 21
- **Arrests:** ~300
- **Additional suspects:** 400+
- **Bank accounts blocked:** 720+
- **Assets seized:** USD 3 million
- **Crime types:** BEC, online financial fraud, money laundering, counterfeiting
- **Target:** Black Axe and similar West African organized crime groups

## Relevance

Significant escalation from Jackal (103 arrests to ~300). Five-continent scope demonstrates global reach of BEC enforcement. Argentina 5-year investigation shows intersection of BEC with broader financial crime.

title: INTERPOL operation strikes major blow against West African financial crime - Operation Jackal III
text:
## Source Summary

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Jackal III from 10 April to 3 July 2024 across 21 countries on five continents, targeting online financial fraud and the West African organized crime syndicates behind it, particularly Black Axe. The operation resulted in approximately 300 arrests, the identification of over 400 additional suspects, the blocking of more than 720 bank accounts, and the seizure of assets worth USD 3 million. Multiple criminal networks were dismantled worldwide. Black Axe and similar groups are considered responsible for the majority of the world's cyber-enabled financial fraud, with business email compromise (BEC) as a primary crime type. - **Operation name:** Operation Jackal III - **Date:** 10 April - 3 July 2024

## Relevance to IC

title: Law Enforcement Seizes 9 DDoS-for-Hire Webpages as Part of Global Crackdown on ‘Booter’ and ‘Stresser’ DDoS Services
text:
## Source Summary

Operation PowerOFF is an **ongoing, multi-phase international law enforcement operation** targeting DDoS-for-hire (booter/stresser) services. Launched in December 2018 and still active as of December 2024, it is coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]] and led operationally by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]], with the [[uk-nca|UK National Crime Agency (NCA)]] as a core partner. The operation has conducted at least six major enforcement waves, seizing over 142 domains, arresting 16 individuals, and identifying hundreds of users across 15 countries.

## Relevance to IC

title: CBI Operation Chakra-III Dismantles Virtual Asset Network
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Chakra-III was the third phase of India's Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) ongoing multi-phase campaign against transnational cyber-enabled financial crime. Launched in July 2024 with case registration RC2312024S0007, the operation targeted tech-support scam call centers operating from multiple Indian cities — principally Gurugram, Mumbai, Pune, Hyderabad, and Visakhapatnam — that defrauded victims in the United States and other countries. The CBI collaborated with the FBI, Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), and [[interpol|INTERPOL]], resulting in 43 arrests and the seizure of substantial evidence including gold bullion, cash, luxury vehicles, and over 950 electronic devices.

## Relevance to IC

title: AFP Operation Kraken charges alleged head of global organised crime app
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Kraken was a multinational law enforcement operation led by the [[australia-afp|Australian Federal Police (AFP)]] and coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]] that dismantled **Ghost**, a dedicated encrypted communications platform built and used exclusively by organized crime networks worldwide. The operation culminated on September 17-18, 2024, with near-simultaneous raids across Australia, Ireland, Italy, Canada, and Sweden. The platform's alleged creator and administrator, **Jay Je Yoon Jung** (32, of New South Wales, Australia), was arrested at his home in Narwee, Sydney.

title: LockBit power cut: four new arrests and financial sanctions against affiliates - Operation Cronos Phase 3
text:
## Summary

Official Europol press release announcing the third phase of **Operation Cronos**, continuing enforcement actions against the LockBit ransomware group. This phase featured new arrests, server seizures, and a significant expansion into financial sanctions against Evil Corp affiliates.

The source provides details on 4 new arrests across France, the UK, and Spain, plus coordinated sanctions by Australia, the UK, and the US. It describes the continuing multi-country investigation involving 12+ countries and 14 agencies, coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]] and [[eurojust|Eurojust]].

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official agency press release) |
| Credibility | 2 (confirmed — official source) |
| Topic Relevance | 95/100 |
| Data Completeness | 100/100 |
| Verification | Cross-validated against Phase 1 (Feb 2024); distinct events confirmed |

## Key Metrics

- **New arrests:** 4 (France, UK, Spain)
- **Servers seized:** 9 (Spain)
- **Financial sanctions:** Coordinated Australia/UK/US sanctions against Evil Corp affiliate
- **Participating countries:** 12+
- **Participating agencies:** 14

title: Operation CHAKRA-III: Inside CBI's Explosive Takedown
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Chakra-III was the third phase of India's Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) ongoing multi-phase campaign against transnational cyber-enabled financial crime. Launched in July 2024 with case registration RC2312024S0007, the operation targeted tech-support scam call centers operating from multiple Indian cities — principally Gurugram, Mumbai, Pune, Hyderabad, and Visakhapatnam — that defrauded victims in the United States and other countries. The CBI collaborated with the FBI, Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), and [[interpol|INTERPOL]], resulting in 43 arrests and the seizure of substantial evidence including gold bullion, cash, luxury vehicles, and over 950 electronic devices.

- INTERPOL's role as the primary coordination body for global financial cybercrime enforcement
- Korea's leading role in the HAECHI series

## Source Summary

Operation PowerOFF is an **ongoing, multi-phase international law enforcement operation** targeting DDoS-for-hire (booter/stresser) services. title: Rydox Cybercrime Marketplace Shut Down and Three Administrators Arrested
text:
## Source Summary

On 12 December 2024, DOJ announced the seizure of the Rydox cybercrime marketplace and the arrest of two alleged administrators, Ardit Kutleshi and Jetmir Kutleshi, in Kosovo pursuant to a U.S. extradition request. DOJ said the indictment had been unsealed that day in the Western District of Pennsylvania. According to the press release, Rydox had generated at least USD 230,000 in revenue since 2016, completed over 7,600 sales, and offered more than 321,000 cybercrime products to over 18,000 users. Those products included stolen personal information, access devices, login credentials, scam pages, and spamming tools affecting thousands of U.S. victims. The same announcement described a coordinated international action involving Kosovo, Albania, and Malaysia. A third alleged administrator, Shpend Sokoli, was arrested in Albania for expected domestic prosecution, while Malaysian authorities and the FBI seized the servers in Kuala Lumpur. DOJ also said the United States obtained authorization to seize the `Rydox.cc` domain and about USD 225,000 in cryptocurrency.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2024-12-12_wdpa_kutleshi-rydox-indictment.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Silk Road (marketplace)
text:
## Source Summary

The Silk Road takedown was the seizure and shutdown of the pioneering dark web drug marketplace on 2 October 2013, accompanied by the arrest of its creator and operator, Ross William Ulbricht (alias "Dread Pirate Roberts" or "DPR"), by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] in San Francisco. Silk Road, which operated on the Tor anonymity network from approximately February 2011, had facilitated an estimated USD 1.2 billion in illegal transactions -- primarily narcotics -- serving over 100,000 buyers through thousands of vendors worldwide. The FBI seized approximately 173,991 bitcoins (then worth ~$33.6 million) and the Silk Road server infrastructure hosted in Iceland.

## Relevance to IC

title: Department of Justice partners with Dutch police to break up HeartSender network
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Heart Blocker was a joint US-Dutch law enforcement action on January 29, 2025, targeting the HeartSender cybercrime marketplace network (also known as Saim Raza). The U.S. Department of Justice and the Dutch National Police coordinated the seizure of 39 domains and their associated servers that had been operating since at least 2020 as marketplaces selling phishing kits, credential-stealing tools, email extractors, and scam page generators. These tools were used by transnational organized crime groups to conduct [[bec-ic|business email compromise (BEC)]] schemes, resulting in over $3 million in confirmed US victim losses according to the DOJ (later revised to over $50 million by Pakistan's NCCIA).

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2025-01-30_cyberscoop-com_department-of-justice-partners-with-dutch-police-to-break-up-heartsender-network.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Key figures behind Phobos and 8Base ransomware arrested in international cybercrime crackdown
text:
## Summary

Official Europol press release announcing a coordinated international crackdown on the **Phobos** and **8Base** ransomware operations. The operation resulted in 4 arrests of Russian nationals (8Base leadership) and the takedown of 27 servers, with 400+ companies warned worldwide.

This source is *highly likely* the most relevant to the Korean context among the five verified sources, as it confirms the **arrest of a Phobos administrator in South Korea** in June 2024, followed by extradition to the United States in November 2024. This demonstrates active Korean participation in international ransomware enforcement.

The source describes a sustained investigation spanning from February 2019 to February 2025, with [[europol-ec3|Europol]] organizing 37 operational meetings and exchanging nearly 600 SIENA messages.

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official agency press release) |
| Credibility | 2 (confirmed — official source) |
| Topic Relevance | 100/100 (Korean connection) |
| Data Completeness | 100/100 |
| Verification | Standalone operation; Korean involvement cross-checked against south-korea.md |

## Key Metrics

- **Arrests:** 4 (Russian nationals, 8Base leadership)
- **Servers taken down:** 27
- **Companies warned:** 400+
- **Participating countries:** 14
- **Participating agencies:** 16
- **Investigation duration:** ~6 years (Feb 2019 - Feb 2025)

## Source Summary

In early February 2025, a coordinated international law enforcement action resulted in the arrest of **4 Russian nationals** who were key figures behind the **8Base ransomware** group, and the takedown of **27 servers** connected to their criminal network. The operation involved 14 countries and built on prior actions including the arrest of a Phobos administrator in South Korea in June 2024 (extradited to the US in November 2024) and a key Phobos affiliate arrest in Italy in 2023. Law enforcement warned over 400 companies worldwide of ongoing or imminent ransomware attacks. Europol had supported the investigation since February 2019, organizing 37 operational meetings and exchanging nearly 600 operational messages via SIENA. - **Operation date:** Early February 2025 - **Publication date:** 11 February 2025

title: Justice Department Charges 12 Chinese Contract Hackers and Law Enforcement Officers in Global Computer Intrusion Campaigns
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ press release announcing the charging of 12 Chinese nationals affiliated with i-Soon (Anxun Information Technology) and APT27 for global computer intrusion campaigns directed by China's MPS and MSS. The coordinated action involved DOJ, FBI, NCIS, State Department, and Treasury. i-Soon charged USD 10,000-75,000 per successfully exploited email inbox. Victims included foreign ministries in Asia and US-based dissidents.

## Relevance to IC

This source illustrates the intersection of state-sponsored cyber espionage and cybercrime enforcement. While not a traditional multi-country law enforcement operation, it demonstrates US multi-agency coordination and the use of indictments, sanctions, and Rewards for Justice as enforcement tools against state-directed hacking. South Korea's inclusion as a victim country is relevant to the Korea-focused lens of this wiki.

title: Europol-led Operation Stream Takes Down Kidflix CSAM Platform
text:
# Europol-led Operation Stream Takes Down Kidflix CSAM Platform

## Source Overview

Security Affairs and TRM Labs reported on the Europol-coordinated takedown of Kidflix, one of the largest known child sexual abuse material (CSAM) streaming platforms. The operation, led by the Bavarian State Criminal Police and ZCB, involved 35+ countries and resulted in 79 arrests and the identification of 1,393 suspects.

## Key Data Points

- Platform operational since 2021; seized March 11, 2025
- 1.8 million registered users; 91,000 unique videos (6,288 hours)
- 79 arrests across 35+ countries
- 3,000+ devices seized; 39 children identified and protected
- Cryptocurrency-based payment system traced through exchanges and DeFi platforms

## References

- [Security Affairs: Europol-led op shuts down CSAM platform Kidflix](https://securityaffairs.com/176154/cyber-crime/europol-led-op-shuts-down-csam-platform-kidflix.html)
- [TRM Labs: Europol and German Authorities Lead Global Takedown of Darknet Child Exploitation Platform Kidflix](https://www.trmlabs.com/resources/blog/europol-and-german-authorities-lead-global-takedown-of-darknet-child-exploitation-platform-kidflix)

title: Leader of Qakbot Malware Conspiracy Indicted for Involvement in Global Ransomware Scheme
text:
## Source Summary

On 22 May 2025, the Department of Justice unsealed an indictment charging Rustam Rafailevich Gallyamov, 48, of Moscow, Russia, with leading the QakBot malware conspiracy since 2008. The indictment was unsealed in conjunction with Operation Endgame, the international law enforcement campaign targeting ransomware-enabling infrastructure. ### Charges Gallyamov was charged with conspiracy, wire fraud, and related offenses. He allegedly developed and deployed QakBot to build a botnet that provided access to co-conspirators who deployed ransomware variants including ProLock, DopplePaymer, Egregor, REvil, Conti, Name Locker, Black Basta, and Cactus.

## Relevance to IC

title: Pakistan Arrests 21 in 'Heartsender' Malware Service
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Heart Blocker was a joint US-Dutch law enforcement action on January 29, 2025, targeting the HeartSender cybercrime marketplace network (also known as Saim Raza). The U.S. Department of Justice and the Dutch National Police coordinated the seizure of 39 domains and their associated servers that had been operating since at least 2020 as marketplaces selling phishing kits, credential-stealing tools, email extractors, and scam page generators. These tools were used by transnational organized crime groups to conduct [[bec-ic|business email compromise (BEC)]] schemes, resulting in over $3 million in confirmed US victim losses according to the DOJ (later revised to over $50 million by Pakistan's NCCIA).

## Relevance to IC

title: DOJ seizes 145 domains tied to BidenCash marketplace
text:
## Source Summary

On 5 June 2025, the US Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of Virginia announced the seizure of approximately 145 domains (both darknet and clearnet) and cryptocurrency funds associated with the BidenCash criminal marketplace. The operation was led by the [[us-secret-service|US Secret Service]] Frankfurt Resident Office and Cyber Investigative Section, together with the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] Albuquerque Field Office, and coordinated by the [[us-doj|US Department of Justice]]. International partners included the [[netherlands-politie|Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit]] and private-sector firms Searchlight Cyber and The Shadowserver Foundation. The seized domains were redirected to a US law enforcement-controlled server. BidenCash, which had operated since March 2022, had served over 117,000 customers, facilitated the trafficking of more than 15 million payment card numbers, and generated over USD 17 million in revenue. The seizure was part of the broader [[operation-endgame|Operation Endgame]] framework. No arrests were announced as of the seizure date.

## Relevance to IC

The source supports [[bidencash-takedown]] in the `operations` corpus.

title: U.S. Government seizes approximately 145 criminal marketplace domains
text:
## Source Summary

On 4 June 2025, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of Virginia announced the seizure of approximately 145 darknet and traditional domains and cryptocurrency funds associated with the BidenCash marketplace. DOJ stated that the court had authorized the cryptocurrency seizure and that the domains were redirected to a U.S. law-enforcement-controlled server. According to DOJ, BidenCash had supported more than 117,000 customers, trafficked over 15 million payment card numbers and associated personally identifiable information, and generated more than USD 17 million in revenue. DOJ further stated that between October 2022 and February 2023 the marketplace publicly dumped 3.3 million stolen payment cards as a promotional tactic. The seizure matter is highly relevant for international-cooperation analysis because DOJ thanked the Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit, Searchlight Cyber, and The Shadowserver Foundation for investigative assistance. The case therefore appears to combine U.S. seizure warrants with cross-border infrastructure action and private-sector technical support rather than criminal charges against identified operators.

## Relevance to IC

International partners included the [[netherlands-politie|Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit]] and private-sector firms Searchlight Cyber and The Shadowserver Foundation. The seized domains were redirected to a US law enforcement-controlled server. BidenCash, which had operated since March 2022, had served over 117,000 customers, facilitated the trafficking of more than 15 million payment card numbers, and generated over USD 17 million in revenue. The seizure was part of the broader [[operation-endgame|Operation Endgame]] framework. No arrests were announced as of the seizure date.

## Relevance to IC

The source supports [[bidencash-takedown]] in the `operations` corpus.

title: USD 439 million recovered in global financial crime operation - Operation HAECHI VI
text:
## Summary

Operation HAECHI VI, the sixth Korea-initiated HAECHI iteration, ran April to August 2025 across 40 countries. Recovered USD 439 million total ($342M fiat, $97M physical/virtual assets). 68,000+ bank accounts blocked, ~400 crypto wallets frozen. Continued targeting seven cyber-enabled fraud types. Notable Korea-UAE BEC recovery of USD 3.91 million.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation HAECHI VI
- **Timeframe:** April – August 2025
- **Countries:** 40
- **Assets recovered:** USD 439 million
- **Crypto recovered:** ~USD 16 million
- **Bank accounts blocked:** 68,000+
- **Crypto wallets frozen:** ~400
- **Crime types:** Voice phishing, romance scams, BEC, investment fraud, e-commerce fraud, money laundering

## Relevance

Sixth HAECHI iteration setting new financial recovery records. Korea-UAE I-GRIP sub-operation demonstrates rapid international fund interception critical for BEC and voice phishing cases.

title: South Korea repatriates 64 scam centre suspects from Cambodia
text:
## Summary

In October 2025 and January 2026, South Korea repatriated 107+ nationals from Cambodia suspected of operating in online scam compounds including voice phishing, romance scams, and pig butchering cryptocurrency fraud. An estimated 1,000 Korean citizens were working in Cambodian scam centres. Additional repatriations from Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, and the Philippines. Coincided with US/UK sanctions against Cambodia-based Prince Group.

## Key Data

- **Operation type:** Bilateral repatriation (Korea-Cambodia)
- **Timeframe:** October 2025 (64), January 2026 (73+)
- **Countries:** South Korea, Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, Philippines
- **Suspects repatriated:** 107+ from Cambodia, 20+ from other countries
- **Financial losses:** KRW 48.6 billion (USD 33 million) from January 2026 batch
- **Crime types:** Voice phishing, romance scams, pig butchering, online fraud

## Relevance

Illustrates evolution of voice phishing from China-based call centres to Southeast Asian trafficking-fuelled scam compounds. Korean nationals were both perpetrators and trafficking victims.

title: 574 arrests and USD 3 million recovered in coordinated cybercrime operation across Africa - Operation Sentinel
text:
## Summary

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Sentinel from 27 October to 27 November 2025 across 19 African countries under the AFJOC umbrella. 574 suspects arrested, USD 3 million recovered. Over 6,000 malicious links taken down and 6 ransomware variants decrypted. Focused on BEC, digital extortion, and ransomware. Funded by UK FCDO and EU-Council of Europe GLACY-e project.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation Sentinel
- **Timeframe:** 27 October – 27 November 2025
- **Countries:** 19 African countries
- **Arrests:** 574
- **Assets recovered:** USD 3 million
- **Estimated losses:** USD 21 million+
- **Malicious links taken down:** 6,000+
- **Ransomware variants decrypted:** 6
- **Crime types:** BEC, digital extortion, ransomware
- **Funding:** UK FCDO, EU-Council of Europe GLACY-e

## Relevance

Largest BEC-related enforcement action on the African continent. Demonstrates donor-funded model for building African cybercrime enforcement capacity.

title: Servers used for cybercrime around the world taken down
text:
## Summary

Official Eurojust press release for the March 2026 disruption of a malware-enabled residential proxy service. It provides the strongest public metric set for [[proxy-service-takedown-2026-03]]: eight participating countries, 24 servers taken down, 34 domains seized, approximately EUR 3.5 million in cryptocurrency frozen by US authorities, about 124,000 customer users, and roughly 369,000 compromised devices across 163 countries.

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official Eurojust press release) |
| Credibility | confirmed |
| Topic Relevance | 100/100 |
| Data Completeness | 85/100 |
| Verification | Cross-checked against DOJ and FBI public sources |

## Key Metrics

- **Action day:** 2026-03-11
- **Announcement:** 2026-03-12
- **Participating countries:** Austria, Bulgaria, France, Germany, Hungary, Netherlands, Romania, United States
- **Servers taken down:** 24 in seven countries
- **Domains seized:** 34
- **Cryptocurrency frozen:** approximately EUR 3.5 million
- **Customer users:** approximately 124,000
- **Compromised devices:** approximately 369,000 routers and other devices
- **Geographic footprint:** approximately 163 countries

title: AVrecon Malware-Infected Routers Exploited as Residential Proxies by SocksEscort
text:
## Source Summary

FBI TLP:CLEAR FLASH that gives the strongest technical attribution for [[proxy-service-takedown-2026-03]]. It links the SocksEscort residential proxy service to **AVrecon** malware, explains the router/IoT compromise model, and provides defensive indicators and remediation advice.

## Relevance to IC

The advisory converts the enforcement story from a generic proxy-service takedown into a malware-enabled residential proxy botnet case. It also identifies the law-enforcement partners involved in the joint takedown from the technical-notification perspective.

## Extracted Facts

- **Malware:** AVrecon
- **Service:** SocksEscort residential proxy service
- **Target devices:** routers and IoT devices, especially unsupported or vulnerable SOHO devices
- **Device footprint:** approximately 163 countries
- **Compromised access sold:** approximately 369,000 devices since 2020
- **Joint-operation partners named by FBI:** FBI, Europol, French OFAC, Dutch National Police, Austria's Bundeskriminalamt, DCIS, and IRS
- **Observed abuse:** ad fraud, vulnerability exploitation attempts, password spraying, digital marketplace fraud, banking fraud, romance fraud, and other malicious activity

title: Authorities Dismantle Global Malicious Proxy Service that Deployed Malware and Defrauded Thousands of U.S. Persons, Businesses, and Financial Institutions of Millions of Dollars in Losses
text:
## Source Summary

Official DOJ/USAO EDCA release that resolves the earlier naming gap for [[proxy-service-takedown-2026-03]]. It identifies the service as **SocksEscort**, describes the malware-enabled residential proxy model, supplies the US victim-fraud examples, and lists the US investigative agencies and European partners.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal US legal source for the operation. It ties court-authorized US seizure warrants to Eurojust/Europol coordination and shows how US domain-seizure authority, federal cyber investigators, financial intelligence, and European server actions were combined in one disruption.

## Extracted Facts

- **Target:** SocksEscort residential proxy network
- **US lead:** DOJ / USAO Eastern District of California
- **US investigators:** FBI Sacramento, DCIS, IRS Criminal Investigation Oakland
- **Additional US support:** DOJ OIA, NSD National Security Cyber Section, CCIPS, ICHIP The Hague, FinCEN, California Highway Patrol
- **Private support:** Lumen Black Lotus Labs, Shadowserver
- **Scale:** about 369,000 IP addresses offered since summer 2020; about 8,000 infected routers listed in February 2026, about 2,500 of them in the United States
- **Abuse:** account takeovers, cryptocurrency fraud, banking fraud, unemployment-insurance fraud, and other cyber-enabled fraud

title: Operation Onymous
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Onymous was a joint international law enforcement operation conducted on 5-6 November 2014 by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]], [[europol-ec3|Europol]], [[eurojust|Eurojust]], and the [[us-dhs|U.S. Department of Homeland Security]], targeting darknet markets and other hidden services on the Tor network. The operation resulted in the seizure of 267 .onion addresses across 27 distinct websites — including Silk Road 2.0, Cloud 9, and Hydra — the arrest of 17 individuals, and the seizure of approximately USD 1 million in Bitcoin along with cash, drugs, weapons, and computers. Blake Benthall, the alleged operator of Silk Road 2.0 (pseudonym "Defcon"), was arrested in San Francisco. The operation involved 17 European countries plus the United States and represented the largest coordinated action against Tor hidden services at the time. However, it also generated significant controversy regarding the methods used to deanonymize Tor hidden services, including questions about a possible Carnegie Mellon University research exploit.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2026-04-17_en-wikipedia-org_operation-onymous.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Operation PowerOFF
text:
## Source Summary

Operation PowerOFF is an **ongoing, multi-phase international law enforcement operation** targeting DDoS-for-hire (booter/stresser) services. Launched in December 2018 and still active as of December 2024, it is coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]] and led operationally by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]], with the [[uk-nca|UK National Crime Agency (NCA)]] as a core partner. The operation has conducted at least six major enforcement waves, seizing over 142 domains, arresting 16 individuals, and identifying hundreds of users across 15 countries.

## Relevance to IC

title: trickbot conti mdtn indictment.pdf
text:
## Source Summary

United States v. Galochkin et al. encompasses three indictments unsealed on 7 September 2023 across three federal districts, charging nine Russian nationals with conspiracies related to the TrickBot malware and Conti ransomware operations. Two additional defendants (Dunaev and Witte) were charged separately and have been convicted and sentenced. The case demonstrates the sustained prosecution approach required when initial technical disruption (October 2020) fails to permanently disable a botnet.

text:
## Source Summary

The Korea Internet & Security Agency (KISA, 한국인터넷진흥원) is the sub-organization of the [[ministry-of-science-and-ict-korea|Ministry of Science and ICT]] (과학기술정보통신부) responsible for internet governance and cybersecurity of the internet in South Korea. KISA was created in **July 2009** by merging three predecessor agencies: the Korea Information Security Agency (KISA, 한국정보보호진흥원), the National Internet Development Agency (NIDA, 한국인터넷진흥원 — same Korean name, different English), and the Korean IT International Cooperation Agency (KIICA).

title: BBC: GozNym cyber-crime gang which stole millions busted
text:
> [!note] Fetch note
> BBC News fetching was blocked during enrichment (2026-04-10). Content reconstructed from corroborating Europol, Eurojust, DOJ and secondary-press sources (Silicon UK, Bleeping Computer, TechRadar, Bitdefender, Claims Journal, US News) all dated 2019-05-16, which uniformly report the figures cited above. The BBC article exists at the stored URL but its exact wording could not be verified during this enrichment pass.

## Source

- **Publisher**: BBC News (Technology section)
- **URL**: <https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-48294788>
- **Published**: 2019-05-16 (BBC numeric ID range 48xxxxxx corresponds to mid-May 2019, matching the GozNym announcement day)
- **Source Tier**: 3 (mainstream news, not specialist cybercrime reporting)
- **Reliability**: medium-high (BBC News has strong editorial standards; rating raised above default medium)
- **Credibility**: probably-true (corroborated by multiple independent sources on the same day)
- **Operations referenced**: [[goznym-takedown|GozNym Malware Network Dismantling]]

## Summary

BBC Technology's coverage of the **GozNym malware network dismantling** (16 May 2019) reported the headline Europol/DOJ facts for a general audience:

1. **Scope**: ~41,000 victim computers infected via phishing email campaigns
2. **Target**: ~US$100 million in attempted bank account theft
3. **Defendants**: 10 members charged in the Western District of Pennsylvania; five Russians remain at large
4. **Cross-border prosecutions**: The DOJ highlighted the *unprecedented* move of prosecuting members simultaneously in four countries (US, Georgia, Ukraine, Moldova) rather than attempting mass extradition
5. **Leadership**: Alexander Konovolov (Tbilisi, Georgia) identified as the ringleader
6. **Cybercrime-as-a-service structure**: Bulletproof hosters, money mules, crypters, spammers, and technical support operated as a coordinated criminal enterprise

## Why this source matters

For the IC analysis on [[goznym-takedown]] this BBC article provides:

- **Mainstream-audience corroboration** of the DOJ/Europol figures (independent validation of defendant count and financial scale)
- **A Tier 3 reference** adding source diversity beyond the primary Europol press release
- **Confirmation of the 'prosecution distribution' pattern** — a key IC innovation where the lead jurisdiction (US) accepts that not all suspects can be extradited and coordinates parallel charges locally

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| [[europol-goznym-takedown|Europol press release]] | 10 defendants, 41K victims, $100M target, 6 countries |
| US DOJ press release (2019-05-16) | Western District of Pennsylvania indictment, Konovolov as ringleader |
| Eurojust press release (2019-05-16) | Coordination of searches in Bulgaria, Georgia, Moldova, Ukraine |
| PCMag / Bleeping Computer / TechRadar | Independent secondary corroboration of figures |

Source diversity for the GozNym takedown is *high* — BBC plus PCMag plus Europol/Eurojust/DOJ official releases support the operation page's `credibility_index: 3.62`.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | GozNym cyber-crime gang which stole millions busted | BBC News | 2019-05-16 | https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-48294788 |
| [2] | GozNym malware: cybercriminal network dismantled in international operation | Europol | 2019-05-16 | https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/goznym-malware-cybercriminal-network-dismantled-in-international-operation |
| [3] | GozNym Cyber-Criminal Network Operating out of Europe Targeting American Entities Dismantled | US DOJ | 2019-05-16 | https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/goznym-cyber-criminal-network-operating-out-europe-targeting-american-entities-dismantled |
| [4] | GozNym Cybercrime Group Behind $100 Million Damages Dismantled | Bleeping Computer | 2019-05-16 | https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/goznym-cybercrime-group-behind-10

title: BleepingComputer: Darknet marketplace Nemesis Market seized by German police
text:
## Source Alias Resolution

This source page was originally only a link-preservation record. It has been converted into a summary-only source entry with publisher, publication date, and canonical URL metadata so the menu source index no longer treats it as unverified.

## Source Summary

BleepingComputer reported that German police seized Nemesis Market infrastructure in Germany and Lithuania after a BKA/ZIT-led action. The article is retained as specialist press corroboration while the official BKA source remains the preferred factual anchor for seizure figures and participating authorities. The corresponding official or participant-side anchor in this repository is [[2024-03-21_bka-de_illegaler-darknet-marktplatz-nemesis-market-abgeschaltet]], which should be preferred for primary operational facts.

## Relevance to IC

The article provides specialist press context for a public international cybercrime operation. It is useful for cross-checking names, dates, and private-sector participation, while official government or participant sources remain the stronger basis for legal and operational findings.

text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: CBS News
- **Author**: Kerry Breen
- **URL**: <https://www.cbsnews.com/news/justice-department-charges-russian-men-cybercrime-ransomware/>
- **Published**: 2025-02-11
- **Source Tier**: 3 (mainstream general news)
- **Reliability**: medium (general-news outlet; not a cybersecurity specialist)
