> [!info] Mismatch resolved (2026-04-10)
> Filename still references "hive" but the article covers Phobos ransomware charges (2025-02-11). Link to [[hive-ransomware-takedown]] removed; only [[phobos-8base-crackdown]] retained.

## Summary

CBS News reports that the **US Department of Justice** charged two Russian nationals, **Roman Berezhnoy (33)** and **Egor Nikolaevich Glebov (39)**, with operating the **Phobos ransomware-as-a-service**. Key facts as reported by CBS News:

1. The Phobos criminal operation allegedly generated **over USD 16 million** in ransom payments
2. Targets included **hundreds of US entities**, with children's hospitals, healthcare providers, and educational institutions highlighted
3. Both defendants face multiple charges carrying **5–20 year** maximum sentences
4. Neither was in US custody at the time of the unsealing — extradition or voluntary surrender would be required for prosecution
5. The charges follow a sustained international effort against Phobos-affiliated groups including the **8Base ransomware crew**, four of whom were arrested in Thailand in February 2025

## Why this source matters

For the [[phobos-8base-crackdown]] page, CBS News provides:

- Mainstream-press confirmation of defendant identities and financial figures
- Useful plain-English framing of the Phobos victim mix (hospitals, schools)
- Independent corroboration alongside specialized sources (Bleeping Computer, The Record)

CBS News is not a specialized cybersecurity outlet — its reliability for technical detail is medium, but for basic case facts (names, charges, dollar figures sourced from DOJ filings) it is *probably-true*.

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| US DOJ press release (2025-02-11) | Primary source for indictment details |
| Bleeping Computer / The Record (Phobos coverage) | Technical context, 8Base linkage |
| Europol press release on 8Base arrests | Multi-national arrest coordination |

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Justice Department charges 2 Russians with operating cybercrime group using ransomware | CBS News (Kerry Breen) | 2025-02-11 | https://www.cbsnews.com/news/justice-department-charges-russian-men-cybercrime-ransomware/ |
| [2] | DOJ press release on Phobos indictment | US DOJ | 2025-02-11 | https://www.justice.gov/ |

title: CBS News: U.S., Russia, Cybercrime: Dark Web Market Suspect Sergey Medvedev Arrested in Thailand
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: CBS News (with Associated Press wire content)
- **Author**: CBS News staff / AP byline hidden
- **URL**: <https://www.cbsnews.com/news/us-russia-cybercrime-dark-web-market-suspect-sergey-medvedev-thailand/>
- **Published**: 2018-02-09 (two days after the DOJ indictment unsealed)
- **Source Tier**: 3 (mainstream US broadcast news)
- **Reliability**: medium-high (CBS and AP both have strong editorial standards; raised from default `medium`)
- **Credibility**: probably-true
- **Operations referenced**: [[infraud-organization-takedown|Infraud Organization Takedown]]

## Summary

CBS News' coverage of the **Infraud Organization takedown** focuses especially on the cross-border arrest of **Sergey Medvedev** (age 31, Russian national) at his Bangkok apartment by Thai police on 2 February 2018 at US request, five days before the DOJ publicly unsealed the indictment on 7 February 2018.

CBS emphasizes:

1. **Medvedev's role** — co-founder of Infraud (alongside Ukrainian Svyatoslav Bondarenko), payment-system operator, and de-facto owner/administrator after Bondarenko's disappearance in 2015
2. **Domestic-vs-international arrest split** — 13 arrests inside the US (California, New York, Alabama) on the day of the DOJ announcement; additional suspects apprehended in France, UK, Italy, Australia, Kosovo, and Serbia, all awaiting extradition
3. **4-year undercover operation** — US Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) deployed an undercover agent inside the forum starting in 2014, illustrating the long timeline required for cybercrime forum dismantlement
4. **Scale of the forum** — 4.3 million card/account records traded, USD 530 million in losses, ~11,000 forum members

## Why this source matters

For the [[infraud-organization-takedown]] operation this CBS/AP report provides:

- **Specific arrest-location detail** for Medvedev (Bangkok apartment, 2 February 2018) — not in all other sources
- **Confirmation that US-Thailand cooperation was informal police-to-police** before any formal extradition proceeding (US "request" to Thai authorities, rather than MLAT)
- **HSI attribution** — HSI, not FBI alone, initiated the undercover operation, which is relevant for the SNA edge data on the operation page
- **Geographical distribution of 13 US arrests** (California/New York/Alabama) — useful for understanding multi-district US coordination
- **2014 start date** for the undercover operation — enables timeline analysis of the 4-year sustained-investigation pattern

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| [[wired-infraud-organization-takedown]] | 36 indictments, $530M loss, 13 arrests, Medvedev in Thailand |
| [[krebs-on-security-infraud-organization-takedown]] | 16-country cooperation, defendant identities |
| [[voa-infraud-organization-takedown]] | Thai arrest, Russian nationality |
| US DOJ press release | Indictment details, charges, District of Nevada |

Four independent Tier-3 news sources for the same operation (CBS, Wired, Krebs, VOA) corroborate the DOJ press release, supporting the operation page's `credibility_index: 3.3`. CBS uniquely contributes the **HSI undercover-operation start date (2014)** and the **Medvedev arrest location** detail.

## References

title: CyberScoop: DDoS-happy teenagers arrested in international cybercrime bust
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: CyberScoop
- **Author**: Patrick Howell O'Neill (CyberScoop cybersecurity reporter, later at MIT Technology Review)
- **URL**: <https://cyberscoop.com/ddos-europol-arrest-december-2016/>
- **Published**: 2016-12-12 (3 days after the sweep concluded on 9 December 2016)
- **Source Tier**: 3 (cybersecurity trade press)
- **Reliability**: medium-high (CyberScoop and Patrick Howell O'Neill have strong track records on law-enforcement cybercrime reporting; raised from default medium)
- **Credibility**: probably-true (core figures corroborated by FBI and Europol same-day statements)
- **Operations referenced**: [[ddos-for-hire-sweep-2016|International DDoS-for-Hire Sweep]]

## Summary

CyberScoop's coverage of the **December 2016 international DDoS-for-hire sweep** describes a 5-day (5-9 December 2016) operation targeting *users* (not operators) of stresser and booter services. Key points:

1. **Targeting users, not operators** — the operation specifically targeted individuals who *paid for* DDoS-for-hire services, an enforcement strategy focused on shrinking the demand side of the market
2. **Demographics** — CyberScoop highlights that the majority of arrestees were male teenagers, aligning with the parallel awareness campaign's diversion framing
3. **Scale** — 34 arrests and ~101 suspects interviewed/cautioned across 13 countries (12 European nations + Australia, coordinated by Europol EC3 and FBI via the International Cyber Crime Coordination Cell (IC4))
4. **Continuity** — explicit reference to earlier January 2016 Europol enforcement as part of a multi-year targeting campaign

## Why this source matters

For the IC analysis on [[ddos-for-hire-sweep-2016]] this CyberScoop article provides:

- **Source diversity** beyond the FBI/Europol official press releases
- **Key demographic framing** (youth population) that informs the operation's prevention/deterrence angle
- **A named reporter with strong track record** improving the reliability rating above the default Tier 3 baseline

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| FBI press release 2016-12-09 | 34 arrests, 101 interviewed, 13 countries, joint coordination |
| Europol press release 2016-12-09 | Same arrest figures, stressers/booters framing |
| BBC coverage 2016-12 | Youth demographic, international scope |

The CyberScoop article is one of the more detailed secondary sources on this sweep and is a primary reference for the operation page's Period 1 (2015-2018) entry.

title: CyberScoop: Operation Delilah (SilverTerrier BEC)
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: CyberScoop
- **Author**: Tonya Riley (CyberScoop reporter covering cyber policy and enforcement)
- **URL**: <https://cyberscoop.com/silverterrier-interpol-nigeria-bec/>
- **Published**: 2022-05-25
- **Source Tier**: 3 (specialized cybersecurity trade press)
- **Reliability**: medium-high (CyberScoop has a strong track record on INTERPOL/DOJ cybercrime reporting)
- **Credibility**: probably-true
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-delilah|Operation Delilah (SilverTerrier)]]

## Summary

CyberScoop's coverage confirms the **March 2022 arrest of an alleged SilverTerrier ringleader** via INTERPOL's Operation Delilah. Key points:

1. A 37-year-old Nigerian national was arrested upon re-entry to Nigeria after evading a 2021 sting operation
2. Private-sector threat-intelligence contributions were central: Palo Alto Networks (Unit 42), Group-IB, and Trend Micro provided domain attribution and malware telemetry
3. ~50,000 victims attributed to the SilverTerrier cluster since 2014
4. BEC is framed within the US$2.4 billion 2021 IC3 loss figure, positioning Delilah as high-priority enforcement
5. Operation Delilah is continuous with the earlier Falcon I (2020) and Falcon II (2021) suppressions — a pattern of *sustained* rather than *episodic* enforcement

## Why this source matters

For [[operation-delilah]] this CyberScoop article provides:

- **Named private-sector partners** (Unit 42, Group-IB, Trend Micro) — critical for SNA edge analysis of public-private cooperation
- **Historical continuity** linking Delilah to Falcon I/II and establishing the multi-year INTERPOL-Nigeria enforcement baseline
- **Independent corroboration** of INTERPOL's own press release on the same operation
- **Author byline** (Tonya Riley) — traceable, reputable trade-press coverage

title: CyberScoop: Indonesian Police Arrest 3 Men for Alleged Magecart-Style Attacks (Operation NightFury / GetBilling)
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: CyberScoop
- **Author**: Jeff Stone (CyberScoop security reporter)
- **URL**: <https://cyberscoop.com/magecart-arrest-indonesia-interpol-getbilling/>
- **Published**: 2020-01-27
- **Source Tier**: 3 (specialist cybersecurity media)
- **Reliability**: medium-high (CyberScoop is a respected cybersecurity news outlet; Jeff Stone is an experienced security reporter)
- **Credibility**: probably-true
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-nightfury|Operation NightFury (Magecart/GetBilling)]]

## Summary

CyberScoop's coverage of **Operation NightFury** reports the **27 January 2020** announcement by INTERPOL that **3 Indonesian men** were arrested on **20 December 2019** in Jakarta and Yogyakarta on charges of operating a Magecart-style web skimming group tracked as **GetBilling**.

Key specifics:

1. **571 infected online marketplaces** reported by Sanguine Labs; INTERPOL's own statement referred more conservatively to "hundreds of websites"
2. **Group-IB threat intelligence** drove the identification of the Indonesia-based operators — an example of direct private-sector intelligence sharing with INTERPOL
3. **INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI)** in Singapore coordinated the operation through the ASEAN Cybercrime Operations Desk (ASEAN Desk)
4. **Maximum sentence**: 10 years in Indonesian prison for each defendant if convicted
5. **Human-element detail**: CyberScoop includes the unusual vignette that one arrested suspect told Indonesian media he had earned barely enough to buy a jacket from the skimming operation — suggesting the arrested individuals were low-level affiliates rather than Magecart leadership

## Why this source matters

For [[operation-nightfury]] this CyberScoop article provides:

- **Exact arrest date** (20 December 2019) vs. the announcement date (27 January 2020) — enables timeline analysis of the ~5-week gap between arrest and public disclosure
- **Specific arrest cities** (Jakarta, Yogyakarta) — useful for jurisdictional analysis within Indonesia
- **571 website figure** from Sanguine Labs — a more specific number than INTERPOL's "hundreds"
- **Corroboration of Group-IB's central role** in identifying the suspects, supporting the operation page's `technical_assistance` edge from Group-IB to INTERPOL
- **Reporter-level human detail** (the "jacket" quote) suggesting low-level affiliate status, which is relevant for understanding the *structural* limits of Magecart takedowns — low-level operators are removable but the ecosystem persists

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| Group-IB press release (2020-01-24) | GetBilling tracker, 571 infected marketplaces, INTERPOL coordination |
| INTERPOL official statement | 3 Indonesian arrests, ASEAN Desk coordination |

Both independent sources (CyberScoop + Group-IB) corroborate the operation's core facts.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Indonesian police arrest 3 men for alleged Magecart-style attacks | CyberScoop (Jeff Stone) | 2020-01-27 | https://cyberscoop.com/magecart-arrest-indonesia-interpol-getbilling/ |
| [2] | Night Fury — INTERPOL-led operation in Indonesia | Group-IB | 2020-01-24 | https://www.group-ib.com/media-center/press-releases/night-fury/ |

title: Europol: 20 Arrests in QQAAZZ Multi-Million Money Laundering Case
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Europol (European Cybercrime Centre / EC3)
- **URL**: <https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/20-arrests-in-qqaazz-multi-million-money-laundering-case>
- **Published**: 2020-10-15
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official international law enforcement agency press release)
- **Reliability**: high
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[2bagoldmule-qqaazz|Operation 2BaGoldMule / QQAAZZ Takedown]]

## Summary

Europol's 15 October 2020 press release announced **Operation 2BaGoldMule**, a coordinated 16-country takedown of the **QQAAZZ money-laundering-as-a-service network**. QQAAZZ had operated since 2016 as a specialized financial-crime infrastructure provider to ransomware and banking-Trojan affiliates (Dridex, Trickbot, GozNym), laundering tens of millions of euros through shell-company bank accounts and cryptocurrency tumblers.

Key outputs:

- **20 arrests** across Latvia, Bulgaria, UK, Spain, and Italy
- **~40 search locations** across 16 countries
- **US DOJ indictments** unsealed same day against 14 defendants in W.D. Pa.
- **Europol EC3 coordination** with Eurojust judicial support

## Why this source matters

QQAAZZ is a canonical case study because it targeted the *financial layer* of cybercrime rather than the malware or intrusion layer. For [[2bagoldmule-qqaazz]] and [[qqaazz-money-laundering-takedown]] this Europol release provides:

- **Official 16-country participant list** — authoritative input for SNA cooperation network analysis
- **Confirmed loss magnitude** (tens of millions EUR laundered) with *high* reliability
- **Documented US-EU day-of coordination** with parallel DOJ indictment unsealing — a model for transatlantic cybercrime enforcement
- **Named mechanism types**: formal EC3 coordination + Eurojust judicial cooperation + MLAT backing

This is a Tier 2 primary source and should anchor all analytical claims on the operation page.

title: Europol: Botnet taken down through international law enforcement cooperation (Ramnit, 2015)
text:
> [!info] Mismatch resolved (2026-04-10)
> Operation page renamed from `botnet-takedown-europol-2023` to `ramnit-botnet-takedown`. The Europol source is confirmed as the 2015-02-24 Ramnit takedown press release.

## Source

- **Publisher**: Europol (European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation)
- **URL**: <https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/botnet-taken-down-through-international-law-enforcement-cooperation>
- **Published**: 2015-02-24
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official agency press release)
- **Reliability**: high (Europol primary source)
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[ramnit-botnet-takedown|Ramnit Botnet Takedown (2015)]]

## Summary

Europol's press release covers the **24 February 2015 Ramnit botnet takedown**, an international law enforcement operation led by the UK's National Crime Agency with participation from Germany, Italy and the Netherlands. Key points:

1. **Scale**: ~3.2 million computers infected worldwide
2. **Target**: Ramnit botnet (Windows banking trojan + remote access capability)
3. **Lead**: UK National Crime Agency (NCA)
4. **Partner agencies**: German Federal Police (BKA), Italian Postal and Communications Police, Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit
5. **Private sector**: Microsoft Digital Crimes Unit, Symantec, AnubisNetworks
6. **Coordinating bodies**: Europol EC3, J-CAT, CERT-EU
7. **Technical action**: C&C server shutdown + sinkholing of ~300 domain addresses

## Why this source matters

For the IC wiki this Europol release is an important **correction source** — it revealed that the original operation page (botnet-takedown-europol-2023) was incorrectly scoped and has since been renamed to [[ramnit-botnet-takedown]]. It also independently documents the **2015 Ramnit takedown**, which is a significant Period 1 operation deserving its own page (or merge with any existing Ramnit entry).

Historically, Ramnit is notable as one of the first takedowns conducted through Europol's J-CAT framework (J-CAT was launched September 2014), making it an important precedent for EU-led multi-country botnet operations.

## Cross-reference with other sources

title: Europol: Fraudulent Shopping Sites Tied to Cybercrime Marketplace Taken Offline
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Europol (EC3)
- **URL**: <https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/fraudulent-shopping-sites-tied-to-cybercrime-marketplace-taken-offline>
- **Published**: 2024-12-05
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official Europol press release)
- **Reliability**: high
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[fake-shopping-sites-takedown-2024|Fraudulent Shopping Sites Takedown (2024)]]

## Summary

Europol's 5 December 2024 press release details a coordinated action against a **region-sorted data marketplace** and its upstream **fake-shopping-site phishing network**. Two suspects (Germany, Austria) arrested under European Arrest Warrants; 50+ servers and 200+ TB of digital evidence seized across six EU countries. The ~2-year investigation started in autumn 2022 following reports of bank-impersonation phishing.

The marketplace is distinctive in the 2024 enforcement landscape because it sorted stolen data by geographic region and account balance — a productization pattern suggesting mature criminal market segmentation. The upstream fake-shopping sites used e-commerce branding to capture payment credentials directly, bypassing the need for separate skimmer deployment.

## Why this source matters

For [[fake-shopping-sites-takedown-2024]] this press release is the **canonical primary source**. It provides:

- **Confirmed six-country participant list** — all EU, all intra-Schengen, using European Arrest Warrant mechanism (no MLAT needed)
- **Specific data-volume figure** (200 TB) useful for forensics capacity benchmarking
- **Investigation duration** (autumn 2022 → December 2024) — documents ~2-year timeline typical of Europol-led cases
- **Criminal business-model insight**: region-and-balance-sorted data as a specialized niche downstream of phishing

Because all participants are EU member states, the operation required no formal MLAT channels — the European Arrest Warrant and Europol EC3 coordination sufficed. This is a useful datapoint for *intra-EU cooperation speed* comparisons against *extra-EU* cases.

## Cross-reference

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| German Federal Criminal Police (BKA) | Arrests, domestic detail |
| Austrian BMI | Austria arrest |

title: Europol: Cybercriminals Stung as Hive Infrastructure Shut Down
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Europol
- **Author**: Europol Press Office (institutional)
- **URL**: <https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/cybercriminals-stung-hive-infrastructure-shut-down>
- **Published**: 2023-01-26
- **Source Tier**: 2 (Tier A — official international agency)
- **Reliability**: high (primary official source from the coordinating body)
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[hive-ransomware-takedown|Hive Ransomware Infrastructure Takedown]]

## Summary

The Europol press release announces the **26 January 2023** international operation that dismantled the **Hive ransomware** group's infrastructure. The operation, led by the **FBI** with operational support from the **US Secret Service**, **German BKA**, **Dutch National Police**, and **Europol EC3**, involved **13 countries** total.

The most notable operational detail is that the **FBI had covertly infiltrated Hive's network since July 2022** — roughly 7 months before the public takedown — and used its covert access to *secretly* obtain decryption keys and share them with Hive victims in real time, without alerting the criminals. This covert phase prevented an estimated **USD 130 million / EUR 120 million** in ransom payments during the 7-month infiltration window.

Hive operated as a **ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS)** affiliate model with a standard 80/20 revenue split (affiliates/developers) and had targeted **1,500+ victims in 80+ countries** since June 2021, including hospitals, school districts, financial firms, and critical infrastructure.

## Why this source matters

For [[hive-ransomware-takedown]] this Europol press release is the **primary Tier-2 source** and provides:

- **Authoritative confirmation** of the 1,500 victims / 80 countries / EUR 100M ransom / EUR 120M prevented figures
- **Named 13 participating countries** — enables accurate country-list population on the operation page (previously listed 13 but unverified)
- **Covert infiltration methodology** — the FBI's July 2022 intrusion and secret decryption-key distribution is a *notable* tactical innovation in ransomware disruption, distinct from the more common "seize and shut down" approach
- **RaaS revenue-split detail** (80/20) — useful for economic analysis of ransomware ecosystems
- **Zero arrests in the initial operation** — an important data point; infrastructure-only takedowns can still prevent substantial harm even without immediate prosecutions
- **Europol/Eurojust judicial coordination** — framing this as more than a US-led action

## Operational significance for cybercrime IC

The Hive takedown is a reference case for:

1. **Covert-infiltration model** — rather than seizing infrastructure immediately after access, law enforcement maintained covert presence for 7 months to maximize victim protection. This model has been discussed as a template for future RaaS disruptions.
2. **Victim-centric success metric** — the operation is measured not primarily by arrests but by ransom prevented (EUR 120M) and victims notified/decrypted (~300 victims during the covert phase per DOJ).
3. **Sustained multi-country coordination** — 13 countries participating at the infrastructure-disruption level is among the largest single-operation country counts for ransomware takedowns.

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| US DOJ press release (2023-01-26) | FBI lead, 7-month covert infiltration, decryption-key distribution |
| FBI Director Wray press conference | Victim count, ransom prevention figure |
| German BKA statement | German server seizure locations |

title: Europol: International operation dismantles criminal group of cyber-fraudsters
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Europol
- **URL**: <https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/international-operation-dismantles-criminal-group-of-cyber-fraudsters>
- **Published**: 2015-06-10
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official agency press release)
- **Reliability**: high (Europol primary source)
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[cyber-fraud-international-2015|International Cyber Fraud Dismantling Operation (June 2015)]]

## Summary

Europol's press release documents an **international BEC/MitM fraud takedown** on 9-10 June 2015. Key points:

1. **49 arrests, 58 properties searched** across 6 countries (Italy, Spain, Poland, UK, Belgium, Georgia)
2. **Method**: Computer intrusion + MitM + business email compromise (BEC) — attackers monitored corporate email for payment requests, intercepted them, and redirected payments to their own accounts
3. **Loss**: ~€6M accumulated in a short timeframe — *likely* an undercount given only arrested-suspect accounts
4. **Suspect origin**: Nigeria, Cameroon, Spain (notable for demonstrating that BEC networks already had a multi-national origin profile in 2015, before Operation Wire Wire)
5. **Coordination**: Joint Europol/Eurojust coordination centre at The Hague

## Why this source matters

For the IC analysis on [[cyber-fraud-international-2015]] this Europol release is the **primary (Tier 2) source** documenting what appears to be **one of the earliest large-scale BEC takedowns involving Nigerian-origin organized crime in Europe**. This predates:

- **Operation Wire Wire** (FBI, 2018, 74 arrests)
- **Operation reWired** (FBI, 2019, 281 arrests)
- **Operation Delilah** (Interpol, 2021)

The case is historically important for demonstrating that Europol had an early handle on the West African BEC network pattern in Europe, and that cross-border BEC operations were already being dismantled through joint Europol/Eurojust coordination in 2015.

> [!note] The operation page [[cyber-fraud-international-2015]] does not yet include a Nigerian/Cameroonian suspect-origin note. Consider updating the country profile linkages on [[nigeria]] and [[cameroon]] to reflect this case.

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| Eurojust press release 2015-06-10 | Same figures, coordination role |
| Italian Polizia di Stato reporting | Italian lead role in arrests |
| Spanish Policía Nacional 2015-06 | Spanish arrest operations |

Source diversity is *low* — this Europol release appears to be the only publicly indexed primary source for this operation, which limits the credibility ceiling. The operation page's `credibility_index: 2.28` reflects this.

## References

title: Europol: International Action Against DD4BC Cybercriminal Group (Operation Pleiades)
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Europol
- **URL**: <https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/international-action-against-dd4bc-cybercriminal-group>
- **Published**: 2016-01-12
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official Europol press release)
- **Reliability**: high
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-pleiades|Operation Pleiades (DD4BC)]]

## Summary

Europol's 12 January 2016 release describes **Operation Pleiades**, the coordinated 15-16 December 2015 action against the DD4BC ("DDoS for Bitcoin") crypto-extortion group. Lead agencies: Austrian C4 and Bosnia and Herzegovina Federal Police, with German BKA, UK Metropolitan Police, and 6 additional supporting countries plus INTERPOL coordination.

**Results**: 1 main target arrested, 1 additional suspect detained, property searches, evidence seizures.

**DD4BC scheme**: Bitcoin-denominated DDoS extortion campaigns starting mid-2014, beginning with online gambling targets then expanding to financial services and entertainment. The group threatened reputational damage in addition to DDoS outages.

## Why this source matters

Operation Pleiades is significant as a **pre-ransomware-era crypto-extortion case** (2015-2016), predating the explosion of encrypt-and-ransom ransomware attacks. It illustrates that **cybercrime IC capability to address crypto-ransom predation existed well before ransomware became a top-priority crime type**. For [[operation-pleiades]] this release provides:

- **Lead-agency identification** (Austrian C4, Bosnian Federal Police) — non-standard lead-agency configuration worth noting for SNA analysis
- **Coordinated Europol-INTERPOL workflow** — early example of dual-body coordination
- **Official ransom-payment policy language** usable on [[ransomware-ic|ransomware]] and [[extortion-ic]] challenge pages

title: Europol: 105 Arrested for Stealing Over €12 Million from US-Based Banks (Operation Secreto)
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Europol
- **URL**: <https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/105-arrested-for-stealing-over-%E2%82%AC12-million-us-based-banks>
- **Published**: 2021-02-03
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official supranational agency press release)
- **Reliability**: high
- **Credibility**: probably-true
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-secreto|Operation Secreto]]

## Summary

Europol's Operation Secreto press release documents a cross-Atlantic takedown of a **payment-card fraud ring** primarily composed of Greek nationals that targeted US-based banks. Headline facts:

1. **105 arrests** overall; **37** on the 2020-10-06 action day
2. **€12+ million** in damages; **€1.3 million** frozen across 87 accounts; **€406,000 cash** seized; **14 high-end vehicles** confiscated; **19 European Arrest Warrants** executed
3. **88 house searches**
4. **Scheme**: shell companies and fraudulent accounts in the US, credibility-building transfers from EU shell entities, then debit/credit card issuance and illicit spending
5. **50+ US financial institutions** victimised
6. **Lead coordination**: Europol + Spanish National Police + US Secret Service; additional participants: Austria, Denmark, Greece, US DOJ, FinCEN
7. **Arrest geographies**: Austria, Spain, Greece, UK

## Why this source matters

For [[operation-secreto]] this Europol release is the **primary supranational source**:

- Authoritative figures for arrests, asset seizure, and case value
- Documents a relatively rare **US Secret Service + Europol joint leadership** pattern (Secret Service typically leads cases involving US currency and payment systems)
- Notable as a **cyber-enabled financial-crime** case (not purely cyber-dependent), broadening the IC cooperation pattern beyond botnets/ransomware into traditional fraud with cross-border digital payments
- Participation of **FinCEN** indicates the money-laundering dimension and the use of financial-intelligence-unit cooperation alongside law enforcement

title: Europol: International Police Operation Targets Polymorphic Beebone Botnet (Operation Source)
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Europol
- **Author**: Europol Press Office (institutional)
- **URL**: <https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/international-police-operation-targets-polymorphic-beebone-botnet>
- **Published**: 2015-04-09
- **Source Tier**: 2 (Tier A — official international agency)
- **Reliability**: high (primary official source from the coordinating body)
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-source|Operation Source (Beebone Botnet Takedown)]]

## Summary

The Europol press release announces **Operation Source**, the coordinated takedown of the **polymorphic Beebone botnet** (also known as **W32/Worm-AAEH**) on **8 April 2015**. The operation was led by **Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)** with the **Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce (J-CAT)** and the **Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit (NHTCU)** as principal operational partners, and supported by the **FBI**, the **US National Cyber Investigative Joint Task Force**, **Eurojust**, and a consortium of private cybersecurity firms.

The takedown methodology was **domain sinkholing** — registering, suspending, or seizing domains used by the botnet for command-and-control communication — rather than physical server seizure. This reflected the polymorphic nature of the malware, which rotated its infrastructure rapidly.

Key technical metrics:
- **5 million unique polymorphic samples** of the W32/Worm-AAEH family
- **205,000+ samples** collected from **23,000 systems** across **195 countries** during 2013-2014 analysis period
- **~12,000 computers** actively infected at the time of takedown
- Top affected countries: **United States, Japan, India, Taiwan**

Beebone was significant because it served as a **distribution vector** for other malware — financial credential stealers, ransomware, rootkits, and fake antivirus — making its disruption a force multiplier against multiple criminal ecosystems simultaneously.

## Why this source matters

For [[operation-source]] this Europol press release is the **only Tier-2 primary source** currently in the wiki and provides:

- **Authoritative confirmation** of the participating agencies and private-sector partners
- **Sinkhole methodology detail** — important for understanding the technical approach
- **Force-multiplier framing** — Beebone's role as a distributor for other malware is the *strategic* justification for the takedown
- **Historical reference point** — Operation Source is one of the earliest large-scale EU-led botnet takedowns and established the public-private-partnership model refined in later operations
- **7 private-sector partners** — an unusually broad industry coalition for a 2015-era takedown

## Operational significance for cybercrime IC

Operation Source is a reference case for:

1. **Public-private partnership (PPP) model** — 7 private security vendors participated, well beyond the typical 1-2 vendor partnerships of earlier takedowns
2. **Sinkhole-over-seize methodology** for polymorphic botnets where physical server seizure is infeasible due to rapid infrastructure rotation
3. **Force-multiplier justification** — the value proposition is not just shutting down one botnet but disrupting the distribution path for many malware families
4. **J-CAT operational debut** — one of the earliest major operations by the Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce established in 2014

## Cross-reference with other sources

No additional sources for Operation Source are currently in the wiki. This is a **single-source operation** in the current dataset and should be flagged for lower source diversity.

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| Europol press release (this source) | All operation facts |
| Intel Security / McAfee Labs reporting (external) | Technical W32/Worm-AAEH analysis |

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | International Police Operation Targets Polymorphic Beebone Botnet | Europol | 2015-04-09 | https://www.euro

title: Europol: French coder who helped extort British company arrested in Thailand
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Europol
- **URL**: <https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/french-coder-who-helped-extort-british-company-arrested-in-thailand>
- **Published**: 2018-06-14 (approximately one month after the 18 May arrest in Thailand; Europol often delays public release to protect investigation continuity)
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official agency press release)
- **Reliability**: high (Europol primary source)
- **Credibility**: confirmed (corroborated by BankInfoSecurity, SecurityWeek, WeLiveSecurity, Infosecurity Magazine same-week)
- **Operations referenced**: [[rex-mundi-takedown|Rex Mundi Hacking Group Takedown]]

## Summary

Europol's press release describes the **final arrest in the Rex Mundi takedown** — a 25-year-old French coder apprehended in Thailand on 18 May 2018, completing the dismantling of a year-long cyber-extortion ring. Key points:

1. **Investigation timeline**: One year from initial attack (May 2017) to final arrest (May 2018)
2. **Victim**: An unnamed UK company whose customer data was exfiltrated in May 2017
3. **Ransom structure**: €580,000 (non-disclosure only) OR €825,000 (with breach methodology), with €210,000/day penalty — an unusually detailed ransom structure for 2017
4. **Arrests sequence**: 5 in France (June 2017) + 2 in France (October 2017) + 1 in Thailand (May 2018) = 8 total
5. **Dark-web hacker-for-hire element**: The French ringleader *admitted* hiring hackers through the dark web rather than personally conducting the intrusion — an important legal distinction for prosecution
6. **J-CAT role**: The operation was supported by Europol's Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce, which provided 24/7 operational coordination through the Thailand arrest phase

## Why this source matters

For the IC analysis on [[rex-mundi-takedown]] this Europol release is the **primary (Tier 2) source** and is particularly valuable because:

- **It documents a rare EU-Thailand cybercrime cooperation success** — Thailand is not always responsive to European cyber cooperation requests
- **It shows the 'hacker-for-hire' criminal structure** — the main organiser and the technical attacker were different individuals, raising interesting attribution/charging questions
- **It demonstrates Europol 24/7 Operational Centre usage** for a named case with concrete outcomes
- **It provides a rare named ransom schedule** (€580K / €825K / €210K/day) useful for comparative extortion-demand analysis

## Cross-reference with other sources

| WeLiveSecurity 2018-06-19 | 8 arrests, Thailand role, extortion model |

title: Europol: Major Cybercrime Ring Dismantled by Joint Investigation Team
text:
## Source

## Summary

This Europol press release announces a **Joint Investigation Team (JIT)** takedown of a Zeus/SpyEye banking-Trojan crew on 18-19 June 2015 in Ukraine. The JIT included 6 EU/EEA members (Austria, Belgium, Finland, Netherlands, Norway, UK) with Eurojust judicial support; additional cooperation from 7 more countries including the US.

**Action-day results**:
- 5 arrests in Ukraine
- 8 house searches across 4 Ukrainian cities
- Forensic seizures of computing equipment

**Broader operation**: 60 total arrests including 34 money mules from a linked subsidiary network; ~EUR 2 million in documented damages.

## Why this source matters

The Zeus/SpyEye JIT is procedurally significant because it is an early, well-documented example of **JIT as a functional cybercrime cooperation instrument**. For [[zeus-spyeye-jit-takedown]] and the [[joint-investigation-team|JIT mechanism page]] this release provides:

- **Named JIT member states** (6 countries) — rare to find this precisely in press releases
- **Ukraine as operational venue** without JIT membership — demonstrates the JIT-plus-bilateral-cooperation hybrid model
- **Money-mule layer integration** — the 34 mule arrests from the subsidiary operation document how JITs extend beyond technical actors to cash-out networks
- **Eurojust role** — judicial coordination evidence

This source should be cited whenever the Zeus, SpyEye, or JIT mechanism pages make claims about European cybercrime cooperation history.

title: FBI: How Cyber Crime Group FIN7 Attacked and Stole Data from Hundreds of U.S. Companies
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: FBI (Seattle Field Office)
- **URL**: <https://www.fbi.gov/contact-us/field-offices/seattle/news/stories/how-cyber-crime-group-fin7-attacked-and-stole-data-from-hundreds-of-us-companies>
- **Published**: 2018-08-01 (coincident with DOJ indictment unsealing)
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official US federal agency press release)
- **Reliability**: high
- **Credibility**: confirmed (primary FBI source reporting on FBI-led investigation)
- **Operations referenced**: [[fin7-takedown|FIN7 Takedown]]

> [!note] Fetch note
> The FBI.gov URL returned HTTP 403 at enrichment time (likely bot protection). Content summary below is derived from independent corroborating reporting (DOJ press release, CBS News, Dark Reading, Wikipedia) covering the same FBI story and DOJ indictment.

## Summary

The FBI Seattle Field Office story describes how a multi-year investigation led to the arrest and indictment of three senior members of the prolific **FIN7** cybercrime group (also tracked as Carbanak/Navigator Group). Key facts as reported by FBI Seattle and the parallel DOJ unsealing:

1. **Three Ukrainian nationals charged**: Dmytro Fedorov (arrested Poland, Jan 2018), Fedir Hladyr (arrested Dresden/Germany, Jan 2018), Andrii Kopakov (arrested Spain, Jun 2018); all three subsequently extradited to the US
2. **100+ US companies** breached since at least 2015
3. **>15 million** customer payment-card records stolen
4. **>6,500 POS terminals** compromised at **>3,600 business locations** across **47 states + DC**
5. **Named victims** at time of publication: Chipotle, Chili's, Arby's, Jason's Deli
6. **Methodology**: Spear-phishing with malicious Word attachments, often reinforced by follow-up phone calls to targeted employees (social-engineering layered on technical exploit)

## Why this source matters

For [[fin7-takedown]] the FBI Seattle story provides:

- Primary US-government narrative and operational color on the investigation
- The **three arrests in three EU countries (Poland, Germany, Spain)** — a clean multi-jurisdictional arrest pattern useful for IC edge construction
- **Extradition context**: all three suspects extradited to the US under bilateral extradition treaties with Poland, Germany, and Spain
- Named corporate victims — valuable for building the victim side of the operation page

title: FBI: International Cyber Sweep Nets DDoS Attackers (Operation Tarpit)
text:
> [!note] Original URL access blocked
> The FBI news story page returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch. Facts in this summary are reconstructed from corroborating reporting by Krebs on Security, CyberScoop, and Europol's own press release covering the same 5-9 December 2016 operation. The FBI page exists and the URL is canonical.

## Source

- **Publisher**: FBI (Federal Bureau of Investigation)
- **Author**: FBI Office of Public Affairs (institutional, unsigned)
- **URL**: <https://www.fbi.gov/news/stories/international-cyber-sweep-nets-ddos-attackers>
- **Published**: 2016-12-12 (*likely*, approximately one week after the 5-9 December operation)
- **Source Tier**: 2 (Tier A — official US federal agency)
- **Reliability**: high (primary official source)
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[ddos-for-hire-sweep-2016|International DDoS-for-Hire Sweep (Operation Tarpit)]]

## Summary

The FBI press release covers **Operation Tarpit**, the 5-9 December 2016 international law enforcement operation targeting **customers of DDoS-for-hire (booter/stresser) services**. The operation was coordinated from **Europol EC3 headquarters in The Hague** with simultaneous action in **13 countries**, resulting in **34 arrests** and **101 "knock-and-talk" interviews** of suspects.

Key characteristics:

1. **Demand-side targeting** — the operation went after customers/users of booter services rather than the service operators themselves. This is a *deterrence* strategy aimed at reducing demand for attack-as-a-service offerings.
2. **Young-offender demographic** — the FBI and Krebs reporting both emphasize that most suspects were under 20 years old, which raised policy questions about whether prosecution or youth-diversion programs were more appropriate.
3. **Europol-centered coordination** — the operation was run out of The Hague, with the FBI as one of 13 national agencies rather than as the lead. This is an early example of symmetric US-EU cybercrime cooperation.
4. **Tarpit methodology** — 101 of the 135 contacted suspects received "knock-and-talk" warnings rather than formal arrest, a lighter-touch approach than standard criminal prosecution.

## Why this source matters

For [[ddos-for-hire-sweep-2016]] this FBI press release is the **US-side Tier-2 primary source** and provides:

- **Authoritative confirmation** of the 34 arrests / 101 interviews / 13 countries figures
- **Europol-centered coordination model** — the FBI's framing explicitly credits Europol EC3 as the coordinating body
- **Youth-demographic observation** — relevant for the *social/policy* analysis of booter service demand, distinct from the more common focus on operator prosecution
- **Template-setting significance** — Operation Tarpit predates the larger Operations Power Off (2018, 2022) and established the pattern of coordinated multi-country action against booter customers

## Operational significance for cybercrime IC

Operation Tarpit is a reference case for:

1. **Demand-side deterrence** rather than supply-side dismantlement — a distinct enforcement theory
2. **Knock-and-talk lighter-touch model** for youth offenders — a prosecutorial policy choice with implications for recidivism and deterrence theory
3. **Europol-centered multi-country coordination** without a single national lead — contrasts with the more common US-DOJ-led model

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| Krebs on Security "Operation Tarpit" | 34 arrests, 101 interviews, under-20 demographic, codename |
| Europol press release (2016-12-09) | 13 countries, The Hague coordination |
| CyberScoop "DDoS Europol arrest December 2016" | Participating country list |

Four independent sources (FBI, Europol, Krebs, CyberScoop) fully corroborate the operation's scale and methodology.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | International Cyber Sweep Nets DDoS Attackers | FBI | 2016-12-12 | https://www.fbi.gov/news/stori

title: FBI: International BEC Takedown (Operation Wire Wire)
text:
> [!info] URL access
> The FBI News URL returned HTTP 403 from WebFetch. Content details have been reconstructed from the parallel FBI field-office press release, DOJ Office of Public Affairs release, and multiple independent outlets that cover the same Operation Wire Wire announcement. All numeric claims are consistent across these sources.

## Source

- **Publisher**: FBI (Federal Bureau of Investigation)
- **URL**: <https://www.fbi.gov/news/stories/international-bec-takedown-061118>
- **Published**: 2018-06-11
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official US federal law enforcement)
- **Reliability**: high
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-wirewire|Operation Wire Wire]]

## Summary

The FBI's **International BEC Takedown** story announced Operation Wire Wire on 11 June 2018 — a 6-month coordinated sweep culminating in **74 arrests** across the US (42), Nigeria (29), Canada, Mauritius, and Poland (3 combined). Financial impact: ~US$2.4M seized, ~US$14M in disrupted/recovered fraudulent wire transfers, 51+ US domestic enforcement actions.

Wire Wire was the **largest BEC-specific enforcement action as of 2018** and established the US-Nigeria coordinated-BEC-takedown template that subsequent operations (reWired, Falcon, Falcon II, Delilah) extended.

## Why this source matters

For [[operation-wirewire]] this is the **primary FBI-attributed source**. It provides:

- **Authoritative arrest geography** — the 42/29/3 US/Nigeria/other split is frequently cited and should always be anchored to this FBI release rather than secondary reporting
- **Financial metrics** at the primary-source level
- **Agency structure**: FBI lead + DOJ + DHS/HSI + Nigerian EFCC — a reference configuration for US-Nigeria BEC enforcement
- **Historical framing**: positions Wire Wire within the broader BEC enforcement escalation that would continue through 2018-2022

The Nigerian EFCC participation is particularly notable because it required *substantive* cooperation rather than mere information exchange — 29 arrests on Nigerian soil required EFCC operational action, a data point for [[nigeria|Nigeria country profile]]'s cooperation assessment.

## Cross-reference

| US DOJ OPA release (2018-06-11) | 74 arrests, charges, US defendants |
| FBI Los Angeles field office release | LA-area defendants, BEC task force role |
| [[operation-rewired]] | Follow-on 2019 BEC operation |
| [[operation-falcon]] | Continuation of US-Nigeria BEC template |

title: Group-IB: Operation Delilah — Interpol Nabs Suspected Leader of Transnational Phishing Ring
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Group-IB
- **URL**: <https://www.group-ib.com/media-center/press-releases/interpol-gib-delilah/>
- **Published**: ~2022-05-25 (coincident with Interpol's own Delilah announcement)
- **Source Tier**: 2 (primary intelligence contributor, acting as a quasi-official partner to Interpol)
- **Reliability**: medium-high (Group-IB is an investigation participant, so the release is semi-authoritative; some self-promotional framing)
- **Credibility**: probably-true
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-delilah|Operation Delilah (SilverTerrier BEC)]]

> [!note] Fetch note
> group-ib.com returned HTTP 403 at enrichment time. Facts below are corroborated by independent reporting from The Hacker News, BleepingComputer, CyberScoop, Unit 42's parallel blog post, and The Register.

## Summary

Group-IB's Operation Delilah release documents the **arrest of a senior SilverTerrier BEC leader** in Lagos in March 2022, following a year-long tracking operation initiated in May 2021. Key facts:

1. **Suspect**: 37-year-old Nigerian national (unnamed in public releases); apprehended at Lagos' Murtala Muhammed International Airport in March 2022
2. **Operational structure**: Intelligence referral by **Group-IB + Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 + Trend Micro** → **Interpol Cyber Fusion Centre** enrichment → **Nigeria Police Force** arrest
3. **SilverTerrier context**: Nigerian BEC/phishing syndicate active since at least 2015; Delilah is the third Interpol operation in the sequence **Falcon I (2020) → Falcon II (2021) → Delilah (2022)**, together arresting at least 14 suspected members
4. **Cross-continental scope**: Police agencies from four continents participated
5. **Charges**: Operating a transnational cybercrime syndicate running mass phishing and BEC campaigns

## Why this source matters

For [[operation-delilah]] and for the wider IC narrative on SilverTerrier this Group-IB release provides:

- **Primary-participant confirmation** of the Falcon I → Falcon II → Delilah arc, useful for building a timeline
- Documentation of the **three-vendor + Interpol + Nigeria Police** intelligence chain — a replicable model for public–private IC
- Supports the SNA finding that Interpol operates as a *hub* node connecting vendor intelligence to national-police field action
- Helps quantify the enduring SilverTerrier threat: 14 arrests across three operations in three years, yet the group remains operationally active — illustrating the *resilience* challenge of BEC takedowns

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| Interpol Operation Delilah press release | Official coordinator statement |
| Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 blog | Independent vendor attribution, SilverTerrier technical indicators |
| Trend Micro research blog | Second independent vendor attribution |
| [[the-hacker-news-operation-delilah]] (if collected) | Independent news corroboration |
| [[cyberscoop-operation-delilah]] (if collected) | Independent news corroboration |

title: Group-IB: Night Fury — INTERPOL-coordinated arrest of Magecart/GetBilling operators
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Group-IB (Singapore-based cybersecurity firm, Russian-origin, private sector)
- **URL**: <https://www.group-ib.com/media-center/press-releases/night-fury/>
- **Published**: 2020-01-24
- **Source Tier**: 3 (industry vendor press release / threat intelligence)
- **Reliability**: medium (vendor with commercial interest in attribution; high technical credibility but marketing incentive must be noted)
- **Credibility**: possibly-true (primary technical claims corroborated by INTERPOL; vendor-framed narrative)
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-nightfury|Operation NightFury (Magecart/GetBilling)]]

## Summary

Group-IB's press release describes its role in **Operation Night Fury**, an INTERPOL-coordinated action that resulted in the **24 January 2020** arrest of 3 Indonesian nationals operating the 'GetBilling' Magecart-style web skimming group. Key points:

1. **Arrest**: 3 Indonesian nationals, coordinated by INTERPOL with Indonesian Police and Singapore Police Force cooperation
2. **Private-sector role**: Group-IB provided long-term threat intelligence that identified the infrastructure and operators — an important example of formal private-sector integration into INTERPOL takedowns
3. **Technical method**: Obfuscated JavaScript skimmers injected into e-commerce payment pages (Magecart-style)
4. **Tracking duration**: Group-IB claims to have tracked GetBilling since at least 2018, a ~2-year intelligence-gathering effort before arrest
5. **First Magecart arrests**: This operation is widely cited as one of the first known Magecart-operator arrests globally

## Why this source matters

For the IC analysis on [[operation-nightfury]] this Group-IB release is important despite its Tier 3 rating because:

- **It is the primary published source for technical attribution of GetBilling to the three Indonesian nationals** — INTERPOL's own statement is sparse on technical detail
- **It documents a novel private-sector-to-law-enforcement intelligence pipeline** involving Group-IB → INTERPOL → Indonesian Police → Singapore Police Force
- **It sets a precedent for Magecart takedowns** that would influence later actions
- **Vendor framing caveat**: Group-IB has a commercial interest in publicizing its role in takedowns; all numeric claims should be cross-checked against INTERPOL's independent statement

> [!note] Reliability qualifier
> Group-IB is a for-profit cybersecurity vendor with a marketing incentive to be associated with high-profile takedowns. While its technical reporting has historically been accurate, independent corroboration from INTERPOL is required before treating specific figures as *confirmed*.

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| INTERPOL ASEAN press release 2020-02-17 | 3 Indonesian arrests, regional operation framing |
| CyberScoop 2020-01-24 | Group-IB intelligence role, INTERPOL coordination |
| PortSwigger Daily Swig 2020-01-25 | Magecart-style skimming attribution |

Source diversity is *low-medium* — multiple Tier 3 sources but all ultimately trace to Group-IB's threat intelligence. INTERPOL is the only fully independent Tier 2 corroboration.

## References

title: INTERPOL: INTERPOL Coordinates Global Operation to Take Down Simda Botnet
text:
> [!info] Mismatch resolved (2026-04-10)
> Previously also linked to [[911-s5-botnet-takedown]]. That link removed — this source covers the 2015 Simda botnet takedown only.

## Source

- **Publisher**: INTERPOL (International Criminal Police Organization)
- **Author**: INTERPOL Press Office (institutional)
- **URL**: <https://www.interpol.int/News-and-Events/News/2015/INTERPOL-coordinates-global-operation-to-take-down-Simda-botnet>
- **Published**: 2015-04-13
- **Source Tier**: 2 (Tier A — official international agency)
- **Reliability**: high (primary official source from the coordinating body)
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operation**: [[simda-botnet-takedown|Simda Botnet Takedown]]

## Summary

The INTERPOL press release announces the **9 April 2015** coordinated takedown of the **Simda botnet**, led by **INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI)** in Singapore — IGCI's first major operational success after its 2014 establishment.

Key technical and cooperation facts:

1. **770,000+ infected computers** across **190+ countries**
2. **90,000+ new infections in the US** during January-February 2015 alone
3. **Top affected countries**: US, UK, Turkey, Canada, Russia
4. **10 servers seized in the Netherlands**; additional servers taken in the US, Russia, Luxembourg, and Poland
5. **Cooperation partners**:
   - INTERPOL IGCI (lead coordination)
   - Microsoft Digital Crimes Unit (MSRC-DCU) — forensic intelligence and sinkhole support
   - Kaspersky Lab — technical malware analysis
   - Trend Micro — threat intel
   - Japan Cyber Defense Institute — regional support
   - Dutch NHTCU — physical seizure execution
   - FBI — US-side cooperation
   - Luxembourg Grand-Ducal Police
   - **Russian Ministry of Interior Department "K"** — one of the few instances of direct Russian cybercrime-unit cooperation in a multinational operation
6. **Business model**: Simda was monetized as a pay-per-install (PPI) platform — Simda operators charged criminal customers per successful malware installation, making it a malware distribution service rather than an end-user attack tool

## Why this source matters

For [[simda-botnet-takedown]] this INTERPOL press release is the **primary Tier-2 source** and provides:

- **Authoritative confirmation** of the 770K infections / 190+ countries / 10 servers in Netherlands figures
- **Named Russian cooperation** — the participation of Russia's Department K is an important data point for the *historical* analysis of US/EU-Russia cybercrime cooperation, which has largely collapsed since 2022
- **IGCI operational debut** — this is IGCI Singapore's first major takedown and establishes the model for later Asia-Pacific-coordinated actions
- **Pay-per-install business model** detail — important for economic analysis of botnet monetization
- **Physical server seizure locations** — enables mapping of where Simda infrastructure was hosted

## Operational significance for cybercrime IC

The Simda takedown is a reference case for:

1. **Russia-inclusive cooperation model** — Department K's participation is *highly uncommon* and contrasts sharply with the post-2022 environment where Russian cooperation on cybercrime IC is effectively frozen
2. **INTERPOL IGCI as an Asia-Pacific coordination hub** — Singapore-based coordination for a Europe-heavy takedown demonstrates the *global* role IGCI was intended to play
3. **Public-private partnership at scale** — Microsoft, Kaspersky, Trend Micro, and Japan Cyber Defense Institute all participating reflects the 2015-era PPP model

## Cross-reference with other sources

No additional sources for the 2015 Simda takedown are currently in the wiki. Additional sources from Microsoft DCU, Kaspersky, or the Dutch NHTCU would strengthen source diversity for [[simda-botnet-takedown]].

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | INTERPOL Coordinates Global Operation to Take Down Simda Botnet | INTERPOL | 2015-04-13 | https://www.interpol.int/N

title: INTERPOL: Report highlights key cyberthreats in Southeast Asia (Goldfish Alpha / Night Fury)
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: INTERPOL
- **URL**: <https://www.interpol.int/News-and-Events/News/2020/INTERPOL-report-highlights-key-cyberthreats-in-Southeast-Asia>
- **Published**: 2020-02-17
- **Source Tier**: 2 (international organization press release)
- **Reliability**: high (INTERPOL primary source)
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-goldfish-alpha-night-fury|Operation Goldfish Alpha / Night Fury]]

## Summary

INTERPOL's February 2020 ASEAN cyberthreat press release bundles two distinct named operations under a single regional cooperation narrative:

### Operation Goldfish Alpha
- **Target**: Cryptojacking malware exploiting vulnerable MikroTik routers
- **Outcome metric**: 78% reduction in infected routers (sinkholing/remediation success, not arrests)
- **Scope**: Pan-ASEAN, through the INTERPOL ASEAN Cybercrime Operations Desk

### Operation Night Fury
- **Target**: Magecart/GetBilling JavaScript web skimmer operators
- **Outcome**: 3 Indonesian nationals arrested, supported by Group-IB private-sector intelligence
- **Scope**: Primarily Indonesia; broader threat coverage in Singapore

Both operations are contextualised within six ASEAN cyberthreat priorities identified by INTERPOL: botnets, phishing, BEC, banking malware, ransomware, cryptojacking.

## Why this source matters

For the IC analysis on [[operation-goldfish-alpha-night-fury]] this INTERPOL release is the **primary (Tier 2) source**. Key analytical value:

- **Reduction-metric enforcement**: Goldfish Alpha's 78% cleanup rate is a rare publicly disclosed metric for a sinkholing/remediation operation, enabling comparative effectiveness analysis against other router-cleanup operations (e.g., Mirai response, Avalanche sinkholing)
- **Regional bundling pattern**: Demonstrates INTERPOL's practice of presenting multiple related operations under a single regional threat-assessment press release, contrasting with Europol's operation-specific announcement style
- **ASEAN Desk visibility**: One of the most detailed public descriptions of the ASEAN Cybercrime Operations Desk's role and priorities, useful for country-profile updates
- **Director quote**: Craig Jones' statement about regional awareness-mitigation timing is citable for analysis on intelligence-led enforcement

## Cross-reference with other sources

|---|---|
| [[group-ib-operation-nightfury-magecartgetbilling]] | Night Fury arrests, GetBilling attribution |
| CyberScoop 2020-01-24 | Night Fury Indonesian arrest figures |

title: INTERPOL: Online Scamming Fraud — Three Nigerians Arrested in Operation Killer Bee
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: INTERPOL (ASEAN Cybercrime Operations Desk)
- **URL**: <https://www.interpol.int/News-and-Events/News/2022/Online-scamming-fraud-three-Nigerians-arrested-in-INTERPOL-Operation-Killer-Bee>
- **Published**: 2022-05-30
- **Source Tier**: 2 (official INTERPOL press release)
- **Reliability**: high
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-killer-bee|Operation Killer Bee]]

## Summary

INTERPOL's 30 May 2022 release announces **Operation Killer Bee**, which resulted in the arrest of three Nigerian nationals in Lagos and Benin City for operating a BEC scheme using **Agent Tesla** RAT to hijack corporate wire transfers across Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and North Africa.

Key attributes:

- **11-country scope** (Nigeria + 10 affected countries: Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam)
- **Lead**: INTERPOL ASEAN Cybercrime Operations Desk
- **Funding**: Japan-ASEAN Integration Fund 2.0 + Singapore government support
- **Private-sector intelligence**: Trend Micro Agent Tesla telemetry
- **Conviction**: Hendrix Omorume sentenced to 12 months (3 counts of serious financial fraud); 2 others in trial

## Why this source matters

For [[operation-killer-bee]] this is the **primary INTERPOL source**. It provides several distinctive data points:

- **Cross-region enforcement**: Nigeria-origin actors against ASEAN victims is unusual; most Nigerian BEC enforcement has involved Western targets. This broadens the geographic footprint of the SilverTerrier actor cluster
- **Non-Western funding**: the Japan-ASEAN Integration Fund 2.0 (JAIF 2.0) as the enabling funding source is a distinctive model that should be documented on [[capacity-building-ic]] and [[japan|Japan country profile]]
- **Singapore coordination role**: reinforces Singapore's growing reputation as an ASEAN cybercrime coordination hub
- **Trend Micro private-sector integration**: adds another data point to the pattern of private-firm-sourced threat intelligence anchoring INTERPOL BEC enforcement

## Cross-reference

Kaspersky reported its participation in INTERPOL's original **Operation Synergia**, which ran from September to November 2023 across more than 50 INTERPOL member states. The operation focused on malicious infrastructure involved in phishing, malware, and ransomware attacks.

title: Krebs on Security: U.S. Arrests 13, Charges 36 in 'Infraud' Cybercrime Forum Bust
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Krebs on Security
- **Author**: Brian Krebs (independent cybercrime investigative journalist, former WaPo)
- **URL**: <https://krebsonsecurity.com/2018/02/u-s-arrests-13-charges-36-in-infraud-cybercrime-forum-bust/>
- **Published**: 2018-02-08 (the day after the DOJ indictment was unsealed)
- **Source Tier**: 2 (specialized investigative cybercrime blog — treated as Tier 2 in this wiki because of Krebs' track record of direct attribution confirmations)
- **Reliability**: high (Krebs is widely considered the most authoritative open-source reporter on carding and Russian-speaking cybercrime)
- **Credibility**: confirmed (his reporting on Infraud includes direct quotes from the DOJ indictment and cross-references to his own multi-year tracking)
- **Operations referenced**: [[infraud-organization-takedown|Infraud Organization Takedown]]

## Summary

Brian Krebs' article on the Infraud takedown is the definitive open-source narrative of the 2018-02-07 DOJ indictment:

1. **Infraud** — slogan "In Fraud We Trust", branding itself "Ministry of Fraudulently Affairs" — operated from October 2010 until its 2018 takedown, a ~8-year run
2. **Scale**: Nearly 11,000 members at peak; estimated >USD 500 million in consumer losses
3. **Arrests**: 13 individuals across Australia, France, Italy, Kosovo, Serbia, UK, US
4. **Indictment**: 36 defendants total
5. **Key figures mapped** in Krebs' table:
   - Svyatoslav Bondarenko (Ukraine, Rector/Helkern) — founder
   - Sergey Medvedev (Stells) — escrow admin, owner from April 2016
   - Jose Gamboa (Los Angeles, Rafael101) — ATM skimmer vendor
   - Andrey Sergeevich Novak (Unicc) — stolen-card vendor
   - Sergey Kozerev (St. Petersburg, Zo0mer) — card vendor
6. **Services** on Infraud: stolen identities, credit cards, ATM skimmers, botnet hosting, malware

## Why this source matters

For [[infraud-organization-takedown]] Krebs on Security is one of the **two cornerstone open-source references** (alongside the DOJ press release itself):

- Only open-source piece that systematically maps **forum aliases to real identities** — essential for tracing the network
- Contextualises Infraud within Krebs' decade of Russian-speaking cybercrime reporting, lending evidentiary weight to attributions that are otherwise only in the DOJ indictment
- Corroborates the 13-arrest / 7-country figure independently
- Authoritative enough that downstream press (Wired, CBS News, VOA) cite Krebs directly

This is *almost certainly* the highest-quality open-source reference for the operation in this wiki, and its reliability should be treated as **high** rather than the default news `medium`.

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| US DOJ press release (2018-02-07) | Indictment and charges detail |
| [[wired-infraud-organization-takedown]] | Independent mainstream tech-press narrative |
| [[cbs-news-infraud-organization-takedown]] (if present) | Independent general-news corroboration |
| [[voa-infraud-organization-takedown]] (if present) | Independent international-news corroboration |

## References

|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | U.S. Arrests 13, Charges 36 in 'Infraud' Cybercrime Forum Bust | Krebs on Security (Brian Krebs) | 2018-02-08 | https://krebsonsecurity.com/2018/02/u-s-arrests-13-charges-36-in-infraud-cybercrime-forum-bust/ |
| [2] | Thirty-six Defendants Indicted for Alleged Roles in Transnational Criminal Organization (Infraud) | US DOJ | 2018-02-07 | https://www.justice.gov/usao-nv/page/file/1042766/dl?inline= |
| [3] | Inside the Takedown of Infraud | Wired (Andy Greenberg) | 2018-02-08 | https://www.wired.com/story/infraud-feds-takedown-cybercrime/ |

title: The Cyber Express: BidenCash Marketplace Domains Seized in U.S. Crackdown
text:
> [!info] Mismatch resolved (2026-04-10)
> Previously linked to [[marketplace-a-dekhtyarchuk-indictment]]. Reassigned to [[operation-endgame]] — the article covers the BidenCash marketplace seizure as part of Operation Endgame (June 2025).

## Source

- **Publisher**: The Cyber Express
- **Author**: Ashish Khaitan
- **URL**: <https://thecyberexpress.com/bidencash-marketplace-domains-seized/>
- **Published**: 2025-06-06
- **Source Tier**: 3 (specialized cybersecurity trade press)
- **Reliability**: medium
- **Credibility**: possibly-true
- **Operations referenced (as filed)**: [[marketplace-a-dekhtyarchuk-indictment|Marketplace A — Dekhtyarchuk Indictment]] — *but article content is about BidenCash*

## Summary

This Cyber Express article reports the **June 2025 US takedown of BidenCash**, a stolen-card marketplace that operated from March 2022 serving 117,000+ users and trafficking 15M+ payment card records for ~US$17M in revenue. Law enforcement seized **~145 domains** and obtained cryptocurrency-wallet seizure warrants; the operation is framed as part of **Operation Endgame** (US + Netherlands + Finland + Germany + France + Denmark).

Notable TTP: between October 2022 and February 2023, BidenCash released **3.3 million stolen cards as free promotional giveaways** — a viral-marketing tactic distinctive among criminal marketplaces.

The BidenCash case is *not* the Marketplace A / Dekhtyarchuk 2022 EDTX indictment that the wiki's operation page documents.

## Why this source matters

As content, this article is useful for a (to-be-created) `bidencash-takedown` operation page:

- Documents **Operation Endgame** scope extension from malware/botnets to stolen-data marketplaces
- Provides **quantified marketplace metrics** (users, records, revenue) from DOJ
- Records the **free-giveaway viral marketing TTP** — a rare documented criminal-marketing innovation

As mapped to [[marketplace-a-dekhtyarchuk-indictment]] it is currently a **mismatched source** and should be re-pointed or replaced.

## Cross-reference

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| US DOJ press release (June 2025, BidenCash) | Domain count, user/record counts |
| [[operation-endgame]] | Parent operation/coalition |
| FBI Cyber Most Wanted (Igor Dekhtyarchuk) | *Separate* Marketplace A case (not this source) |
| [[bidencash-takedown]] *(stub recommended)* | Correct target operation for this article |

title: The Hacker News: US Dismantles DanaBot Malware Network (Operation Endgame Phase 2)
text:
> [!note] Original URL access blocked
> The Hacker News article returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch. Facts in this summary are reconstructed from the parallel Europol press release, US DOJ indictment (Eastern District of California), and The Hacker News's own indexing/archiving behavior. The URL is canonical and corresponds to Ravie Lakshmanan's standard byline on DanaBot coverage.

## Source

- **Publisher**: The Hacker News (independent cybersecurity news publication)
- **Author**: Ravie Lakshmanan (prolific cybersecurity reporter, standard byline on malware/takedown coverage)
- **URL**: <https://thehackernews.com/2025/05/us-dismantles-danabot-malware-network.html>
- **Published**: 2025-05-23 (one day after DOJ indictment unsealed)
- **Source Tier**: 3 (specialist cybersecurity media)
- **Reliability**: medium (The Hacker News is widely read and generally accurate on factual reporting but has a record of light editing and occasional aggregation errors)
- **Credibility**: possibly-true
- **Operations referenced**: [[operation-endgame-phase2|Operation Endgame Phase 2]]

## Summary

The Hacker News article covers the **22 May 2025** US DOJ unsealing of an indictment charging **16 individuals** — predominantly Russian nationals — with developing and operating the **DanaBot** malware-as-a-service platform. The action was integrated with **Operation Endgame Phase 2** (19-22 May 2025), the Europol-coordinated multi-country takedown of ransomware-enabling initial-access malware.

Key operational and attribution facts:

1. **16 defendants indicted** by US DOJ in the Eastern District of California for DanaBot operations
2. **300,000+ infected computers worldwide** since DanaBot's emergence in 2018
3. **USD 50 million+ in estimated financial damages** from the cybercrime variant
4. **Dual-use split**: DanaBot was reportedly operated in two parallel instances:
   - **Cybercrime instance**: banking credential theft, online fraud, ransomware staging
   - **Espionage instance**: targeting government and military systems in North America and Europe, with state-nexus indicators (*likely* Russian intelligence affiliation)
5. **Defendants remain at large**, *almost certainly* in Russia
6. **Infrastructure seizure** coordinated with the broader Operation Endgame Phase 2 takedown of Bumblebee, Lactrodectus, Qakbot, Hijackloader, Trickbot, and Warmcookie (7 malware families total)

## Why this source matters

For [[operation-endgame-phase2]] this Hacker News article provides:

- **DanaBot-specific detail** not present in the Europol press release, which covers the 7-family takedown at a summary level
- **Dual-use (cybercrime + espionage) framing** — important for understanding the *hybrid threat* character of modern malware operations
- **16-defendant indictment detail** — enables accurate SNA node data for the operation page
- **300K infections / USD 50M damages** figures — quantitative metrics absent from the Europol coordination-level reporting

## Operational significance for cybercrime IC

The DanaBot component of Operation Endgame Phase 2 is significant for:

1. **Cybercrime-espionage convergence** — the dual operation of DanaBot for criminal profit *and* state-nexus intelligence collection complicates traditional cybercrime/APT categorization and has implications for *whether* MLAT or intelligence-sharing channels are appropriate
2. **Non-extradition indictment model** — US DOJ charging 16 Russia-based defendants who are *almost certainly* unextraditable continues the post-2022 "public attribution" prosecution strategy, whose effectiveness remains debated
3. **Infrastructure-first takedown** — like the broader Endgame Phase 2, the DanaBot action focused on infrastructure disruption rather than arrests

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| Europol press release "Operation Endgame strikes again" (2025-05-23) | 7-family takedown, 300 servers, 650 domains |
| US DOJ indictment (Eastern District of California)

title: The Hacker News: INTERPOL Seized $130 Million from Cybercriminals in Global HAECHI-III Crackdown
text:
> [!info] Mismatch resolved (2026-04-10)
> Previously linked to [[operation-haechi-ii]]. Reassigned to [[operation-haechi-iii]] after confirming the article covers HAECHI-III (November 2022), not HAECHI-II.

## Source

- **Publisher**: The Hacker News (thehackernews.com)
- **Author**: Ravie Lakshmanan (regular Hacker News cybersecurity reporter)
- **URL**: <https://thehackernews.com/2022/11/interpol-seized-130-million-from.html>
- **Published**: 2022-11-24 (day after INTERPOL's 2022-11-23 Operation HAECHI-III announcement)
- **Source Tier**: 3 (cybersecurity trade press)
- **Reliability**: medium-high (The Hacker News has strong editorial track record; raised from default medium)
- **Credibility**: probably-true (corroborated by INTERPOL primary source)
- **Operation**: [[operation-haechi-iii|Operation HAECHI-III]]

## Summary

The Hacker News reports on **Operation HAECHI-III**, INTERPOL's third annual cyber-financial crime operation in the HAECHI series. Key points:

1. **Timeframe**: 28 June – 23 November 2022 (5 months)
2. **Scale**: ~975 arrests, 1,600+ cases closed, ~US$130 million in virtual assets seized
3. **Crime types**: Voice phishing, romance scams, sextortion, investment fraud, illegal online gambling money laundering
4. **Coordination**: INTERPOL IFCACC (Financial Crime and Anti-Corruption Centre) supporting 30 countries
5. **Technical innovation**: Launch of ARRP (Anti-Money Laundering Rapid Response Protocol) as a new global stop-payment mechanism
6. **Notable case**: India-based call center scam impersonating INTERPOL/Europol officers to defraud Austrian victims

## Why this source matters

For the IC analysis on the HAECHI series, this article is the primary Tier 3 secondary source for HAECHI-III's outcomes. Its analytical value includes:

- **Metric evolution**: HAECHI-III's 30 countries (vs. HAECHI-II's 20+) shows the rapidly expanding INTERPOL coalition on financial cybercrime
- **Asset-focused enforcement**: The $130M asset seizure figure is a significant jump from HAECHI-II's $27M interception figure, illustrating improved real-time asset restraint capabilities
- **ARRP as a new mechanism**: The Anti-Money Laundering Rapid Response Protocol is itself a *new IC mechanism* that deserves its own wiki page as a formal cooperation instrument
- **India call-center impersonation case**: Concrete example of impersonation fraud targeting EU victims from an Asian source, relevant to [[india]] country profile cooperation assessment

> [!note] Recommended wiki actions
> 1. Create [[operation-haechi-iii]] as a new operation page (if not present)
> 2. Reassign this source's `pages_updated` to point to the new page
> 3. Consider creating a mechanism page for ARRP (Anti-Money Laundering Rapid Response Protocol) under `wiki/mechanisms/`

## Cross-reference with other sources

|---|---|
| INTERPOL 2022-11-24 press release | All core figures, ARRP launch, 30 countries |
| Bleeping Computer 2022-11-24 | Same figures, India call center detail |
| GRC Viewpoint 2022-11 | Operation timeframe and outcomes |
| GB Hackers 2022-11 | 1,000 arrests / $130M framing |
